Appian Interview Experience
About the Company:
Appian Corp (Appian) is an enterprise application platform. The company’s main activities
include delivering solutions for process orchestration, automation, and intelligence. Its
software allows users to build task forms, process solutions, and dashboards by considering
the business policies, models, and procedures and enables users to monitor and respond to
instantaneous critical events. The company works majorly on low code development.
Role: Software Development Engineer
Package: 25.5 LPA
Location: Chennai
Selection Process:
Online Test: 1 hr
Coding Questions : 3
The test was conducted on an Glider AI platform.
Question 1: Generating the Next Unique Booking Reference Code
Input:
curr = sequence of integers representing the unique booking reference
Output:
next = next greater ordered arrangement
Example:
Input: 1, 2, 3 Output: 1, 3, 2
Question 2: Mall Treasure Hunt
1. Online Test
2. Technical Round - 1
3. HR Round - 1
4. HR Round - 2
You are working for an airline reservation system.
Each booking gets a unique reference number that is a permutation of digits.
The system must generate the next greater ordered arrangement each time.
If the current code is the last possible arrangement, reset to the smallest order.
A shopping mall is divided into a grid of rooms.
Rooms can be:
'S' = start (entrance)
'O' = open/accessible
'X' = blocked
'T' = treasure chest
Task:
Find the shortest path (minimum steps) from 'S' to any 'T'.
Example:
Input:
[
['S','O','O','O','O'],
['X','O','X','X','O'],
['X','O','T','X','X'],
['O','O','O','O','O']
]
Output: 5
Question 3: Reorganize Queue with Multiple Team Assignment
Input:
Output:
Reorganized list with team names and/or students.
Example:
`Input: Teams = { "T1": ["Bob", "Carol", "Dave"], "T2": ["Alice", "Eve"], "T3": ["Frank",
"Grace"], "T4": ["Heidi", "Ivan", "Judy", "Eve"] } Queue = ["Alice", "Bob", "Ivan", "Frank"]
You are given a list of students in a queue and a mapping of project teams.
Reorganize the queue such that:
1. Students from the same team are grouped together.
2. If all members are present, replace them with the team name.
3. Students not in any team remain as is.
4. A student in multiple groups can only be claimed by one (the team with earliest
occurrence wins).
1. List of names in queue order
2. Mapping of students to project teams
Output: ["Alice", "Bob", "Ivan", "Frank"]`
Technical Round - 1: 1 hr 30 mins
This round was primarily focused on Data Structures & Algorithms, Projects and Resume
based questions.
The interviewer started with a short intro about himself. Then I gave my intro and I had
mentioned my passion for "Astrophysics". The interviewer seemed interested in
Astrophysics too. So he asked questions from that. He asked about the recent trends in the
field of space for which I mentioned about the different countries of the world trying to prove
that America hasn't reached space yet. (Regarding the landing on the moon)
After this, the interviewer went to the DSA part of the interview. The DSA question asked
was:
Given an array of  intervals  where intervals[i] = any integer, merge all overlapping
intervals, and return _an array of the non-overlapping intervals in the form of {start,end} that
cover all the intervals in the input.
Example:
intervals = [3,6,2,5,8,1,9,11]
output = [{1,3},{5,6},{8,9},{11}]
My approach to the problem: I proposed an approach of sorting the array and then checking
whether there exists an overlap and in case of that add it to the existent output variable.
The approach had a time complexity of O(n log n). The interviewer helped me with an
approach of using a stack. However, I pointed out an edge case with a time complexity of
O(n^2) in that and so he proceeded with questions from my resume. It was very much
shocking that Appian had only one DSA question for the technical round.
After the DSA questions, the questions were based on my intern experience at Amazon. I
was asked about the task I did in Amazon and also how I performed in the team and about
my experience at Amazon. After this, the interviewer moved to the tech stack used in
Amazon and how I made use of Artificial Intelligence in my intern days.
Project based Questions:
I was asked which was the project in the resume I had worked on and is considered my
personal favorite. I chose my Railway DBMS project. I explained what inspired me to do it
and the things I tried to do in the project. After which I was asked about the ER structure of
the project and the tech stack used. Also the interviewer suggested few more things I could
add on to the existing project.
Finally he asked for any questions to him and I asked few questions about work-life balance
and career growth at Appian.
HR Round - 1: 15 minutes
The questions were very general
HR Round - 2: 15 minutes
This round was conducted via online mode at CUIC. Started with an introduction.
Questions asked:
General Tips:
Finally, don't feel disheartened if you get rejected by a company. You still got lot options
coming. Put your 100% effort and stay consistent. Your effort would guide you to your
company. My first rejection was at Appian and now I am placed at Appian.

Ex-Amazon SDE Intern, Placed at Appian.
MIT IT 2026
Reach out @ [https://www.linkedin.com/in/aashin-a-p-21jan2005/](LinkedIn | Aashin-A-P)

1. What do you know about Appian?
2. What is the meaning of your name?
3. What is your thought about Artificial Intelligence?
4. What AI tools have you used?
5. Regarding school and college CGPA and rankings?
6. What will you choose : Appian or Amazon?
1. What was implemented in the Railway DBMS project?
2. How was the large amount of data was stored for the railway project?
3. Amazon or Appian - Choice?
1. Be consistent and stay positive.
2. Be clear in DSA concepts and other core concepts.
3. Practice DSA questions in Leetcode and HackerRank.
4. Have a good resume with clear understanding of all the terms you have put into it.
5. The way you present your approach matters -> Start with the brute force and then
optimise the solution and accept feedbacks from the interviewer.

My SAP Labs Interview Experience  
Total Rounds: 5 
Date: 18th August 2025 
Hi everyone! I wanted to share my SAP Labs interview journey because when I was 
preparing, I used to read these kinds of experiences and they helped me a lot. So, here’s my 
detailed story — from the online test to the HR round. Hopefully, it gives you some idea of 
what to expect and how to prepare. 😊 
 
🖥️ Round 1: Online Assessment (60 mins) 
The very first hurdle was the online coding test, which was conducted in a virtual mode at 
CUIC. We had to solve two coding questions in one hour. 
🔹 Question 1: Bank Transfers Simulation 
We were given: 
 
A string R and an array V. 
 
If R[i] = 'A', transfer V[i] amount from Bank A to Bank B. 
 
If R[i] = 'B', transfer V[i] amount from Bank B to Bank A. 
The task was to find the final balance of both banks after all transactions. 
Constraints were huge (N up to 100,000), so efficiency was key. 
🔹 Question 2: Balancing the Board 
We had two strings (row1, row2) representing a board: 
 
Cells contained 'R', 'W', or '?'. 
 
'?' could be replaced with 'R' or 'W'. 
We had to make the board “balanced” with the minimum replacements. If impossible, 
return "not possible", else return the count of replacements. 
This round tested both logic and optimization skills. After completing it, I felt quite 
confident and fortunately, I cleared it. 
 
 
 
 
⚙️ Round 2: Technical Interview – 1 
From hundreds of students, 90 were shortlisted. 
This round started casually with: 
 
Self-introduction 
 
A few questions about my internship experience 
After this warm-up, we quickly moved to problem-solving. 
💻 SQL Questions I faced: 
1. From the Employees table, find the top-most manager of a given employee. 
2. From Orders and Restaurants tables, find the Top 5 restaurants with the 
maximum orders in the last 7 days. 
This round was a mix of SQL + clarity of thought. I cleared it and was super excited to move 
forward. 
 
⚡ Round 3: Technical Interview – 2 
Now things got serious. Out of 90, only 30 candidates moved ahead, and this round was the 
most grilling one. 
I was interviewed by two experienced professionals who really tested me. It started with 
another introduction (yes, again 😅), and then they literally went line by line through my 
resume. 
Here’s what happened: 
 
I had to explain my projects in detail — not just what I built, but also how I built 
them and why I chose certain approaches. 
 
Then came the conceptual questions: 
o Difference between a process and a task. 
o Internal working of compiler vs interpreter. 
 
One coding task: Implement a basic sorting algorithm on the spot. 
 
Some deep Operating Systems concepts: mutex, critical section, producer-consumer 
problem — and I had to explain them with real-life examples (for example, producer-
consumer like “chef cooking food and waiter serving customers”). 
This round went on for almost 1 hour 30 minutes. Honestly, it was tough, but I stayed calm 
and answered with examples whenever possible. Thankfully, I got through! 🙌 
 
🧑💼 Round 4: Managerial Round 
Now only 15 candidates were left. This round was more about my personality and 
behavior rather than coding. 
The panel was quite friendly (and luckily, one of them had already interviewed me in a 
previous round, so I felt comfortable). 
They asked: 
 
Why do you want to join SAP? 
 
Where do you see yourself in 5 years? 
 
What are your strengths and weaknesses? 
 
How are you working on improving your weaknesses? 
This was more like a conversation than an interview. It went on for about 40 minutes and I 
came out smiling. 
 
👥 Round 5: HR Round 
Finally, 13 candidates were shortlisted for this stage. 
This was the most relaxed and informal round. It started with some storytelling questions 
like: 
 
How did I choose MIT? 
 
My college journey so far. 
After that, the HR explained the compensation details, the internship structure, and the job 
location. I also got a chance to ask my own questions about the role and internship. 
It ended smoothly, and after about 15 minutes of waiting… the results were out! 
🎉 Out of 13, 10 were selected. I was thrilled to be the 4th person on the list. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
💡 Suggestions 
 
Resume is everything – Every line will be questioned. Be honest and well-
prepared. Don’t put something unless you are ready to defend it. 
 
Simple questions matter – They’re not checking if you know “rocket science”. They 
want to see if you understand basics and can explain them clearly. 
 
Use real-life examples – Whenever possible, connect your answers to real-world 
scenarios. It shows you really understand the concept. 
 
Communication is key – Speak confidently but don’t bluff. If you don’t know, it’s 
okay to admit and move on. 
 
📌 My Details 
Name: Anselm Flavian P 
Roll No: 2022506101 
Batch: 2026 
Department: Information Technology 
College: Madras Institute of Technology (Anna University) 
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/anselm1305 
 
✨ That was my SAP Labs journey! If this experience helps you and you get placed at SAP, 
do remember to thank me later 😉

APPIAN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Role: Software Engineer 
 
Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise software 
company headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology 
Corridor. The company provides a low-code platform for building enterprise 
applications, focusing on process automation, case management, and business 
process management in regions like North America, Europe, the Middle East, 
and Southeast Asia. 
The platform allows businesses to develop apps with low coding, using a visual 
interface and prebuilt modules. 
Online Assessment  
The online assessment consisted of 3 coding questions to be solved in 1 hour 
30 minutes. The timestamp of submission is considered during evaluation, so 
solving faster is an advantage. 
1. Insert Interval - Leetcode  
2. Find first and last position of element in sorted array - Leetcode  
3. Find missing and repeating – GeeksForGeeks 
Important: Plagiarism check is done by AI. 
Shortlisting Process 
The shortlisted candidates list was released one day before the interview. 
Be ready with: 
• Updated Resume 3 copies 
• DSA, DBMS, OOPS 
• Real-time puzzles 
Interview Rounds 
There were three rounds, each an elimination round. 
 
Technical Round 
Never lie in your resume 
 every word in it can become a question. 
The interviewer spent about 2 minutes reading my resume and simultaneously 
generating questions based on it. Meanwhile, I gave my self-introduction. 
Questions I Faced: 
1. React: 
•    Implement a list in a React functional component. 
2. Project Discussion: 
• I had mentioned three projects — he asked about every single line in the 
descriptions. 
3. API Questions: 
• Explain what an API is. 
• Types of API and their purposes. 
• Additional: Explain about API fire/hit rate. 
4. Project Purpose: 
• Asked for the purpose of my project — I explained clearly. 
5. Certification-Based Questions: 
• I had listed Oracle SQL credential by IBM in my resume. 
• What is the difference between schema and table? 
• In DBMS, a many-to-many relationship can be resolved into how many 
tables? (Answer: 3 tables) 
• Write an SQL query to select the highest CGPA from each department (he 
gave me the table schema). 
6. LeetCode Experience: 
• I mentioned 480+ problems solved. 
• Preferred language? → C++ 
• Most interesting problem solved? → I explained constructing a binary 
tree from inorder and preorder traversals. 
 
LeetCode Link: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder 
Traversal 
7. DSA Question 1: 
• Get non-duplicated elements from a given array containing integers (both 
duplicates and uniques). 
• Explained my approach and wrote the code. [unordermap] 
 
LeetCode Link: Single Number 
8. OOPs: 
• What is polymorphism? → Explained with a real-world example. 
9. Security in Project: 
• How did you encrypt the password during login? 
• Write the JSON format for an image. 
• Explained about Firebase authentication email verification. 
10. DSA Question 2: 
• A modified Two Sum problem (scenario-based). 
• Only after solving it, I realized it was LeetCode’s Two Sum problem. 
 
• LeetCode Link: Two Sum 
HR Round 
• Questions were similar to the technical round, with more focus on: 
• My projects 
• DBMS basics 
• Academics 
• Many-to-many relationship table conversion was again asked. 
Key Takeaways 
• Never lie in your resume — be prepared for any question based on it. 
• DSA + DBMS + OOPS are must-prepare topics. 
• Be ready to explain every project you mention. 
• Have knowledge of API concepts and real-world scenarios. 
• Solve LeetCode problems regularly — they may appear with slight 
modifications. 
• CGPA plays a vital role in the shortlisting process. 
My tips to juniors…. 
Prepare well. 
When asked "Tell me about yourself", speak clearly and confidently. Also, 
know about the company — their goals and the new steps they are taking. 
While solving DSA problems, keep speaking continuously. Talk about your 
approach, explain whatever you are thinking, and share what is running through 
your mind about the problem. Don’t let the interviewer search for the next 
question — keep the conversation active. 
Highlight the novelty of your project, its purpose, and the target consumers of 
your project. Have these points at your fingertips. 
 
GitHub: https://github.com/ARUNULAGAPPAN/ 
LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/arunulagappan2024/ 
Leetcode : https://leetcode.com/u/ARUN_THE_REX/ 
Portfolio: ArunUlagappan  
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/the__great__rex/

Wells Fargo EGS 
 
About Wells Fargo 
 
Wells Fargo provides banking, investment and mortgage products and services, as well as 
consumer and commercial finance, through 8,050 locations, 13,000 ATMs, the internet 
(wellsfargo.com) and mobile banking, and has offices in 38 countries and territories to support 
customers who conduct business in the global economy 
Source: Linkedin page of Wells Fargo 
 
Number of rounds 
The recruitment process had a total of 4 rounds. 
1. Online round 
2. Technical round 1 
3. Technical round 2 
4. HR round 
 
Details about the rounds 
 
Round 1: Online round: 
 
This round had questions ranging under different sections to test our skills under 
respective categories. There was a general aptitude section, technical MCQs, a coding section 
that consisted of 1 or 2 questions. There was also a business knowledge section that had 
questions regarding stocks, gain and loss etc,. This round happened on an online platform. The 
students who were shortlisted from this round moved to the next round of interviews. 
 
Round 2: Technical round 1: 
 
This round was conducted on zoom.  Questions on basic and advanced data structures, 
Operating systems, Databases and Computer organisation were the key focus. Few questions on 
specific skills I had mentioned in my resume were also asked. 
 
Round 3: Technical round 2: 
 
This round majorly focussed on skills and projects that were mentioned in my resume. 
These questions were framed in such a way to include further requirements to the problem 
statements I have mentioned and how I would extend my ideas to meet the requirements. 
 
Round 4: HR round: 
 
In this round, communication skills and attitude were the main things checked. Few 
questions on my earlier internships and how I got them were asked. The main point was to check 
how social and communicatively able I was, to voice out my ideas and gel to a team. 
 
 
 
Questions asked 
Though I don't exactly remember all the questions, here is how much I can recollect. 
 
The questions in round 1 had technical MCQs on areas like Databases, Computer 
organisation and Operating systems. Debugging and programming based questions were also 
present. For business analysis related questions, questions on stocks etc were asked.  One coding 
question was based on graph and other on arrays. 
 
 
In the interview round that happened for around an hour, first questions on basic data 
structures, sorting were asked. Then, I was asked to share screen and code basic problems in 
arrays and linked lists. 
 
Next, questions on Operating system, databases, dynamic programming were asked to 
test my understanding in these concepts. Questions specific to the skills I had put in the resume 
were asked. Since my areas of interests were Machine learning and Natural language processing, 
a few questions to test my basic knowledge in this area were asked. I was also asked on what 
kind of technical issues would these technologies, say autonomous driving, face in its efficient 
operation. 
 
The second technical round was majorly focussed on the projects I mentioned in my 
resume. Specific questions on the methodology I used were the main areas tested. The 
interviewer extended the problem statement to include newer details and asked me how I would 
solve them efficiently. A few questions on supervised and unsupervised learning, advanced data 
structures like trees and graphs were asked. 
 
The HR round was like a casual talk where the interviewer asked me about my interests, 
previous internships and family background. The main focus was to test my confidence, attitude 
and communication skills. 
 
Topics to be focussed 
 
 Data structures: Arrays, Linked lists, stacks, queues, trees and graphs 
 A basic understanding of dynamic programming and greedy programming (optimal 
substructures and overlapping subproblems, how to approach a DP problem etc) 
 Virtual memory (paging and segmentation), Relations in databases, Memory organisation 
were the frequently tested areas in MCQs 
 
Take away 
 
 
Usually interviewers expect candidates to be punctual and clearly focused. Answer the 
questions in a precise and detailed manner. Do not abstract or assume their questions, always 
answer in such a way that the central point of the answer is clear and other details are clearly 
explained. 
 
 
For questions in the lines of “Tell me about yourself”, answer in a much realistic way, 
say, a brief introduction on your family and academic background and how you found your area 
of interest etc,.  
 
 
Do not add projects or skills you don’t have. Recollect the details about the projects 
you’ve done so that you can precisely elaborate.  
 
Articulate your thoughts well in mind and start answering, do not be nervous. 
 
Communication matters, but even if your English isn’t great, it’s nothing to worry about. 
All that matters is if you are able to answer their questions and convey your ideas clearly. 
Note down the issues faced in one online exam or interview and try to sort them out in the next 
interviews. 
 
ALL THE BEST!

Appian Interview Experience
(Quality Engineering Intern)
Abhijith.M
About Appian Corporation
Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise software company
headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology Corridor. The com-
pany sells a Platform as a Service (PaaS) for building enterprise software applications.
It focuses on:
• Low-code development
• Process mining
• Business process management
• Case management markets in North America, Europe, the Middle East, and South-
east Asia
Appian offers a low-code platform with a visual interface and pre-built development
modules, enabling businesses to create apps and automations using little or no code.
Interview Rounds
The selection process consisted of four stages:
1. Online Assessment
2. Technical Round 1
3. Technical Round 2
4. HR Round
A total of 149 students appeared for the assessment.
After the assessment, 18
students were shortlisted for the interview process.
1. Online Assessment
Held on the Glider platform. This stage consisted of:
• Multiple-choice questions on:
– Analytical and logical reasoning
– Software testing fundamentals
– Basic quality concepts
• One coding question:
– Problem: Remove duplicate strings from a string array.
– Languages allowed: Java and Python.
– Note: The program had to accept manual input from the user during execu-
tion; there were no predefined inputs or automatic test cases.
1
2. Technical Round 1
This round included:
• A puzzle: There are nine coins, one heavier than the other eight. Using a two-pan
balance and only two weighings, find the heavier coin.
• A coding question: Write a program to check whether a given string is a palindrome.
– Discussed edge cases such as empty strings, whitespace-only strings, and var-
ious input formats.
– Performed a dry run of the code.
• Discussion of my internship in the CSE Department Internal Automation
Project.
• Discussion of my contributions to the Web Development Team of the CUIC
Portal.
• Questions on the projects listed in my resume.
3. Technical Round 2 (Techno-cum-Behavioural)
This round combined technical testing knowledge and behavioural questions:
• Questions on different types of testing:
– Unit testing
– Integration testing
– System testing
– Acceptance testing
– Regression testing
– White box testing
– Black box testing
• Behavioural questions such as:
– Why Appian?
– Why do you want the QE role?
4. HR Round
The HR round covered:
• Academic details: marks in 10th, 11th, 12th, CGPA (8.29 at the time), and JEE
Mains percentile.
• Motivation: why I want the QE role.
After all the rounds, only 3 out of the 18 interviewees were selected — 2 from CEG
and 1 from MIT. I am fortunate to be one of the three selected candidates for the role of
Quality Engineering Intern.
Key Takeaways
1. Your communication skills matter — express your thoughts clearly.
2. Be genuinely curious to learn. Even if you do not know about something, show
eagerness to understand and explore it.
3. Start your preparation early, and know your resume inside out — every single
point should be explainable.
4. DSA is important for all roles; learning puzzles and aptitude too is important.
2
5. Maintain a good CGPA from the first semester itself; it really does give you
an advantage.
If you have a low CGPA, compensate with relevant experience such as internships
(even short-term or unpaid), open-source contributions, maintaining club or depart-
ment websites, or projects in the CSE department. These activities demonstrate
teamwork, initiative, and practical exposure.
6. Finally, have hope rather than rigid expectations. Keep the mindset: “Whatever
happens, happens,” because luck can also be a factor.
Overall Experience
The interview process was an enriching experience that tested my analytical skills, coding
abilities, testing knowledge, and communication skills.

Take away: 
 
The interviews mainly focus on testing the coding knowledge. Prepare to code in 
document and in a limited time. Be interactive and share your ideas throughout.  
 
All the best!

Barclays 
Barclays is a British multinational banking and financial services company 
headquartered in London, with a rich history dating back to 1690. It operates globally, 
providing a wide range of banking, investment, and wealth management services to 
individuals, businesses, and institutions. With a significant presence in the United 
Kingdom and an international footprint, Barclays offers retail and corporate banking, 
investment banking, asset management, and credit card services. The bank has been 
involved in various philanthropic initiatives and is known for its commitment to 
sustainability and environmental practices. 
There were 3 rounds namely: 
• Round 1: Online Assessment (Virtual) 
- 
This round mainly focused on fundamentals of Software Engineering(SDLC 
lifecycle), OOPS, DBMS, finding outputs of  c/cpp/Java codes, debugging 
programs, and Aptitude questions. 
- 
There were a total of 25 MCQs and 5 sql queries to be completed within 70 
minutes. 
- 
The test was conducted on the Hacker Earth Platform. 
- 
Note: Out of around 400 students who applied for attempting the OA, only 
around 200 were shortlisted for writing the test. 
- 
46 students proceeded further for the interview process conducted at CUIC. 
 
 
• Round 2: Technical Interview (@ CUIC ) 
- 
This round started off with a brief introduction about myself, and I moved on 
to explain about the project that I was most proud of, since I worked really 
hard to deploy it as well. 
- 
The interviewer asked me why I chose MERN Stack and why MongoDB over 
SQL. (I explained the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling here) 
- 
The interviewer was interested in knowing every nook and corner of how I 
did the project and how I managed to deploy it within a month. 
- 
She asked me what motivated me to take up the projects I invested my time 
in. 
- 
What were the key challenges I faced during my projects. 
- 
Major/Key Take-aways and Learnings made . 
- 
She asked me to give a brief explanation about Gen AI, since I mentioned the 
certification (as part of our Naan Mudhalvan Syllabus) in my resume 
- 
Difference between GPT-5 and GPT-4 , which I couldn’t answer properly, but 
managed to give some convincing differences. 
- 
What Data Structure is used in your phone gallery app? With reference to 
how you can easily scroll the pictures in your gallery without having to wait 
for each picture to load. Explain your choice. 
- 
Finally, she asked why I chose to study in CEG and how these 2 years have 
changed me. 
- 
This round lasted for 1 hour and 15 mins 
- 
25 students proceeded for the HR round. 
 
 
• Round 3: HR Round (@ CUIC ) 
- 
This round was entirely behavioural, where the interviewer presented me 
with various scenarios to assess my crisis management skills and my 
response in critical situations. Some questions include:  
- 
She asked me to elaborate on my hobbies, as I had mentioned them in my 
resume. 
- 
Where do you see yourself in the next 4 years? 
- 
Imagine your team is going to release/deploy a project tomorrow. Today 
night you realise that there is a bug in the code and that it was you who made 
the mistake of creating the bug. What will you do? 
- 
Microsoft or Barclays? 
- 
Materialistic life or Work-Balance life? 
- 
Finally, she asked basic details about my parents and this round ended within 
20 minutes. 
- 
Out of 25 students, 15 students (5 from our dept) were selected for the 2026 
Summer Internship Programme at Barclays and I was lucky to be a part of it. 
 
 
 
• Some Tips  
- 
Be confident while answering the questions. 
- 
Be truthful to your resume. You must be thorough with every nook and 
corner of your resume, especially your projects. 
- 
Have a habit of solving DSA problems, and revising DBMS / OOPS concepts 
regularly.  
- 
In Barclays interviews many were asked on concepts like ER Diagram, 
Normalization, etc. 
- 
Try to maintain a CGPA of about 8+ , if otherwise, focus on other projects to 
compensate. 
- 
Read the job description and you must do a research about the company.  
- 
 Refer to the company's website to learn about RISES. 
• A little intro about myself 
- 
 Looking back, I realize I didn’t fully utilize my summer holidays. I did 
practice a few easy-to-medium problems on LeetCode now and then, but 
even after receiving this offer, I can clearly see that my problem-solving skills 
still have a long way to go. 
- 
Most of my time went into working on projects, while I also made sure to 
spend some leisure moments with my family. 
- 
The seriousness of internship preparation struck me only when internship 
offers started flooding the CUIC WhatsApp group—it was a wake-up call. 
- 
In the end, I managed to make good use of the time I had during the final 
stretch, but if there’s one piece of advice I’d give, it would be to start 
preparing early, ideally from your 3rd or 4th semester itself. 
- 
I was shortlisted for interviews at Appian and Western Digital (the latter 
being heavily focused on C programming, which wasn’t my strong area), but 
my preparation at that stage wasn’t sufficient to clear them. 
- 
Barclays was my 3rd glow of hope, and I was determined to grab that 
opportunity. 
- 
All the very best for your future internship opportunities!!

AMAZON DEVELOPMENT CENTRE 
 
SCREENING ROUND: 
 
All the students who met the eligibility criteria took this test (Online). It was a coding + aptitude + 
behavioural round conducted on AMCAT platform.    
 
Part 1: Debugging: 
 
It had around 7 questions with buggy code snippets. Candidates were expected to clear the bug and 
produce the required output.  
Difficulty Level: EASY 
 
Part 2: Coding: 
 
It had 2 Coding question, the questions may seem complex at first glance, but mostly are direct 
application of well-known data structures and algorithms. Candidates will be provided an hour to 
solve. Remember to handle edge cases as well since u will not be aware of failed test cases as they 
are hidden.  
Difficulty Level: Moderate 
 
Part 3: Behavioural Analysis: 
 
This was more of a personal round. There wasn’t a single right answer to any question. It had 
questions like candidate’s interest, his/her strength/weakness. Do attend this part very carefully since 
this is the only round where you can outperform others, also try to remain consistent throughout all 
the questions and don’t contradict yourself across two different questions.  
Difficulty Level: There isn’t such for this part, may be Moderate for you to pay extra attention.     
 
Part 4: Aptitude: 
 
It was a typical aptitude round. I found it difficult to keep up with the time since it had some questions 
testing comprehension skills. 
Difficulty Level: Moderate 
 
 
 
 
INTERVIEW ROUND(S): 
 
There were 4 rounds of interview, out of which first 3 were Technical and last one was technical cum 
managerial. 
 
First Technical Interview: 
 
I was asked to solve two coding problems in one hour. The proposed solution should be optimized to 
the fullest (minimal time and space complexity). The interviewer will have a conversation to help to 
arrive at the solution. 
The two coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Similar to searching in a row and column wise sorted 2-D Matrix 
2. Similar to finding if one string is a substring of another 
 
Second Technical Interview: 
 
Similar to first round, I was given another two problems and was asked to give an optimal solution 
in one hour. 
The two coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Sorting a stream of integers.( I used  an heap) 
2. Finding longest substring in a dictionary of words.( I used a trie ) 
 
Third Technical Interview: 
 
It was similar to first 2 rounds. 
The 2 coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Process scheduling with custom constraints 
2. Application of Binary Search Tree Searching, Insertion and Deletion 
 
Final Round (Managerial + Technical Round): 
 
Questions from various domains like DBMS, Operating Systems, and Object Oriented Programming 
were asked. Finally a coding question based on Bit-magic was given. 
 
 
Topics to be focussed: 
In decreasing order of importance 
1. Data Structures 
2. Algorithms 
3. Basic knowledge on domains listed in the final round experience.  
 
Use Geeksforgeeks articles for preparation and Leetcode for practice. 
Tip: Watch “Back to Back SWE” videos in youtube

Round 2 : Technical Interview 
Time Duration: 50-60 minutes

c. Chain of pairs can be formed in this fashion. You have to find the longest chain which

Codechef, Codeforces links if available). 
2) Present Your Education Properly.

5) Add experiences that are relevant to the job description.

Overall tips how to ace the placements:

Even passing partial test cases is often given added weightage. If you can't 
arrive at an optimal solution, just try a brute force approach to pass as many 
test cases as possible. 
22 students were shortlisted for the Technical and HR interview. I am one 
among them. 
Round 2: Technical Interview 
The technical interview lasted approximately 40-50 minutes, covering a broad 
spectrum of computer science fundamentals and practical problem-solving 
skills. 
Motivation and Background: The interviewer was keen to understand my 
reasons for choosing Computer Science and my self-learning journey. My focus 
on problem-solving platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank during my pre-
college days was highlighted. 
SQL Fundamentals: Given my expressed interest in SQL, I was questioned 
about core concepts like Inner JOINs and Outer JOINs and was asked to 
construct a basic query using JOIN. 
Database Management: A practical scenario involving concurrent transactions 
in a hotel or restaurant setting was introduced. I was expected to propose 
solutions for handling such scenarios, which included concepts like locking and 
abstraction levels. 
Indexing in DBMS:  I explained that its purpose is to speed up data retrieval in 
databases by improving query performance. 
Programming Proficiency: A simple coding challenge involving string reversal 
was presented. Additionally, I was expected to identify potential exceptions 
that could arise during string manipulation. 
List VS Tuples: The interviewer sought to assess my understanding of 
fundamental data structures by comparing lists and tuples in Python, including 
their real-world applications. 
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): My understanding of OOPS concepts 
was assessed. I explained the core principles - abstraction, encapsulation, 
inheritance, and polymorphism and was asked to implement a simple class in 
C++ or Python, as well as explain how abstraction is utilized in this 
implementation. 
Memory Management: Questions on deep vs. shallow copy and garbage 
collection highlighted key concepts in memory management. 
Ended with the interviewer asking if I had any questions. 
The round concluded with a positive note, advancing me to the next round. 
Few candidates were eliminated after this round. 
Round 3: Technical Interview 
The interview commenced with a focus on understanding my career aspirations 
and identifying my key strengths. To assess problem-solving abilities, the 
interviewer presented real-world scenarios. 
• I mentioned my expertise in database management systems, operating 
systems, and data structures.  
• The discussion then focused on SQL and NoSQL databases, including 
when to use each. 
• Why NoSQL Databases Were Developed and Implemented? 
The round was characterized by a focus on learning and understanding 
rather than solely assessing. 
Round 4: HR Interview 
• During the interview, I was asked to introduce myself. I mentioned my 
strengths, technical interests, club responsibilities, and personal 
interests.  
• I highlighted my role as Head of the Dance Club, which prompted a 
follow-up question about my interest in dance.  
• I elaborated on my passion for dance and how it has contributed to my 
personal growth and leadership abilities. 
The interview proceeded smoothly, with discussions covering various 
aspects of my background and technical knowledge. I felt confident in my 
responses and engaged in meaningful conversations about my experiences 
and skills. 
At the end of the selection process, a total of 12 students were chosen for 
the internship. I am pleased to share that I was among those selected, 
marking a successful conclusion to the interview process. 
Key takeaways from my interview experiences:-  
• Focus on problem-solving and DSA fundamentals. Utilize your summer 
holidays to brush up on the basics. I followed C.O.D.E by ACM Club, 
LeetCode, and Take U Forward on YouTube. 
• Practice aptitude questions using resources like IndiaBix. 
• Build confidence in expressing your thought process during interviews, 
as interviewers often assist when you’re stuck. 
• Be ready for both concept and application-oriented questions in OS, DSA, 
DBMS, and OOP. 
• Prepare answers for common non-technical questions, such as your 
strengths/weaknesses and reasons for wanting the internship.

About the company

three locations: Mumbai, Pune, and Chennai  
No of Rounds: 3 (Online Test + Technical interview + Technical & HR interview)  
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month  
Duration of internship: 8-10 weeks 
 
SCHEDULE: 
• ONLINE ASSESSMENT (16/08/24) 
 
Coding Round: Conducted in Hacker-rank Website  
Test Duration: 120 mins Camera On 1 easy, 2 medium and 1 hard question.  
 
 
1. BALANCING ELEMENTS  
When an element is deleted from an array, the higher-indexed elements shift down one 
index to fill the gap, A “balancing element” is defined as an element that, when deleted 
from the array, results in the sum of the even-indexed elements being equal to the sum 
of the odd-indexed elements. Determine how many balancing elements a given array 
contains.  
Example: Input: n=5 arr = [5,5,2,8]  
Output: 2 When the first or second 5 is deleted, the array becomes [5, 2, 5, 8].  
The sum[even] = 5+5=10 and sum[odd]=-2+ 8-10.  
No other elements of the original array have that property.  
There are 2 balancing elements: arr[0] and arr[1]. 
 
              2.MINIMUM FUEL COST 
                     There are n cars located on a 2 dimensional plane at positions (x[i],y[i]) where 0<=i<=n. 
                     They need to be parked in a straight line parallel to the x axis with no spaces between  
                     them. The fuel consumed to move a car is abs(x[finish]-x[start])+abs(y[finish]-y[start]). 
                      Determine the minimum fuel cost to arrange the cars side by side in a row parallel to the  
                      X axis  
                      Example: X=[1,4] , Y=[1,4] Total fuel : 2+3=5 
 
 
 
3.PRODUCT DEPENDENCY TREE 
Given a network of product dependencies represented as a tree. The tree consists of 
treeNodes vertices and the relationships between products are defined by two arrays: 
treeFrom and treeTo, both of size treeNodes – 1. Thes arrays specify the connections, 
where treeFrom[i] is connected to treeTo[i]. 
 
For this product dependency tree, the task is to determine the number of triplets of 
vertices (i ,j ,k) that meet the following conditions : 
0 <= i <j <k < treeNodes 
There should be no simple path connecting vertices i, j and k. 
Due to the potentially large number of triplets, return the answer module ( 10^9 +7) 
 
4.PORTFOLIO BACKTESTING : 
 
                    The traders of Hacker-land have stock prediction model that they want to backtest. 
                    The predicted profit earned every month by a certain portfolio is given by an array 
                    pnl of n integers where a negative integer denotes loss.  
 
                   The traders decide to iterate through the months from 1 to n, and if the pnl is positive, 
                   they add it to their profit and if the pnl is negative, they can either subtract it or skip it.  
                   The net profit must always be greater than or equal to 0.  
                    
                   Given the array pnl and an integer k, find the maximum total profit that can be earned 
                   by the portfolio after subtracting at least k losses. 
                   If it is not possible to take at least k losses, report -1 as the answer.  
 
     Example: Suppose n=5, k=2 and pnl=[4, -3, 6, -2, -1]  
 
     The optimal strategy is to add the profits 4 and 6, and the losses [-2, -1] to get a net  
     profit of 7 which is the maximum possible.

2/15 for the 4th question

About the company 
The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, is an American investment banking services 
holding company headquartered in New York City. BNY Mellon was formed from the merger 
of The Bank of New York and the Mellon Financial Corporation in 2007. It is the world's 
largest custodian bank and securities services company. The bank's primary functions are 
managing and servicing the investments of institutions and high-net-worth individuals. Its 
two primary businesses are Investment Services and Investment Management. The bank's 
clients include 80 percent of Fortune 500 companies. The Mumbai Representative Office of 
The Bank of New York Mellon was set up in 1983 and continues to serve as BNY Mellon's 
main liaison point for Indian client base. In 2013, Pershing India Operational Services Private 
Limited (Pershing India) was established in Chennai. Currently, the company have over 
12,000 employees across our three locations: Mumbai, Pune and Chennai 
No of Rounds: 3 (Online Test + Technical interview + Technical & HR interview) 
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month 
Duration of internship: 7 weeks 
Round 1 – Online Assessment 
The online assessment was conducted on the Hackerrank platform, there were a total of 4 
questions that were asked, which consisted of 1 easy, 2 medium and 1 hard question 
Duration: 2 hours 
The questions that I had gotten are as follows: 
1. Category: Medium 
Topic: Graphs  
 
A server network is represented as a tree of g_nodes servers indexed from 1 to 
g_nodes and g_nodes-1 edges where ith edges connect the servers g_from[i] and 
g_to[i] the transfer time between any 2 connected servers is 1 unit. Given the graph 
g find maximum time taken to transfer the data between any 2 servers in the system. 
Write a function getMaxTime in python with the parameters:  
int g_nodes: the no of servers in the network  
int g_from[n]: the end point of edges  
int g_to[n]: the end point of edges  
 
Function returns: 
int: the maximum time to transfer data between 2 servers 
 
 
2. Category: Hard 
Topic: Graphs  
 
A game contains a maze represented as a graph with n vertices numbered 1 to n and 
m bidirectional edges numbered 1 to m. The ith edge connects endpoint1[i] and 
endpoint2[i]. It takes edheLength[i] units of time to traverse the ith edge. Each vertex 
I will disappear at time t[i] and will not reappear. If a visit occurs at the same time the 
vertex disappears, the vertex cannot be reached. The game starts at vertex 1 and 
time 0. Find the earliest visit time for each vertex starting from vertex 1. If a vertex 
cannot be reached the arrival time is -1 Write python function mazeGame that has 
following parameters  
Int t[n] : array of integers the ith vertex goes away at t[i] 1 based index 
             int endpoint1[m]: vertex no of 1 end of an edge[i] 1 based index  
             int endpoint2[m]: vertex number of the other end edge[i] 1 based index  
             Int edheLength[m] : edheLength[i] is the time it takes to traverse edge[i] 1 based 
index  
Returns:  
int[m]: the arrival times at each vertex I or -1 if visit is not possible 
3. Category: Easy 
Topic: Single Dimensional Arrays 
 
There are n devices in the network where the vulnerability of ith device is 
represented by vulnerability[i]. All devices in a security level must have the same 
vulnerability level and the number of devices in any 2 security levels should not differ 
by more than 1 Given the array vulnerability, find the maximum no of security levels 
needed to ensure protection of network Write a function getMinimumGroups that 
has the parameter:  
Int vulnerability[i] : the vulnerabilities of devices  
 
Returns  
Int: the minimum no of groups required 
 
4. Category: Medium 
Topic: Multi-Dimensional Arrays 
 
There are n customers and m shops in HackerMall. The no of items of each type in a 
shop is unlimited so several customers can buy items of same type in a shop A 
customer is considered as served if all types of items the customer wishes to 
purchase are available in open shops. Initially the shops are closed. Find maximum 
no of customers who can be served by the mall if almost 2 shops are opened to serve 
the customers Write function countmaximumcustomers has the following 
parameters:   
int customer[m][p] : the types of items each customer wishes to purchase  
int shop[m][k]: the types of items offered by each shop  
 
Returns  
Int: the maximum no of customers that can be served  
 
Round 2 – Technical Interview 
The interview was conducted in person at CUIC. It went for about 35-40 min.  
The interviewer first introduced herself and in turn asked for my introduction. Then she 
went through my resume and asked me which programming language I’m proficient in. I 
told Python, since I was very familiar with it.  
She then asked me why I chose Python over the other languages, and I told her its because 
its an interpreted language and its easy and user-friendly nature and syntax. 
The interviewer started to test me in Data Structures. She gave me a piece of paper and 
asked me to write down each data structure that I have learnt, draw its representation and 
write functions to perform basic operations like creation, insertion, deletion and search. I 
started off with arrays, how to access elements in an array and wrote functions for 
performing the elementary operations. I explained every line of the code as I was writing. I 
did this for every data structure upto graphs.  
While doing this task, I was asked many questions like: 
 
1. What is the advantage of linked list over other linear data structures? 
2. Given a binary tree, I was asked to write its inorder, preorder and postorder traversal, and 
given two out of three traversals, I was used to construct the corresponding binary tree 
4. What is the difference between tree and graph 
5. Given a graph, write its BFS and DFS traversals and explain the procedure to perform the 
same 
After this was done, the interviewer moved on to asking me basic questions in Python 
programming, to test my knowledge. I was then asked to write an optimal solution to 
remove duplicates in a string, and to perform basic arithmetic operations on matrices. I 
wrote down the programs on paper and explained my approach to her step by step. She was 
satisfied with it. 
Next, the interviewer tested me in DBMS. She gave me a Student database with 4-5 tables in 
it, and asked me to write SQL queries to retrieve appropriate records from the table. This 
task involved the use of joins – equijoin, left join, right join, inner join, natural join, triggers 
and cursors. Given an equijoin query, I was asked to translate it to natural join, left join, 
right join and natural join. This extensively tested my understanding of join operations and 
the basic syntax of various types of joins in SQL. It was a bit challenging but interesting.  
 
This was followed by a few general questions in SQL: 
1. Difference between order by and group by 
2. When do we use having clause 
3. Aggregate functions  
4. Difference between drop and truncate command 
The interviewer concluded by asking me what my favourite subject was, in Computer 
Science, and I said DBMS. She then asked me if I have any questions for her, and I asked her 
what tech stacks we’ll be generally using for doing projects during the summer internship, 
and what prior knowledge we are expected to have.  
Overall, the interview went well and I was shortlisted for the next round 
 
Round 3 – Technical + HR Interview 
It took place in person at CUIC. It went for about 45 min. I was asked to bring my laptop 
along with resume. 
The interviewer greeted me and asked me to introduce myself. Then he went through my 
resume thoroughly. 
Since I’m doing the BS Data Science and Applications program concurrently with my college 
degree and I had mentioned it in my resume, the interviewer was curious to know more 
about the program and its modalities, so I spent a lot of time explaining how I got into the 
program, why I wanted to pursue it and how I had benefited out of the program overall. He 
then asked me a few questions he had about the IITM program and I answered them. 
After this, the interviewer gave me three questions, I was asked to open my laptop, use any 
editor of my choice to type out the code and run the programs in front of him. The 
questions that were given to me were: 
1. Given some numbers in an array, use bubble sort and merge sort to sort the 
numbers in the array, and show the results obtained after every pass 
2. Write code to perform division of 2 numbers without using the ‘/’ operator, and 
multiplication of 2 numbers without using ‘*’ operator 
3. Write optimal solution to reverse a number and string, and to check whether it is a 
palindrome or not 
I had to code in the presence of the interviewer and explain every part of the code as I 
typed. I was asked to write separate functions to perform each operation in a program for 
modularity. 
Following this, I was questioned extensively about the projects that I had put on my resume. 
I had a music management system project and hotel booking system project, and I was 
asked to describe what tech stacks I had used for frontend and backend development. I was 
also told to describe the structure of the database created and how the tables were linked, 
what was the choice of primary key, foreign key and why. I was also asked which normal 
form the tables existed in, and how to normalise it completely. The interviewer was also 
curious to know the unique selling point of my project and what added benefits it offers 
compared to traditional systems 
After this was done, the interviewer looked into my resume and asked me about my 
positions of responsibilities. Since I was a Deputy Director in CSAU, ACM-CEG, Guindy Times, 
I explained about my role in each of these clubs and told him what kind of sessions and 
programs each of these clubs conduct in college. He was eager to know more about these 
clubs and also suggested a few sessions and events that technical clubs in our college could 
conduct, to attract many people. It was a very friendly conversation overall.  
The interviewer also asked me how I was able to manage being in so many clubs while 
pursuing a dual degree, and I told him that managing time wisely was the key. 
Finally, the interviewer asked me whether I have any questions for him. I asked him about 
the various teams for summer internship at BNY and what each team does. I then thanked 
the interviewer and came out. 
After an hour, the results were announced.  
Overall, 9 of us from CEG and MIT got selected for the summer internship program at BNY 
Mellon, and I was one among them. 
Tips: 
1. Be confident with what you put on your resume. Do not fake anything there as you 
can be questioned on anything there, and you are expected to be thorough with 
what is mentioned there. 
2. Have 2-3 solid projects to put on the resume. This is very crucial, as you are 
interviewing for a technical role. So, the interviewer would be more interested to 
check out your current knowledge and gauge your understanding of various tech 
stacks, to know whether you’ll be able to handle any project given to you during the 
internship 
3. Core concepts that you need to know – DSA,DBMS, OS, OOPS. This forms the main 
part of testing in any interview. 
4. Never panic if you don’t answer a couple of questions in the interview. The 
interviewer is just seeing how you would act in different situations. 
5. Prepare well beforehand for Tell me about yourself. Remember - what you say in 
this is going to drive the rest of your interview, so ensure that you highlight all your 
strengths, hobbies and achievements in your introduction. 
6. Maintain a good rapport with the interviewer. That is very crucial. Keep a smile on 
your face throughout. Show them that you are genuinely interested about the role. 
 
If you have any further questions, feel free to reach out to me

Quality Engineering Intern – Appian 
Name : AJAY M S 
Reg No. : 2023503053 
Role : Quality Engineering Intern 
About the company: 
Appian Corporation is a leading provider of a low-code automation platform 
that enables organizations to rapidly design, build, and deploy enterprise 
applications with minimal hand-coding. Appian integrates process automation, 
case management, AI, robotic process automation (RPA), business rules, and 
data integration into a unified environment. Its platform empowers businesses 
to streamline workflows, improve operational efficiency, and accelerate digital 
transformation. Appian serves industries such as financial services, 
government, healthcare, and manufacturing, providing both cloud-based and 
on-premises deployment options. 
Online Assessment: 
The Appian online assessment consisted of two primary sections: 
1. Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs) – These covered a range of topics, 
including: 
o Aptitude: Logical reasoning, numerical problem-solving, and 
puzzles. 
o Basic SQL: Basic use of keywords and retrieval of data from a 
database. 
2. Coding Challenge – This section assessed problem-solving and 
programming skills. The specific task involved writing a program to 
determine whether a given list of strings contained any duplicate values. 
The solution was simple - iterating through the array and returning an 
appropriate result indicating the presence or absence of duplicates. 
Interview: 
The interview began on a cordial note with the classic “Tell me about yourself” 
opener, giving me a chance to set the stage. Without much delay, the 
interviewer presented me with a simple DSA challenge: find the maximum 
element in an array. While this might sound like a warm-up problem, it was 
relevant to the analytical nature of the Quality Engineer role — after all, even 
testers need to think like problem-solvers. 
Next, we started discussing about my projects. One idea in particular caught 
the recruiter’s attention, and the conversation went from “Tell me more” to a 
full-on deep dive into the origin, functionality, and execution of the project. 
This set the tone for the next phase: testing knowledge. I explained the 
different types of testing I had implemented in my “Hospital Appointment 
System” Project, covering Unit Testing, Integration Testing, and Regression 
Testing. 
Then came the main stuff — questions about Artificial Intelligence and 
whether it could design all test cases. I stood my ground, explaining that while 
AI is powerful, it lacks the hands-on experience of a seasoned quality engineer 
and might hallucinate (yes, I used the word) or overlook tricky edge cases. 
When asked why AI couldn’t test a project it had developed, I explained that AI 
is trained on existing data, while a human tester uses real-world insight to 
design precise, context-aware test cases. The interviewer countered that AI 
could do it, but I held my stance — politely but firmly. 
We then shifted to Selenium, where I described how I used it to test a login 
page, walking the interviewer through the process from initiating a browser 
connection to gracefully shutting it down after testing. 
Before wrapping up, I asked about the secret behind Appian’s sustained 
success, and the interviewer’s answer was as flawless as their product pitch. 
Round Two (HR): This was a mix of introductions, “Why Appian?” and “Why 
this role?” — the standard HR essentials. 
Round Three (Final HR): A quick check on whether I was comfortable with the 
role and a subtle nudge to see if I preferred a developer position instead. I 
reaffirmed my interest in Quality Engineering and assured them I would align 
with the company’s principles. 
Tips: 
For a Quality Engineer Intern role, a solid understanding of the Software 
Testing Life Cycle (STLC), Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), and core 
testing concepts is essential. Focus on fundamental DSA topics such as arrays, 
strings, hash maps, and sets, as these are typically sufficient for the coding 
portion. Familiarity with testing tools like Selenium is a valuable advantage. 
Having a basic understanding of CI/CD pipelines can also strengthen your 
profile. 
Be thoroughly prepared to discuss your projects in detail, as most technical 
questions will stem from your resume. Only list skills and topics in your resume 
that you are confident in, and ensure you can explain them clearly. Be clear in 
whatever you speak and BE CONFIDENT – your body language and attitude tells 
them a lot about you before you speak. Dress professionally, and ensure proper 
grooming to create a strong first impression. 
ALL THE BEST!!

ARCESIUM INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 2021 – 22 
Arcesium is a global financial technology and professional services firm, delivering 
solutions to some of the world's most sophisticated financial institutions, including hedge funds, 
banks, and institutional asset managers. 
There were 4 rounds of  selection process, 1 online test (23/08/2021) and 3 technical 
rounds (27/08/2021) . 
Online Test :  (80 mins) 
Platform : HackerRank 
Section 1 : Aptitude – 15 MCQs (20 mins) : 
Questions difficulty level ranged from medium to high. This section had negative marking. 
Section 2 : Technical MCQs – 15 MCQs (15 mins) :  
Questions were challenging and covered a variety of concepts. This section also had negative 
marking. Concepts covered :  
 Predict the output for C++ and java code snippets – based on pointer manipulation in C++, 
inheritance based questions, interfaces in java. 
 DBMS – SQL queries 
 OS – process, memory management, etc. 
Section 3 : Coding – 2 questions (45 mins) 
1. Naruto Clan Curse – It is an application of level-order traversal on binary tree, where we 
have to return the avg of all the unaffected node values from each level. Min value of the 
extreme nodes of each level is calculated. A node is affected when its value is less than the 
above min value.  
2. ATM Change problem – It is a dynamic programming problem. A req amount, preferred 
denomination and set of available denominations are given. We have to return the number 
of  ways to make change. 
Ex : Amount = 1200  Preferred denomination = 500  denominations = [ 100, 200, 500] 
1. 1200 = 500 + 500 + 100 + 100 
2. 1200 = 500 + 500 + 200 
Output : 2 
 
Solution : Modification of  https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/coin-change-dp-7/ 
 
Technical Round 1 : (1 hour) 
This round was a pair coding round. 
I was given a backtracking question. First asked to write the pseudo code and optimize it. At last, 
code it in the language of your choice. 
Question : Given n x n matrix filled with 1, 2, 3 . Find the path with max reward from src to dest. 
1 -> move one cell right 
2 -> move one cell down 
3 -> movie one step either right or down 
1 2 1 2 
3 3 1 2 
1 2 1 2 
3 1 1 2  
Src = (0, 0) 
Dest = (3, 3) 
Max reward path = 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 2 = 13  
 
Technical Round 2 : ( 1 hour) 
This round assessed the technical skills and projects mentioned in the resume. 
1. Asked to explain the database design of the DBMS project mentioned in my resume. 
Discussed about the scalability and potential security issues of the project. 
2. Several concepts of DBMS such as – Indexing, SQL queries, views – were also discussed as 
a part of project discussion. 
3. Brief discussion on several OOPs concepts and Java, as I mentioned Java in my resume, 
on : 
 JVM and platform independency of java 
 Garbage collection in java 
 Exception handling  
 Finalize keyword 
 Virtual functions 
 Method overloading vs over-riding 
 Inheritance 
 Abstract , static in java 
4. Questions from the following OS Concepts were asked : 
 Interrupts 
 Paging & Page fault 
 Thrashing 
 Deadlocks and its avoidance ( Banker’s algorithm) 
 
 
 
Technical Round 3 : (1 hour) 
I was given few coding questions and come up with an algorithm with specified time and space 
complexities. 
Note : Discuss all possible edge cases and efficiency of the algorithm in each case. 
1. Given an array of n consecutive integers where one of the element is over-written by 
another element in the array. Now this array is shuffled and find the element being over-
written with minimum time as possible without any auxiliary space. 
Ex: A[] = [51, 52, 53, 54, 55] -> [51, 52, 53, 52, 55] -> [52, 55, 51, 53, 52] 
      Result = 54 , as 52 over-writes 54 
 
2. Given a sorted array where all elements except one element is repeated twice. Find the 
distinct element with minimum time and O(1) space. 
Ex : A[] = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 5]  Result = 3 
 
3. Given an mathematical expression place the parenthesis such that value of the expression 
is maximum. 
Ex : 5*2-4/1+3 
Ref : https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/all-ways-to-add-parenthesis-for-evaluation/ 
 
Key takeaway and concepts to be covered : 
1. Prepare “Tell about yourself” as attractive and concise, covering the key points of your 
resume. Answer confidently and try the questions you don’t know, don’t leave them 
unanswered. They’ll test your knowledge on the skills you mentioned in the resume, so be 
clear on those concepts. 
2. When given a coding question, first explain your intuition and analyze it’s time and space 
complexities and try to optimize it as much as you can. Feel free to use a pen and paper to 
solve and also try to approach the problem mathematically if possible. 
3. DSA – Arrays, Trees, Sorting, Heaps, Backtracking and Dynamic programming – among 
others. 
4. DBMS – Database design and relation schema, Indexing and hashing, SQL, Views, Triggers. 
5. OS – Basics of OS, Process and synchronization, deadlocks, memory management 
(especially Paging) 
6. OOPS – Fundamentals, Inheritance types, Polymorphism types, Singleton class 
implementation (not asked to me, but worth learning). 
7. Revise some common interview questions on the above mentioned concepts. 
8. Company archives in GFG gives a prior idea on the online test and technical interviews. 
Also practice the coding questions asked in the past from GFG Practice.

SAP Labs Interview Experience 
-Ansh Bomb (CSE 2021-2025) 
About the company: 
SAP (short for Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is one of the biggest 
names in enterprise software, helping businesses around the world run smarter, whether it’s 
managing their supply chains, handling finances, or making sense of their data. Headquartered 
in Germany, SAP has been around for over 50 years and works with companies across almost 
every industry you can think of (they are pretty much omnipresent - being a b2b company 
integrated with almost every major company today). For students and early-career 
professionals, it’s a great place to explore both tech and impact at a global level. 
 
Selection Process: 
Online Assessment 
466 students were shortlisted (based on eligibility criteria) for the online assessment. It spanned 
45 minutes and included 2 DSA questions. Platform - SHL. The questions were of easy-medium 
level, and I am not sure what the shortlisting criteria for the next round were; cause I wasn’t 
very fast (spent more time than I should have on the second question). 
First Question (Easy): Different products were being stored at specific temperatures (vector of 
products). Each product had a required storage temperature, and we had to figure out how many 
of them needed to be stored below zero and reflect those. 
Second Question (Medium): Don’t fully recollect it but the problem focused on tracking 
category-specific elements within a dynamically shifting window of pizzas and their types xD. 
At each step, the goal was to identify the earliest occurrence of a given type of pizza (meat or 
vegan) within a bounded, sliding display window (like the ones we see in Dominoes, etc) and 
list out such occurrences (including at times it wasn’t observed to be there). 
Result: Post this OA, ~35 students were shortlisted for the in-person interviews. 
 
Round 1 – Technical 
The interviewer almost immediately jumped into my resume and asked me technical questions 
about my previous internship and projects. It is crucial to know your project at a conceptual 
level as the interviewer tested fundamental concepts and components involved in the listed 
projects and internships. Post this, a few questions on DSA (linked lists, basic trees, etc) were 
asked. I did not have to code out anything, only explain my approaches.  
Sample question - Would you use a stack or a queue for DFS? And which one for BFS? 
We kept jumping into different DSA questions. In fact, during the interview, my project (listed 
on the resume) was also tied into a DSA question. I do not recollect whether there were any 
OOPS or DBMS questions in this round, but I think there was one OS question asked. This 
round lasted for nearly an hour. 
 
Round 2 – Technical 
There were two interviewers. This one included a lot more DSA. I was asked to write out the 
code for one question, and although my initial approach was passable, they walked me through 
it to identify edge case optimizations. My solution’s time complexity was horrendous, but let’s 
not get into that. I was also tested on a few basic and medium-level OOPS and DBMS concepts. 
Additionally, there was a scenario-based question, where I had to ideate a solution to a business 
case, following which my high-level approach was broken down into smaller tasks. I was asked 
about the technical implementation of these smaller tasks. File handling, etc was covered, and 
I forgot the exact syntax for BufferedReader but luckily the interviewers did not penalize me 
for that, and said they were interested in the logic and approach. A few fun riddles were also 
asked (human-computer 21 matches, etc), and before I knew it almost 1.5 hours had gone by. 
Result: ~10-12 people were shortlisted for the next round. 
  
Round 3 – Techno-Managerial 
This was perhaps the most perplexing round. We started with a discussion about my interests, 
and I mentioned current affairs, Both the interviewers then started asking me about politics, 
GK, etc. We then moved on to my resume. Almost every single line of my resume was analyzed 
and questions were asked. My strong recommendation is to be honest on your resume and know 
everything you have listed there. Do not list out technical skills just for the sake of it, they will 
probably ask you questions from it. I was even asked a few in-depth technical questions about 
topics in my research papers. This round also involved asking me about my thoughts on Climate 
Change, etc (all tied to SAP’s current branding and initiatives).  
Result: ~6 people were shortlisted for the HR round. 
 
Round 4 – HR 
Standard, as far as HR rounds ago. Was asked about what SAP works on (being a B2B 
company, people outside the industry rarely know about it), SAP initiatives, about myself, etc.  
Result: 4 people were offered a position at SAP Labs, Bangalore. 
 
Some general pointers –  
1. For DSA, I practiced using ACM-CEG’s resources, Strivers A2Z Sheet and looking up 
company-specific most frequently asked questions – available on sites like gfg. 
2. Go through crash courses the day before the interview for OOPS, DBMS, OS, etc. 
3. It’s ok to say you are unsure about an answer, they don’t expect you to know everything. 
All the best and good luck 
            
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
You can reach out to me on my LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/ansh-bomb/ or contact 
me on my email id anshbomb16@gmail.com in case you want to connect  .

Infinera Placement Interview Experience   
  
Role: Software Developer  
Number of Rounds: 4 offline  
Job Location: Bengaluru   
Job Description: Infinera India Private Limited is a leading provider of intelligent 
optical communication networks, where you'll work on cutting-edge technologies 
like photonic integrated circuits, software-defined networking, and machine learning 
to develop innovative solutions for high-performance networking, long-haul optical 
transport, and data center interconnects. You'll be involved in optimizing global data 
transfer, building scalable, high-capacity networks, and driving next-generation 
advancements using diverse platforms such as C/C++, FPGA, Python, and Linux. At 
Infinera, you'll contribute to creating sustainable, efficient communication systems 
that power the global internet and connect businesses worldwide.  
   
1st Offline Round:    
MCQs: Aptitude, OS, CN and OOPs  
Difficulty Level: Easy   
Coding Questions: 1 coding question was asked.   
1. Given a string (sentence), return the word with second highest frequency. If 
there are multiple words with same frequency, return the lexicographically 
smaller string   
  
Totally 18 candidates were selected for next round.   
   
Technical Round 1 (45mins):   
Difficulty Level: Easy  
After the initial introduction, the interviewer asked me some questions about 
objectoriented programming (OOP) concepts, followed by a request to implement 
them using Java. We then discussed my internship experience and the work I had 
done there. To assess my C++ skills, I was given two straightforward coding 
questions: one to generate Fibonacci numbers and another to check if two strings 
are anagrams. I had to write the code in notepad and explain my approach to the 
interviewer. We also had a brief discussion about the projects I had highlighted on 
my resume.  
  
Technical Round 2 (1 hr):   
Difficult Level: Medium   
Computer Networks (CN):  
The questions covered various protocols such as HTTP, FTP, and SFTP, as well as the 
differences between UDP and TCP protocols. We also discussed the OSI and TCP/IP 
network layers, and I was asked to explain how a message is transmitted using these 
models.  
Operating Systems (OS):  
The interviewer then focused on operating system concepts. We discussed virtual 
memory and its purpose, the difference between paging and segmentation, and 
deadlock prevention techniques. I was also asked about various CPU scheduling 
algorithms and some application-based questions, such as how memory can be 
efficiently utilized if two users are running the same web browser simultaneously. I 
explained the memory layout of a process, which includes code, text, stack, and heap 
segments. While I could describe the layout, I initially could not identify that the code 
and text segments could be shared between both instances of the web browser.  
Logical Problem:  
Finally, I was given a logical problem: how to compute the average of two integer 
numbers without causing overflow. I proposed several solutions, such as using (a/2 + 
b/2), handling edge cases for overflow, and type casting the calculation to double 
and converting it back to integer. However, the expected solution was a + (b - a) / 2.  
5 people were shortlisted after this round.   
   
HR Round (10-15 minutes):   
This HR round was mostly a formality. I was asked what I liked about the company 
where I interned and why I would prefer Infinera over that company. We also 
touched on some of the soft skills mentioned in my resume and had a casual 
conversation about the company's work and culture. The interviewer even inquired 
about my family background.   
Finally, 5 people were selected.   
 My advice:   
• 
Always prepare based on the company's focus areas. Knowing that the 
company emphasized operating systems (OS) and computer networks (CN), I 
tailored my preparation accordingly.  
• 
Be honest in your answers because they will always cross question based on 
your answer.  
• 
Communication is key—it is important to clearly share your thought process, 
whether or not you are confident in the answer.  
• 
Since the interview was mostly theoretical, there were some concepts I was 
not fully familiar with, but instead of saying "I don't know," I made an effort to 
provide relevant answers based on my understanding.
