D.E. SHAW INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
About the company: 
The D. E. Shaw Group (DESCO) is a global investment and technology development firm with over 
$60 billion in investment capital as of March 2024, and offices across North America, Europe, and 
Asia. Founded in 1988, the firm has earned a strong reputation for innovation, risk management, and 
the quality of its staff. DESCO maintains a significant presence in capital markets worldwide, investing 
in diverse companies and financial instruments. D. E. Shaw India (DESIS), established in Hyderabad in 
1996, is a core member of the Global Information Technology Group, providing research, 
development, and support for the firm’s global initiatives. 
Role 
 : Software Development Engineer in Test – 2 Month summer Internship 
Stipend : 2,00,000 INR / Month 
 
Selection Process: 
1. Online Test  
2. Technical Interview 1  
3. HR Round  
4. Technical Interview 2 
Online Test: 
• 
Coding questions: 2 (20 + 30 minutes) 
• 
Technical MCQs: 12 (17 minutes) 
• 
Aptitude MCQs: 14 (18 minutes) 
The total test duration is 75 minutes and is conducted on the HackerRank platform in the campus 
computer center. 
Coding Questions: 
1. You have to select a subset of size k from an array of integers and calculate the sum of the 
absolute differences between all pairs of elements in that subset. Among all possible subsets of size 
k, you need to find the maximum possible value of this sum. 
For example, consider the array [2, 3, 1, 2] and k = 3. You generate all possible subsets of size 3, 
namely [2,1,2], [2,3,1], [2,3,2], and [3,1,2]. For each subset, calculate the sum of absolute differences 
between all pairs. For [2,3,1], the sum is ∣2−3∣+∣2−1∣+∣3−1∣=1+1+2=4. The maximum value across all 
subsets is 4. 
I solved this using a backtracking approach. I generated all possible subsets to find the solution and 
was able to pass all three example test cases. 
 
2. You have to construct the lexicographically smallest possible string, called post, from a given string 
s, using two helper strings: pre and post. You are allowed only two operations: 
1. Take the first character of s and append it to the end of pre. 
2. Take the last character of pre and append it to the end of post. 
The goal is to carefully sequence these operations so that once all characters have been moved, the 
final string post is as small as possible in dictionary order. (Using a Robot to Print the 
Lexicographically Smallest String - LeetCode) 
For example, if s = "zza", we want to form the smallest possible string. Step by step: 
• 
Start with pre = "" and post = "". 
• 
Move all characters from s to pre, so pre = "zza", s = "". 
• 
Then, repeatedly move characters from the end of pre to post. First, post = "a" (moving the 
last character). Then post = "az". Finally, post = "azz". 
• 
The result is "azz", which is the lexicographically smallest arrangement achievable under the 
given rules. 
I implemented the whole logic using stack and greedy approach but could not pass the example test 
cases. 
 
Technical and Aptitude Questions 
The technical questions are from core subjects: DBMS, OOPS, OS, and Networks. They include 
sample programs for output prediction and error finding. All questions are easy to medium difficulty. 
The aptitude questions are mathematical and required more effort to solve. The MCQs have negative 
marking (+3/-1). 
Out of 511 applicants, 17 students were selected for the interview process. I was one of them. 
 
Technical Interview 1: 1 hour 
This round was primarily focused on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) questions.  
After I introduced myself, we began the interview. 
1. You are given a 2D array, distances, of size k×n, where distances[i][j] represents the distance 
jumped by player j in round i. In the first round, players jump in ascending order of their IDs (0, 1, 2, 
..., n−1). From the second round onward, the jumping order is decided by the previous round’s 
results, with the player who made the longest jump going first. The winner is the player who 
achieves the single longest jump across all rounds. Return the ID of the winner. 
Input: distances = [[5, 8, 10, 9], [6, 2, 10, 4.5], [11, 9, 5, 6]],  
Output: 0 
 
Explanation: 
• 
Round 1 order = [0, 1, 2, 3] → jumps = [5, 8, 10, 9] → next order = [2, 3, 1, 0] 
• 
Round 2 order = [2, 3, 1, 0] → jumps = [10, 4.5, 2, 6] → next order = [2, 0, 3, 1] 
• 
Round 3 order = [2, 0, 3, 1] → jumps = [5, 11, 6, 9] 
Maximum jumps: Player 0 = 11, Player 1 = 9, Player 2 = 10, Player 3 = 9. The winner is Player 0. 
I was able to solve this problem. First, I paired the first round's distances with their respective player 
IDs. I tracked the maximum distance of each player in a separate array and then sorted them based 
on distance to determine the order for the next round. I continued this process for all subsequent 
rounds. 
 
2. You are given a mountain array arr (which is strictly increasing to a peak, then strictly decreasing). 
Find the peak element in the array. Your solution must run in O(logn) time complexity. (Peak index in 
a mountain array - Leetcode)  
Input: arr = [1, 3, 5, 7, 6, 2] 
Output: 7 
I initially provided a linear solution, then explained the binary search approach. Interviewers then 
asked me to write the binary search code on paper. 
 
3. Given a string of words and a maximum width (maxWidth), you need to write code to format the 
text such that each line is fully justified. (Text justification - Leetcode) 
I first provided a solution, and then they continuously added more constraints and different edge 
cases. This went on for quite some time, with additional constraints, modifications, and logic being 
added to the code. In the end, I was able to satisfy them with my solution. 
 
4. You are given an employee table with the following columns: empID (primary key), ManagerID. 
You need to write a query that outputs every pair of employees who have the same manager. 
I wrote the query using a self-join. 
 
They then moved on to some general computer-related questions. The first question was, "What is a 
CPU?" I answered this. The next question was, "Why does a computer system become slow or less 
efficient after long use?" I discussed both general and technical reasons with them. 
Finally, they asked for a brief introduction in the last few minutes of the interview. 
 
 
 
HR Round: 25 Minutes 
This round primarily tested my interest in the job and my project management skills. The questions 
were related to testing and project management. 
1. What is your interest in software? 
2. What is testing? 
3. If we offered you two opportunities, one in software development and one in testing, which 
would you choose? 
4. What is your experience with testing? 
5. Tell me about your projects. 
6. What was your role in your projects? 
7. How would you handle your team if you were a leader? 
8. How would you handle a slow learner or a beginner on your team? 
After I answered these questions, HR allowed me to ask some of my own. I asked two questions 
about the company and the internship. 
 
Technical Round 2: 1 hour 10 minutes 
This interview began with my introduction. The interviewers asked me some questions from my 
resume about programming languages. 
1. What is the difference between a process and a thread?  
I answered with the definitions and provided some examples. 
 
2. Design a lift system. There are two lifts, and you have to design it in such a way that it can be 
handled by a single button on each floor. They wanted me to come up with a system and different 
test cases. 
I gave a priority queue-based lift system and provided some test cases. They asked me to draw a 
flowchart for the system's workflow and posed a specific test case to see if my system could handle 
it. I confirmed that my solution would pass the test.  
Finally, they asked me to write three more test cases for the same system. After a few minutes of 
thinking, I provided two. Then, we moved on to the next question. 
 
3. You are given a 2D grid (array) where each cell contains an integer. Your goal is to find the 
maximum path sum when traveling from the first column to the last column. You can start from any 
cell in the first column and move to any cell in the last column. (Maximum path sum in matrix - 
GeeksforGeeks) 
The allowed movements are: 
Move directly to the cell to the right, Move diagonally up-right and Move diagonally down-right. 
Input Format: [[3, 1, 2], [6, 5, 4], [7, 8, 9]] 
Output: 24 (7 -> 8 -> 9) 
I gave a recursive solution and discussed its space and time complexity. I was then asked to optimize. 
After a tip from the interviewer, I was able to give the DP solution. I was asked to write the code for 
both the recursive and the dynamic programming (DP) solutions on paper. 
 
4. You are given two arrays: a) Weights: An array where each element represents the weight of a 
stone. b) Directions: An array where each element represents the direction in which the stone is 
moving ('R' for right and 'L' for left).  
When stones collide, the stone with the larger weight remains while the lighter stone is destroyed. If 
both stones have the same weight, both are destroyed. Stones continue to collide and be destroyed 
until no more collisions occur. Your task is to write a function that returns the indices of the stones 
that remain after all collisions have been resolved. (Asteroid Collision - LeetCode) 
Input Format: 
Weights: [10, 5, 8, 6, 3] 
Directions: ['R', 'R', 'L', 'L', 'R'] 
Output: [0, 2] 
I had not solved the asteroid collision problem before. I came up with an O(n2) solution that involved 
a loop to check all adjacent nodes for collisions, repeating this process until no more collisions 
occurred. I was then asked to write the code for this. When I asked for more time to optimize, they 
told me that this solution was sufficient. 
Finally, they allowed me to ask some questions. I asked if my explanation was clear or if I needed to 
improve, and I also asked for their advice on a future career in the software industry. 
 
Then the results came out. Out of the 17 students, 3 students were selected for a summer 
internship, including me. I was from MIT, and the other two students were from CEG. 
After completing our two-month internship, we all received Pre-Placement Offer (PPO) from the 
company. (I'm writing this after the internship has ended.) 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Tips: 
• 
Be consistent and stay positive. 
• 
You don't have to be perfect - just keep trying. 
• 
Struggles and self-doubt are completely normal parts of the learning process. 
• 
Learn to communicate clearly. 
• 
LeetCode is great for practicing competitive coding, and HackerRank is, too. 
• 
NeetCode.io and Striver are free gems for preparation. (Many other resources are available; 
choose the one that best suits you.) 
• 
Revise your core concepts regularly, and practice aptitude questions. 
 
“You don’t have to be selected to prove you’re the best. Just give your best, be proud of 
your effort, and trust that hard work always pays off. All the best  ”

AMAZON INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
About the company-   Amazon is one of the MAANG (formerly FAANG) 
companies. It is an American multinational technology company, engaged in e-
commerce, cloud computing, online advertising, digital streaming, and artificial 
intelligence 
Role Offered- Software Development Engineer 
Stipend- 1,10,000 INR + 400 USD ( Relocation allowance ) per month 
Duration -   2 Months 
Location -   Bengaluru,Hyderabad,Chennai (Available locations)  
Selection process- 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview (Virtual) 
Online Assessment - 27th August 2024 
The Online Assessment was conducted on Amazon’s own platform ( I don’t 
remember its name ) with a total duration of 120 minutes. The online 
assessment had 3 sections- 
• Coding assessment ( 2 questions , Time: 70 minutes) 
• Workstyles Assessment  
• Work Simulation 
 
Workstyle Assessment : 
  
 
The second section is built around Amazon’s Leadership principles. This section 
asks you to choose statements that represent your work style. There is no 
practice needed for this portion of the assessment. 
This section has NO TIME LIMIT. 
 
 
 
Work Simulation: 
 During the simulation, you’ll be prompted with messages which replicate real-
time working scenario  to solve problems.  
For each scenario, you’re provided with four to five viable options to respond to. 
You’re asked to rate the effectiveness of each response or select the single 
response that you think is best.  
This is kind of a Behavioural test. This section has no time limit BUT the duration 
took to complete this section will be considered for evaluation. 
 
TIP: In any coding interview , the description of the questions will be lengthy and 
not clear, reading the entire question could consume a lot of time.SO TRY to 
read the last paragraph or the last few lines of the question which is the real 
problem statement. 
Coding Question 1 
Given Two integer arrays which depict the location of AWS server stations and 
destination stations. Find the minimum sum of absolute difference between the 
stations. 
 
Example input –  
 
Center = [1,2,2] //positions of the server stations  
Destination = [5,2,4] //positions of the receiver stations 
Output- 
6 
Approach: 
Sort both arrays and find the sum of  absolute difference. 
 
Coding Question 2 
Given a list of strings patterns, each consisting of characters and wildcard 
symbols (?), determine the minimum number of columns where a character 
conflict occurs. A character conflict in a column happens when different non-
wildcard characters are found in that column. Wildcard symbols are ignored in 
conflict checks. Return the count of such conflicting columns. 
 
Example: 
 input :- 
patterns = [“hub”, ”gub”, ’gu*”] 
Output – “*u*”  –  2 (count of * is 2) 
 
Approach: 
For each column, we shall track characters seen so far, ignoring wildcard 
characters (?). If two non-wildcard characters differ within the same column, it 
indicates a conflict, and we increment the count of such conflicts. 
 
 
Note: I assume that the non coding part of assessment had a more weightage 
than the coding part, because few people who solved both questions were not 
shortlisted whereas people with both partial outputs were qualified for the 
next Round. 
 
 
Based on the results of the Online Assessment, almost 55 people were 
shortlisted for the Interview. 
 
Technical Interview (1 hour) – 2nd September 2024 
This was a virtual interview hosted in Amazon’s Chime platform. There were two 
panelist , but only one person was interacting with me, the other was an 
observer. 
 
The interviewer started by asking a brief intro about myself and then they both 
introduced themselves. Following this, I was  asked to share my screen and use 
LiveCode to tackle the coding questions. It is just a text editor , where both me 
and interviewer have access to edit. 
The interviewer pastes the question in the editor and asks to read the question. 
 
Always read the question at least two – three times, at first you may think that it 
is simple and known question but sometimes there may be a small twist in any 
part of the question statement. 
 
Try to clarify any doubt that arises to you about the question. 
Usually in any interview panel members want to see how good a candidate can 
express themselves  and communicate what they think than the correct  solution 
for a question. So explain your thought process on how to solve this problem. 
 
Question 1 : 
Given a string which has few characters repeated multiple times, return a string 
which has each character repeated only once. 
Since there would be more than one possible ways of removing repeated 
characters , return the string which is the first occurring instance when all the 
possible answers are sorted according to dictionary order. 
Example: 
 “dcsccdd” 
Ans - “csd” 
Explanation : 
The output string should contain all the unique characters in input string without 
repetition ,also the characters must be the same order as in input string. 
 
Possible strings=[“dcs”,”csd”,”scd”] 
Lexicographically smallest one = “csd” 
 
Some more examples: 
• Input =”ss” Output = “s” 
• Input =”bcac” Output = “bac” 
• Input =” abddccd” Output = “abcd” 
 
At first I explained my approach using heaps, but he asked me to try a better 
approach. After few minutes , I came up with a Trie approach , he agreed with 
my solution , yet he asked me to try for a more easier and optimal approach. 
 
He was giving me hints like , “When you want to solve a string problem for 
lexicographically smallest one , what data structure will you use ??” 
I seriously didn’t know what to use at that moment, I was repeatedly saying my 
previous approach .  30 minutes went like this , so he himself gave  one  more 
clue – “ Arjun think whether you can solve this using a Stack data structure …“ 
 
After this I tried  solving it using a stack , and the solution was pretty simple, 
and I was like  “Why did I miss this one “ . I immediately said my approach 
to him and he also agreed with my approach and told me to type the code in 
any of my preferred language. 
 
I started to code my approach and while doing so I started to explain him why I 
used this data structure and why I’m using this variable. He interrupted me said 
not to explain now, explain after I complete the code. 
BUT it is good to explain your code as you type. 
 
After completing my code , I explained him each and every line  and its 
functionality. He was somewhat doubtful about my coded solution, so he gave 
me several examples and asked me to dry run my code for them. 
He also enquired about the time and space complexity of my code. 
After 5 such dry runs , he finally accepted my solution and went to the next 
question. 
By this time , I used 45 minutes of my interview . 
Question 2: 
He mentioned that we are almost at the  end of interview and here is your 2nd 
question. I don’t know whether it was the time that was left out  or his 
impression on me from the 1st question  ,which made him give me this question. 
 
Q2: Given a linked list, detect if a cycle is present or not. 
Example: 
 
1 -> 2     ( 1->2->4->5->1…) 
|     |  
5<-  4 
Answer : true 
 
Approach: 
I used the slow-fast pointer method (two pointer or Hare-Tortoise technique) 
 
After I said this approach , he asked  me to write the code for it. 
Since it was already late , even before I finish my code he said that ,”I 
understand your code , It is the correct answer. Time is up . Bye. Thank you “  
I tried to stop him and told him  the time and space complexity of my solution. 
 
It is good to mention the time and space complexities of your code. 
 
After this he waited few minutes and asked whether I have any questions. 
I remembered that the other panel member mentioned that he is in Amazon 
for the past 7  years, so I asked him-  Apart from Amazon being a tech giant and 
market leader in e-commerce ,what makes someone to stay in the same 
company for this long period in a field where shifting companies is very 
common? 
He then replied to my question and left the meeting. 
Later that night, results were announced , and 13 members including myself 
were offered the internship. 
 
General Tips- 
It’s completely fine if you are not a great coder in the start. You always have 
time to improve your skills. 
• Practice coding in websites like Leetcode, Hackerrank as it helps improve 
problem solving skills. 
• Have a look at questions in Leetcode Premium for the company. 
• Practice ACM CODE questions for an understanding of every single topic 
in DSA. 
• Learn from the experiences of seniors. 
• Try practicing aptitude questions also as it has great importance in the 
online assessment. 
• Improve your speaking skills. The interview will be more of a discussion 
rather than an oral test and the interviewer also notices your ability to 
interact easily. 
• The interviewer is more interested to know your creative answers to 
their coding questions. So let your creativity flow while finding optimized 
solutions. 
• Keep revising old concepts while learning new ones. 
• For Amazon, DSA played a crucial role, so concentrate on it. 
• For Online assessments, do your best to follow all the above mentioned 
tips OR  have a friend  near you during assessment who follows the 
above. 
Last but not the least…. Stay calm and confident! 
All the best!

VISA INC. INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 2022 
BATCH: 2020 – 2024 
 
SELECTION PROCESS FOR: Summer Internship 
 
NAME OF THE COMPANY: VISA Inc., Bangalore 
 
ABOUT THE COMPANY: 
 
Visa Inc. is an American multinational financial services corporation headquartered in 
San Francisco, California. It facilitates electronic funds transfer throughout the world, most 
commonly through Visa-branded credit cards, debit cards and prepaid cards. Visa is often 
misinterpreted as a credit card company, but it is not so. Visa is the world’s leader in digital 
payments. Its mission is to connect the world through the most innovative, reliable and secure 
payment network – enabling individuals, businesses, economies to thrive. 
 
NUMBER OF ROUNDS: 2 
1. Online Assessment – Coding round 
2. Interview – Technical + HR 
 
ONLINE ASSESSMENT – CODING ROUND: 
Platform: HackerRank 
Duration: 60 minutes 
Number of questions: 2 Coding questions (no aptitude questions) 
Level of difficulty: Easy – Medium 
 
 
The test was held on 22nd of August, 2022. It was conducted in online mode under the 
invigilation of college officials, where the students were provided systems at computer lab of 
IST Department to attend the test. Everyone got different set of questions and the following 
were the 2 questions that I got: 
• Given two arrays named a and b of size n and m respectively. Find the maximum number 
of pairs (a[i], b[j]) such that a[i]>b[j], 0<=i<n and 0<=j<m. Also, an element of the array 
can appear at-most in only one pair. 
o Example 1: Array a = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}; Array b = {1, 6, 1, 6, 1}; 
Answer: Maximum Possible Pairs = 3 
Explanation: The possible pairs are: (2, 1), (3, 1), (4,1)  
Note: Many such sets of possible pairs can be found. But the question here is 
to find the number of such possible pairs and not to find the pairs. 
o Example 2: Array a = {3, 2, 3}; Array b = {8, 1, 2}; 
Answer: Maximum Possible Pairs = 2 
Explanation: The possible pairs are: (2, 1), (3, 2) 
This was an easy question, and my solution passed all the testcases given. 
• One unit of an alloy can be made from n different metals. The composition of the n 
different metals required for alloy preparation will be given in composition matrix of size 
n. The current stock availability of each metal and the cost of buying one unit of a metal 
will be given in stock matrix and cost matrix, each of size n. Given a budget (>=1), the task 
is to find the maximum number of units of alloy that can be prepared by utilizing the 
available stock and buying more stock within the budget. 
o Example 1: 
Value of n = 3; Budget = 14; 
Composition = {2, 1, 2}; Stock = {1, 0, 0}; Cost = {2, 2, 1}; 
Answer: Maximum number of units that can be produced = 2 
Explanation: 3 different metals A, B, C are required for alloy preparation. One 
unit of alloy can be prepared by 2 units of Metal A + 1 unit of Metal B + 2 units 
of Metal C. Currently, the available stock is: 1 unit of Metal A, 0 units of Metals 
B and C. Now, we can buy 1 unit of Metal B + 2 units of Metal C for a total cost 
of 6 rupees (1*2 + 2*2) and make the first unit of alloy. We are still left with a 
budget of 8 rupees. Now we can buy, 2 units of Metal A + 1 unit of Metal B + 2 
units of Metal C for a total cost of 8 rupees and produce the second unit of alloy. 
We cannot buy more metals; thus, we stop here. 
o Example 2: 
Value of n = 4; Budget = 45; 
Composition = {3, 2, 3, 1}; Stock = {2, 6, 5, 4}; Cost = {4, 2, 1, 3}; 
Answer: Maximum number of units that can be produced = 3 
Explanation: Cost required for producing first unit of alloy = 4 
 
          Cost required for unit 2 production = 13 
                       Cost required for unit 3 production = 15 
                       Cost required for unit 4 production = 22 
Thus, only 3 units can be produced (4+13+15<=45). 
 
This was a medium level question, and my solution passed 10/15 testcases given. 
The results were declared on 25th August 2022. A total of 14 students were selected for the 
next round.  
 
INTERVIEW: 
 
The interview was conducted on MS Teams, and was about 45 minutes long. It was held 
on 26th August, 2022. Initially the interviewer asked me if I had any question to ask him before 
we moved on to the actual interview. 
 
He then asked me about the projects that I did till now in college. I briefly explained 
each of my three projects that I had put on my resume. Then, he asked me what are the 
subjects that I have studied till now. I listed few courses (OS, DBMS, CA). He then asked me 
which one of those was my favourite subject. I said I liked the Operating Systems course much, 
and gave him a satisfactory reason for choosing it. He then asked me about the topics that I 
studied in OS. I listed a few topics (general introduction to OS and its functionalities, threads, 
process synchronization, deadlocks and virtual memory). The follow back questions on the 
topics were: 
• What is a thread? Difference between a process and a thread. 
• What are deadlocks? 
• What is the difference between mutex and semaphore?  
With this, we moved on to the coding pair of the interview. The HackerRank link was 
shared for the code-pair part. There were totally 4 questions in this part of the interview. 
• Consider the below pseudocode and predict the output if main function is called. 
public class abc { 
 
public void f() { 
 
 
f(); 
 
} 
 
public void main() { 
 
 
f(); 
 
} 
}; 
We had a long discussion on this question, wherein I was not able to come up with the 
correct answer the first time. But, with the hints given by the interviewer, I was able to 
answer it up to some point, after which we moved on to the second question. 
 
• The 
second 
question 
was 
“Detect 
loop 
in 
a 
linked 
list" 
- 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/detect-loop-in-a-linked-list/. The interviewer explained 
me the question well with 2 examples, and asked me to code. I explained a simple 
approach, which will work if the maximum limit of nodes is known and then explained the 
fast and slow pointer method to detect a loop in the linked list. (Always start with the naïve 
approach and try to optimize it further). The interviewer told to just write the function 
code/pseudocode for the function which takes in the head pointer of linked list as 
argument and returns 1, when there is a loop found, else returns 0. I coded the function 
by explaining each line while I was typing. The interviewer seemed satisfied on my solution. 
He also asked about the time and space complexity of my solution. 
 
• What will be the output of the following cases? 
i. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a==11 && b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
Which part of the if-else statement will be executed and what are all the 
expressions that are evaluated (whether b==20 will be executed or not)? 
 
ii. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a=11 && b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
What will be output now and what happens here? 
 
iii. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a=11 & b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
What will be the final evaluation of condition given inside the if statement? 
All these questions were on the basics of C Programming and I answered these 
questions satisfactorily. 
• What will be printed if this program gets executed? 
void main() { 
 
int* b = f(); 
 
printf(“%d”,*b); 
} 
int* f() { 
 
int c = 10; 
 
return &c; 
    } 
For this question, I was not able to come up with the correct answer the first time. But 
the interviewer gave me hints to come up with the correct answer. After that, I was able 
to provide a correct answer, with which the interviewer seemed satisfied. 
With this the interview got over. Before leaving, he asked me if I had any questions to 
ask. I asked him two questions which I prepared beforehand. He answered my questions and 
took leave. The results were out by 10 pm the same night. And, finally 11 of us were selected 
for the summer internship programme. 
 
TOPICS TO FOCUS: 
• Data Structures and Algorithms 
• Operating Systems 
• Database Management Systems 
• Object Oriented Programming Concepts 
• Whatever you’ve mentioned in your resume 
 
RESOURCES THAT WERE USEFUL FOR PREPARATION: 
• “Must 
to 
do 
coding 
questions” 
available 
in 
GeeksforGeeks 
- 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/must-do-coding-questions-for-companies-like-
amazon-microsoft-adobe/ 
• Resources shared by ACM – Student Chapter of CEG through CODE Intern Preparation 
programme. 
 
KEY TAKEAWAYS: 
• Always be in touch with DSA. Keep solving coding questions on platforms like LeetCode, 
HackerRank, GeeksforGeeks and HackerEarth. Consistency in solving coding questions is 
an important factor. Do try to solve 2 to 3 problems everyday if possible. 
• Know to code from scratch as well. Some companies expect you to code from scratch in 
the online assessment. So, kindly make sure you are thorough with the header files and 
syntax of the programming language well. (If you are using STL in cpp, make sure you 
know the header files for each of the elements used in the program and the syntax for 
each of the functions used with the template). 
• Once you have been shortlisted for the interview round by any company, kindly go 
through the interview experiences of that particular company available online. 
“GeeksforGeeks Archives (or) Interview Experiences” helped me a lot to get a basic idea 
on how the interview will proceed. Not only for the interviews, this helps for the online 
assessment rounds as well. 
• Prepare for “Tell me about yourself” beforehand. Make sure it is short and crisp. 
• Read and re-read your resume. If questioned on your resume (projects as well), you 
should be able to answer it satisfactorily. So, be thorough with whatever you’ve 
mentioned in your resume. 
• Before attending any interview, do research on the company’s profile. This gives you a 
basic idea on how the company functions, it’s mission and goal as well. If the interviewer 
asks “What do you know about the company”, you should be able to answer it. 
• Be honest and don’t panic. Stay calm even if you don’t know an answer for a question. If 
struck at any point, don’t hesitate to say “I’m not sure about this, I will definitely look into 
it”.  The interviewer will help by giving hints as well. 
• Don’t lose hope during the interview, thinking that you have not done well so far. This 
may affect you throughout the rest of the interview and that is not what the interviewer 
expects from you. 
• During the code-pair round, if you are asked to code, explain the lines of code while you 
are coding. This gives the interviewer an idea that you are going with the flow of the 
approach and you have understood the approach well. Also, give sensible names for 
identifiers and functions during code-pair part of the interview. 
• Prepare well for “HR Questions” too. Along with the general HR questions, based on the 
soft skills mentioned on your resume, you might be expected to narrate an incident where 
you think you possessed the particular skill. So, be prepared with narrating such incidents 
as well. 
• Always stay motivated and optimistic throughout the period of preparation. Never get 
upset, if you don’t get through the process of any company. You will definitely get a better 
company than that.

Barclays 
Barclays is a British multinational banking and financial services company 
headquartered in London, with a rich history dating back to 1690. It operates 
globally, providing a wide range of banking, investment, and wealth 
management services to individuals, businesses, and institutions. With a 
significant presence in the United Kingdom and an international footprint, 
Barclays offers retail and corporate banking, investment banking, asset 
management, and credit card services. The bank has been involved in various 
philanthropic initiatives and is known for its commitment to sustainability and 
environmental practices. 
 
Hiring Role: HTD Technology Graduate Analyst 
The hiring process is divided into 2 Rounds. 
 Online Test 
 Technical + HR Round 
Online Test: 
Platform: HackerEarth 
Duration: 1hr 30mins 
It consists of 12 MCQs, 1 SQL Query, and 1 coding question. 
They were based on Java, C, and C++, and find the output of given pseudocode, 
DBMS, SQL, and OOPs. Having a strong knowledge of CS fundamentals and 
SQL will help you clear this round. Practice more technical aptitude and predict 
the output questions.  
The SQL query was straightforward. It was an easy one. It was just a simple 
question framed into a very big paragraph. 
The coding question:   
Lexicographically smallest string 
Reference: 
https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/4006812/string-generation-oa-
question/2045987 
 
Technical + HR Round: 
Duration: 45 minutes 
 
The interviewer greeted me and he started to read the “About me” section in my 
resume. He didn’t ask me for self-introduction. 
He was impressed by my CGPA and 12th marks and asked more about it. 
He asked about my favorite subject in school and I told Maths and Computer 
Science. He further asked what did I like in Maths. I said a variety of topics like 
matrices, linear algebra, calculus, and probability. In probability, he asked about 
the difference between permutation and combination. I explained it with an 
example and he was satisfied. 
Then he asked about my favorite subject in college. I told OOPS, Data 
Structures, Computer Networks, and Operating Systems. He then asked: 
 Why OOPS is your favorite subject? 
 Write C++ code for all 4 pillars of OOPS? 
I explained the code clearly with simple examples and he was ok with it. 
He asked about what sorting algorithms I know. 
I was always waiting for this question in an interview because I know around 9 
sorting algorithms which include: 
o Bubble sort 
o Insertion sort 
o Selection sort 
o Quick sort 
o Merge sort 
o Heap sort 
o Count sort 
o Radix sort 
o Shell sort 
I was not asked to write the code. I explained every algorithm along with its 
time and space complexity. I even explained their use cases and limitations. He 
was greatly impressed by this. It is not mandatory to learn all these algorithms 
but this was the turning point in my interview which majorly helped me. But I 
recommend you to know of all these because it makes you stand out from the 
rest of the others.Next, he asked me how strong you are in SQL. I said I am 
pretty strong in SQL. He handed me a pen and paper and asked about the ACID 
properties in DBMS. 
I explained each property in brief with an example. 
He looked at my projects and asked why I used Mongo DB since I had 
mentioned two full-stack projects in my resume which included it. 
I explained it with an example relating to my project and also told him why I 
preferred Mongo DB over MYSQL in my project. 
He further asked about the difference between SQL and NoSQL. 
He asked whether I knew about graph databases. I said I had not used it but I 
had heard about it since I came across it in my previous semester course "Big 
Data Analytics" 
The HR questions asked were: 
o What do you know about Barclays? 
o Why did you choose CEG? 
o Why did you not opt for IIT and NIT? 
o If not CEG, what are the other colleges you would have chosen? 
The interview ended with him saying “It was nice talking to you!” I was so 
happy to hear that. He asked me if I wanted to ask him any questions. Always 
ask questions to show your interest and prepare questions beforehand. I asked 
him about how they incorporate new technologies into their services. He 
answered my question and again said "It was nice talking to you “. He shook my 
hand and wished me all the best.  
 
Tips: 
 First and Foremost, you have to know the Barclays values- RISES. 
 Refer to the company website to learn more about RISES. 
 In behavioral interview questions, you have to answer in such a way that 
your answer aligns with at least one of the values. Because they evaluate 
you based on that. 
 Never lie in your resume, because in Barclays every interviewer has at 
least 10+ years of experience and they can easily catch you in a lie. 
 Be strong on what you mention in your resume. Questions will only be 
asked based on your resume. 
 When asked about your favorite subject don't tell him any subject that 
you are not so confident about. Because questions will be asked only 
based on that. 
 Read the job description beforehand. Questions will also come from that. 
If the job description includes cloud, microservices, GUI. Questions will 
also be asked from that. You don't need to know every concept they have 
mentioned in the job description, just basic knowledge is enough. 
 
Aptitude Preparation: 
 
Refer Indiabix. They have a ton of aptitude questions. Practice regularly. 
Important concepts: Problems on trains, Time and Distance, Time and Work, 
Simple and Compound Interest, Profit and Loss, Partnerships, Permutation and 
Combination, Probability, Ratios, and Percentages, Family members relations 
and ages. 
Tech MCQs- mostly Code outputs and Debugging. Predict the output for C, 
C++, and Java code snippets – based on pointer manipulation in C++, 
Inheritance-based questions, interfaces in java. 
 
DSA: 
 
Refer to Striver Sheet. It is more than enough. You should understand the 
intuition, thought process, and the time and space complexity behind every 
problem. Practice coding daily on websites like leetcode, hackerrank, coding 
ninjas and GeeksforGeeks. Participate in contests to manage your time in 
solving coding problems. Prepare standard questions like sorting algorithms, 4 
pillars of OOPS implementation, Queue to stack, and stack to queue. 
 
Core subjects: 
o DBMS 
o OOPS 
o OS 
o CN 
o System Design 
You need to be thorough with these concepts. Refer to websites like geeks for 
geeks, interview bit, PrepInsta, JavatPoint, and your semester notes. 
 
Project: 
This is the backbone of your resume. You need to know everything in and out 
of your project. Standard questions are: 
o Why did you choose this project? 
o What are the security features? 
o What are the challenges faced in the project? 
o Why did you choose this particular tech stack for this project? 
If you have hosted your project, do mention it because it makes a good 
impression on you. Update your GitHub and LinkedIn profile accordingly. 
Because the interviewer may also check your profile. You can also mention 
your coding profiles. At last mention some extracurricular activities in your 
resume because your resume should not be too technical. You should show him 
that you can balance both academic and extracurricular.   
 
 
HR and behavioral: 
 
Refer to many websites for Barclay’s behavioral questions and answers 
so you will get an idea of how to approach these questions. 
Important: RISES 
 
Most importantly, Refer to GFG archives before you appear for an 
interview 
 
Hope this helps. 
Thank you and All the Best.

VISA INTERNSHIP OFFER 
  ABOUT VISA, 
Visa Inc. is an American Multinational payments technology company which aims in providing 
fast, reliable and secure digital payments all around the world. It is often misinterpreted as a bank or 
credit card company. But actually, it provides financial services and facilitates digital payments. 
There were 2 rounds of selection process. One was an Online test and another was a Technical 
cum Managerial interview. 
1. Online test: 
It was conducted on hackerRank platform for 70 minutes. There were 4 coding questions of 2 Easy level 
difficulty and 2 medium level difficulty. 
• 
First Question: Given an array of n integers, write a function that returns the index of the first 
element in the array such that the next two consecutive elements (i.e., the element at the 
current index and the next two elements) are strictly greater than a given threshold. If no such 
index exists, return -1. 
• 
Second Question: You are given an array numbers. Distribute its elements between two arrays, 
first and second, as follows: 
1. The first element goes to first, the second to second. 
2. For each subsequent element, if the number of elements strictly greater than the 
current element is higher in one array, place the element in the other. If both have the 
same count, place the element in the shorter array. If still tied, place it in first. 
3. Return the merged result of first followed by second. 
• 
Third Question: You are given an n x m grid and an array figures representing different Tetris-
like shapes (A, B, C, D, E). Starting with a matrix of zeros, your task is to draw each figure on 
the grid using its 1-based index from the array. The shapes are placed on the grid according to 
specific patterns. Return the resulting matrix after placing all the shapes. 
• 
Fourth Question: Given an array of integers arr, write a function to count how many 
contiguous subarrays form a sawtooth sequence, where each element alternates in parity 
(even or odd) from its neighbors. 
Since there were only 4 questions, we were expected to solve both. A total of 15 students were 
shortlisted for the next round. 
2. Technical cum Managerial interview: 
It was conducted in MS teams. The duration of the interview was around 1 hour. Initially he asked me to 
introduce myself. 
There were some usual HR questions like: 
• 
Can you share a specific experience where you faced a significant challenge? How did you 
overcome it, and what important lesson did you take away from the situation? 
• 
What do you consider to be your weakness? 
• 
Tell me a situation where you have been so stressful in your life and how did you overcome it? 
• 
How would you handle a project with limited resources and a tight deadline? 
• 
Can you describe a situation where you received no initial instructions and explain how you 
managed to successfully navigate it? 
Finally, he asked me whether I had any questions for him. I asked him a couple of questions. 
• 
What does the work culture at Visa look like? 
• 
What are some common challenges interns might encounter during their internship? 
• 
What is the primary responsibility of an intern during the internship process? 
• 
I inquired about accommodation options during the internship. 
After the interview concluded, the results were announced the next day. Out of 15 
candidates, 5 were selected, and I’m one of them 
Key takeaways from interview experience: 
• 
Read & re-read your Resume. Be very clear about every single line put in your Resume. 
• 
Do an intensive research on the company profile and try to understand how do they make 
money out of their business. 
• 
Joining the interview 5 mins prior to the scheduled time will be very helpful. 
• 
Also, we can have a small conversation with the interviewer to get connected well. 
• 
Interviewers are not here just to reject us and they will definitely help us when we get struck 
somewhere. 
• 
Don’t lose your hope in-between the interview, if you answer wrong to 1 or 2 questions. 
 
Topics to be focused: 
• 
Data Structures and Algorithms. 
• 
Database Management Systems. 
• 
OOPS. 
• 
OS. 
• 
whatever skills and projects mentioned.

Interview Experience – NatWest 
Hi everyone, I am Badrinath S (2022503017) and I am glad to share my NatWest 
interview experience. 
 
Round 1 – Online Assessment (On Campus) 
The initial screening test was conducted on SHL. It tested: 
• 
Basic English grammar and comprehension 
• 
Logical reasoning (including visual reasoning) 
• 
SQL (MCQs) 
• 
2 coding questions 
The first coding question was very easy: 
Print even numbers first and odd numbers next. 
Here, there was a small trick. If you use i % 2 == 1 for odd numbers, it will fail for 
negative integers. 
The second question was a medium-level greedy problem. I always use Java in coding 
interviews since many companies prefer it (as most backends are in Spring Boot). For 
this question, I created another class called Pair with three variables so that managing 
comparisons after using Collections.sort became easier. 
My tip for this round: 
• 
Do not waste time reading the entire long paragraph. Many companies add 
lengthy text to consume your time. Instead, first read the input format and 
required output. That will give you a clear idea of what the problem is about. 
• 
Once you understand the requirement, try to map the problem to a suitable data 
structure. 
• 
Be strong in basics, such as how to take input using the Scanner class. 
• 
If your code does not work immediately, don’t panic. Use console print 
statements to debug and trace the error. 
• 
Above all, stay honest throughout your placement season. That’s the best way to 
prepare better and shine. 
• 
Prepare aptitude through mock test in SHL portal. 
I got shortlisted along with 23 others for the next round. 
 
Round 2 – Technical + HR (Combined, 45 minutes) 
Both interviewers were kind and introduced themselves at the beginning. They started 
by asking about the Pre-Placement Talk that happened just before the interviews. They 
also asked me if I knew about NatWest earlier. I replied that, as a cricket fanatic, I knew 
that NatWest had once sponsored the England Cricket Team. 
Next, they moved on to my resume. They asked about my projects and were impressed 
that they solved real-world problems. They said they liked my approach and mentioned 
that it aligned well with the company’s vision. At that point, I was confident that I had a 
good chance of receiving the offer. 
Then came the DSA round. They gave me a simple array question involving a HashSet. I 
explained my approach, and the interviewer suggested storing the index instead of the 
element. I explained that even then, the time complexity would remain O(N). The 
interviewer agreed and was satisfied. 
After that, they asked a SQL query to find the third highest mark. Initially, I wrote a 
nested query. The interviewer pointed out that its complexity could be reduced. I then 
suggested using ORDER BY DESC and picking the third record (I forgot the word 
“OFFSET,” but the interviewer mentioned it himself and said my approach was correct). 
They also asked a few basic ML questions—such as What is supervised learning? and 
What is an activation function?—based on my resume, which mentioned ML. 
Behavioral Round 
Next, they moved toward behavioral questions. 
They asked me about my view on learning new technologies. I replied that I am always 
open to learning anything necessary to solve a client’s problem. However, I also added 
that I am not the type of person who upskills without a clear need, as I prefer to focus 
my efforts where they bring value. 
They then asked me about my integrity. One question was: “What would you do if an 
ethically wrong person asked you to do something in the bank that is profitable for both 
the company and the bank?” 
I answered that I value honesty above all, as it is crucial for long-term success, even if 
shortcuts may give temporary relief. I also mentioned that if I had to reject an unethical 
request, I would do so firmly but with kindness, ensuring I handle the situation with 
respect. 
Another question was about AI. They asked me how I would use it. I responded that I 
would not allow AI to hamper my own thinking capabilities. Instead, I would use it to 
perform tasks where I already know the logical approach, thereby improving speed and 
execution. 
The interviewers were impressed with my responses. At the end, they asked if I had any 
questions. I politely said I didn’t have any. They told me they enjoyed the conversation, 
wished me all the best, and I left the room with a sense of gratitude. 
 
My Tips 
1. Always include ML in your resume if you have worked on it. Interviewers value 
that highly. 
2. Do not panic; stay calm and honest. 
3. Never over-exaggerate your projects. Interviewers are more impressed when you 
explain your projects truthfully and confidently. Explain your projects in the 
perspective of how they would solve a real life problem. 
4. Resume is the starting point: The flow of the interview was driven by my 
resume. Be ready to explain every project you include and link it to solving real-
world problems. 
5. Explain your thought process: In coding questions, the interviewer often looks 
at how you approach the problem rather than just the final solution. 
6. SQL efficiency matters: Knowing both a straightforward and an optimized 
approach shows maturity. 
7. Behavioral questions test alignment with values: Integrity, honesty, and 
adaptability were tested more than technical brilliance. Companies want people 
they can trust in sensitive domains like banking. 
8. AI and emerging tech: Instead of exaggerating, give a balanced view. Show 
openness to use new technologies while maintaining human reasoning. 
9. Confidence > perfection: Even when I forgot the keyword OFFSET, I handled it 
calmly. That mattered more than knowing every detail. 
 
 
Finally I got the offer along with 8  other canditates.Felt very relieved and was at peace 
after so much grind.

SAP Labs Interview Experience                       
Role : Developer Associate                                                                       
Total Rounds: 5  
Date: 18th August 2025 
 
 
It all began on a nervous evening in front of my laptop, preparing for the 
SAP Labs interviews.  
I had read countless experiences from other candidates, and now it was 
finally my turn to create one of my own.  
From the very first online test to the final HR discussion, here’s a step-
by-step look at how my SAP Labs interview unfolded and the lessons I 
took away. 
 
 
About Company: 
About SAP Labs India 
SAP Labs India is a major research and development hub of SAP, 
located in Bengaluru, Gurgaon, Pune, Mumbai, and Hyderabad. The 
Bengaluru campus is the largest, focusing on AI solutions and 
enterprise technology with a strong, customer-centric approach. 
About SAP 
SAP (Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is a 
global leader in enterprise software, helping businesses manage 
operations like finance, supply chain, and data analytics. Headquartered 
in Germany, SAP has been driving innovation for over 50 years and 
works with companies in nearly every industry worldwide. 
 
[Learn the basic concepts of SAP ERP products and the SAP HANA 
database they use. They usually don’t ask direct questions from these 
topics, but having this knowledge can give you a slight edge over other 
candidates by showing genuine interest in the company.]  
 
 
Selection Process 
1.Online Assessment (OA) 
 Duration: 100 minutes (1 hour 40 minutes) 
 Platform: Codility 
 Proctoring: Both video and audio 
 Questions: 2 DSA problems – one easy and one medium level. 
 Note: Each candidate received a different set of questions. 
 
Questions Asked to me  
Question -1 
Problem Statement 
You are given an array A containing N integers, sorted in non-
decreasing order. 
Your goal is to transform this array by performing a series of moves. A 
"move" can be one of two operations: 
1. Remove an integer from the array. 
2. Insert an integer anywhere in the array. 
The objective is to reach a state where for every unique value X present 
in the final array, it appears exactly X times. 
You need to find the minimum total number of moves (removals + 
insertions) required to achieve this goal. Note that it's permissible to 
remove all occurrences of a number. 
 
 
Example 1 
 Input: A = [1, 1, 3, 4, 4, 4] 
  Expected Output: 3 
Explanation: The initial array has two 1s, one 3, and three 4s. You can 
perform the following 3 moves: 
1. Remove one 1. 
2. Remove the 3. 
3. Insert one 4. The resulting array is [1, 4, 4, 4, 4]. In this array, the 
value 1 appears once, and the value 4 appears four times, 
satisfying the condition. 
 
Example 2 
 Input: A = [1, 2, 2, 2 ,5, 5, 5, 8] 
 Expected Output: 4 
Explanation:  
1.Remove 8 
     2.Remove 2 once 
3.Add two times 5  
Total moves : 4 
 
Example 3 
 Input: A = []  
 Expected Output: 0 
 
 
 
 
Question 2 
Problem Statement 
In a city, there are N districts (numbered from 0 to N-1) connected by M 
streets. The street network is described by two arrays, A and B, where 
each pair (A[K], B[K]) represents a street between two districts. 
There are also L hospitals in the city, and their locations are given by an 
array H. 
When an ambulance is needed in a district, it is sent from the hospital 
that can reach the district via the shortest possible route. It takes 
exactly 1 minute to travel along any single street. 
The task is to find the longest time required for an ambulance to reach 
any district in the city. This means you must: 
1. For every district, find the shortest possible travel time from its 
closest hospital. 
2. From all these shortest times, find the maximum value. 
If any district cannot be reached by an ambulance (i.e., it's not 
connected to any district with a hospital), the function should return -1.
 
I don’t remember the example for this question 
Similalar question : https://www.chegg.com/homework-help/questions-
and-answers/city-n-districts-numbered-0-n-1-connected-m-streets-
connections-described-two-arrays-b-len-q92788342 
 
I solved both questions  
Result of this Round : 
A total of 89 students were shortlisted for the interview. 
The results came out just one day before, in the afternoon, so I had only 
that evening and the next morning to prepare. 
 
My advice: if you’ve solved both problems, don’t wait for the results—
start preparing for the interview right away. 
If you get shortlisted, you’re ready. If not, that preparation will still help 
for the next company. 
 
This way, even if the shortlist is announced at the last minute, you’ll 
already feel confident and ready. 
 
 
Technical Interview – 1  
 
Duration: For me, this round lasted about 1 to 1 hour 15 minutes. 
1. Introduction & Project Discussion 
The interview started with the classic question: “Introduce yourself.” 
Next, the interviewer asked me to explain my second project listed on 
my resume. 
I began explaining my project, and he seemed very satisfied with my 
explanation. 
Later, he moved on to the technical part and asked, “Are you good in 
Java?” 
I confidently replied, “Yes, I’m good at it.” 
 
Questions asked related to java  
 
1.Exception Handling 
2.Whether try block cant exist with/without catch and finally 
3.checked exception and unchecked exception with examples for them 
4.Throw vs throws  
5.finally keyword 
6.Oops concept in java  
My Advice: 
Don’t just memorize OOP theory from sites like GFG. Instead, focus on 
understanding the concepts clearly and, if possible, relate them to your 
project. Explaining OOP concepts with examples from your own project 
makes it easier for the interviewer to understand and shows practical 
knowledge. 
 
 7.Polymorphism one example code question 
class Calculator { 
    int sum(int a, int b) { 
        return a + b; 
    } 
    long sum(long a, int b) {  
        return a + b; 
    } 
} 
Calculator.sum(10,5) 
Which method will be called  
I said sum(int a ,int b) 
Why long sum() isn’t called he asked  
Then what happens if only we have long sum() 
I said if we have only long sum() then long sum will be called  
He asked me to explain the reason 
Java looks for exact type matches first. 
If no exact match, it considers widening conversions (int → long, float 
→ double, etc.). 
8.Wrapper class 
 
we moved on to the DSA part. I was given four standard problems: 
1. Binary Search 
2. Set Matrix Zeros 
3. Find all permutations of an integer array 
4. Merge Sort 
I was asked to write code for all four problems and also explain each 
solution with a dry run. 
I managed to solve all the problems, but I struggled a bit with the dry 
run of the recursion in the permutation problem, even though it’s a 
standard question from Striver. 
The good part was that the interviewer helped me a lot throughout the 
session, guiding me when needed, which made the experience much 
smoother. 
Then he asked me whether you have any questions 
At the end of the interview, the classic question comes up: “Do you have 
any questions for me?” 
Instead of leaving it blank, use this moment to demonstrate curiosity 
and genuine interest.  
Ask about the team, the role, growth opportunities, or the projects you 
might work on. Thoughtful questions at this stage leave a strong, 
positive impression on the interviewer. 
 
My Advice: 
Avoid asking the usual, generic questions that everyone asks. Instead, 
try to ask a unique and thoughtful question—one that stands out and 
makes the interviewer remember you. 
I asked about the Agentic AI and Joule AI that SAP is currently using in 
development.  
 
 
 
NOTE: 
My interviewer didn’t introduce himself at the start, so at the end I 
asked, “Sir, could you please tell me about yourself and your role?” 
Always remember who interviewed you and what their role is—this 
information can be very helpful for future rounds. 
[My First Round little bit lengthier when compared to others] 
 
After this round, I got shortlisted for the next stage. From all the 
participants, only about 25 students moved forward to

Then, the discussion moved to DBMS concepts, where he asked about

BANK OF NEW YORK MELLON 
FTE INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
ABOUT THE COMPANY: 
 
The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, commonly known as BNY Mellon, is an American 
multinational investment banking services holding company headquartered in New York City. It is by 
far the world's largest custodian bank and asset servicing company. It provides investment 
management, investment services and wealth management helping institutions and individuals 
succeed in markets all over the world. BNY Mellon Technology Private Limited, established in 2000, is a 
group company of BNY Mellon operating in India, providing technology solutions to the bank. The 
Technology division has over 13,000 professionals, working across nine innovation centres worldwide, 
to deliver innovative technology solutions powering BNY Mellon business. 
 
      You can know more about the company at this website:       https://www.bnymellon.com/in/en.html 
 
 
Number of Rounds: 4 
 
1. Online Coding Assessment (90 mins) 
2. Technical Interview I (60 mins) 
3. Technical Interview II (45 mins) 
4. HR Interview (60 mins) 
 
Round 1: Online Coding Assessment: (90 mins) 
The online coding round consisted of 4 programming problems to be solved in 1 hour 30 mins. 
The test was conducted on Hackerrank platform. The problems were of different difficulty levels(1 easy, 
2 medium and 1 hard). The questions were as follows: 
Easy problem: (Distinct digit numbers) 
Print the numbers with unique digits in a given range (x, y) x and y inclusive. Time 
complexity was a factor to note while solving this. I solved it partially (8/12 testcases passed). I 
wasn’t able to optimize my solution and that’s why the 4 testcases got time limit exceeded. 
 
Medium problem 1: (Slot machine 2.0) 
 
I solved this completely. 
 
Medium problem 2: 
Given 3 coordinates (a, b, c) in a plane, check whether they form a triangle (It’s a 
triangle if the following conditions hold...|ab + bc| > |ac|, |ac + ab| > |bc|, |bc + ac| > |ab|). 
Check whether the points P and Q lie on the triangle formed. Return a number as per the 
below mentioned conditions: 
0 - If the points form a triangle and P, Q doesn't lie on the triangle 
1 - If only P lies on the triangle 
2 - If only Q lies on the triangle 
4 - If both P and Q lies on the triangle 
5 - If the triangle cannot be formed by (a,b,c) 
 
 
I solved this completely. 
 
 
Hard problem: (Bit profit) 
 
I was not able to solve this but I identified an edge case and returned the 
corresponding value for the same (2/15 testcases passed). 
 
Candidates who solved at least 2.5 questions were shortlisted for the next round. (22 were shortlisted 
for the next round) 
All the interview rounds were Face to Face and held at CUIC. 
  
Round 2: Technical Interview I (60 mins) 
 
The interview started with my self-introduction. And then the interviewer tested the 
knowledge of my fundamental data structures and OOPs. Concepts from arrays, linked lists, stack, 
queues and strings were asked. Then, the interviewer asked me to solve an easy level coding question 
both iteratively and recursively. Then, the interviewer asked fundamental questions from DBMS. 
Questions like, how APIs work and how Google search works were asked. The interview ended with 
questions based upon projects listed in my resume. 
 
Round 3: Technical Interview II (45 mins) 
The interview started with my self-introduction as before. The interviewer tested the 
knowledge of my fundamental data structure and OOPs. Then, the interviewer asked fundamental 
concepts from DBMS and some queries for a given table. He gave me two tables and asked me to 
write queries for the scenarios given. He asked me to explain my projects which I had put in my 
resume and also asked me some questions about the projects. He asked to explain how I normalised 
the tables of the database at each step in my project with diagrams. Couple of easy coding questions 
were asked and I solved both.  
 
 
Round 4: HR Interview (60 mins) 
 
The interview started with questions based upon my skills listed in the resume. He asked what 
skills I acquired as part of the curriculum and what I learnt at my own interest. He also asked how did I 
learnt the skills which I listed as learnt at my own interest. Following this, typical HR questions were 
asked. Some of them are: 
 What challenges you faced in past 6 months and how did you overcome them? 
 Did any conflict/misunderstanding arised between you and your teammate while doing the 
project and how it was resolved? 
 Are you praised by your surrounding people for your qualities? If so, what are those? 
 What did you learn apart from your college curriculum and what made you to learn it? 
 What do you do in your free time? 
 What do you know about BNY and why BNY? 
 
A behavioural question was asked after this. A hypothetical situation was given and he tested how 
would I react for the same. The situation is described as, I am a fresher who is part of a team comprising 
of 10-12 members. The team is so unresponsive that I wasn’t able to clear my doubts. None of them 
listens to my grievances and if some listened, no proper response was coming from them. 
After this a puzzle was asked to test my lateral thinking. The link to the puzzle is: 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/puzzle-18-torch-and-bridge/ 
 
 
12 people were given the FTE offer. 
 
 
 
General Tips: 
 Practice regularly on any of the coding platforms. 
 Try to complete Must Do Coding questions from GFG. 
 Familiarise with data structures and algorithms. 
 Concentrate on DBMS and OS too. 
 Have 2-3 solid projects. Self-Interest projects might be more helpful than subject related 
projects. 
 
Be ready to answer any type of questions based on your resume. 
 
Explain your thoughts while solving problems in technical interviews. 
 Try to give real life incidents as an example to the questions asked in HR interviews. 
 
Do not over-familiarize yourself with standard problems. 
 
 
 
This is Barathraj T from CSE, CEG (Batch 2023). If you have any queries, you can ping 
me at https://www.linkedin.com/in/barathraj-t.

CITI SUMMER INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Company & Role Overview 
• 
Company: Citi (Citigroup) is a leading global financial services company 
headquartered in New York. Founded in 1812, it offers a wide range of services 
including banking, investment banking, and wealth management to individuals, 
corporations, and governments in over 100 countries. 
• 
Role Offered: International Clients Group (ICG) Technology 
• 
Other Role: Personal Banking & Wealth Management Technology (PBWMT) 
• 
Stipend: ₹75,000 per month 
• 
Internship Duration: 8 weeks 
Selection Process 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview 
3. HR Interview 
Round 1: Online Assessment (September 27, 2024) 
The online test was 90 minutes long and consisted of four sections. 
Important Note: It was not possible to switch between sections; they had to be completed in 
the order they were presented. 
Test Structure: 
• 
Coding: 2 questions (45 minutes) 
• 
SQL MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
• 
React MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
• 
Angular MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
Section Details: 
• 
Coding Questions - The questions were considered easy and were based on arrays. 
• 
SQL Questions 
o The difficulty level was easy to medium. 
• 
React & Angular JS Questions 
o These multiple-choice sections included questions about finding missing terms 
and identifying correct code statements. 
Following the online assessment, 28 candidates were shortlisted for the next round of 
interviews for the ICG Technology role. 
Round 2: Technical Interview (Duration: 1 hour) 
The interview began with my introduction. I structured it to highlight my favourite subjects 
and fields of interest, specifically mentioning my proficiency in Database Management 
Systems (DBMS). This led the interviewer to start with questions about my DBMS project. 
Project 1: DBMS & Backend 
• 
Project Explanation: I spent about 10 minutes clearly explaining my project. Since I 
mentioned handling the backend and database, the interviewer asked for more details. 
I gave him an overview of the backend operations, including how my PHP scripts 
worked and interacted with the database. 
• 
Database Schema: He then asked me to explain my database schema. I detailed the 
structure of each table and explained the reasoning behind my normalization choices. 
This was followed by a question about the foreign key relationships I had 
implemented. 
• 
On-the-Spot Database Design: The interviewer then proposed a new project idea 
and asked me to design a database for it. 
o I started by asking clarifying questions to understand the project's 
requirements and finalize the necessary tables. 
o I designed each table with relevant columns and constraints, periodically 
checking in with the interviewer to ensure my understanding was correct. 
o After structuring the tables, he asked me to normalize the design. I explained 
my normalization strategy and justified my decisions. 
o Finally, he asked me to add foreign key relationships where needed, and I 
identified and included one. 
• 
SQL Queries: To conclude this section, he asked me to write several SQL queries for 
the database we had just designed. The queries primarily involved the use of JOINs 
and subqueries. 
Frontend & Other Technologies 
• 
React/Angular: The interviewer asked if I knew React or Angular. I was honest and 
stated that I was not very familiar with them. 
• 
HTML/CSS Task: He then roughly sketched a webpage design and asked me to 
write the basic HTML and CSS for it, which I did. 
Project 2: Python (DSA) 
• 
The discussion moved to my second project, which was written in Python and 
involved data structures and algorithms. 
• 
Shortest Path Algorithm: The project used a shortest-path algorithm. He asked 
which one I implemented, and I replied that I used Dijkstra's algorithm. 
• 
He followed up by asking why I chose Dijkstra's over other algorithms. I explained 
my reasoning and then named other shortest-path algorithms I knew, such as 
Bellman-Ford, explaining each one briefly. 
General CS Fundamentals 
• 
Java: He asked about my experience with Java. I explained that I was currently 
learning it as part of my semester coursework but was not yet proficient. 
• 
Sorting Algorithms: He then asked if I knew sorting algorithms. I said yes, and he 
specifically asked me to explain Radix Sort. I told him I was more familiar with 
Merge Sort and Quick Sort. He then asked me to explain Quick Sort, which I did with 
a diagram and an example. 
• 
Data Structures: The interviewer presented a scenario and asked which data 
structure I would use to solve it. I suggested a Queue, explained my reasoning, and 
compared it with other data structures to justify why it was the most suitable choice. 
• 
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): Finally, he asked if I knew OOP concepts. I 
had prepared a concise, 10-minute explanation with a strong example, which I 
delivered clearly. He was impressed and appreciated how well I explained it. 
Conclusion & Feedback 
At the end of the interview, he asked if I had any questions for him. I had prepared two in 
advance: 
1. "How did I perform in the interview?" 
o He responded, "You are strong in what you know, and you know your 
strengths." 
2. "What areas should I improve on?" 
o He answered, "There is nothing you should improve; you are good. However, 
it would be helpful for your career in the industry to get to know more 
technologies like React and Angular." 
Round 3: HR Interview 
This was the final stage. Although it felt like a casual conversation, it was also an elimination 
round. The interview lasted for about 15-20 minutes. 
To my surprise, I was called in 9th out of the 11 candidates, even though I was second on the 
shortlist. When I entered, the HR manager mentioned he did this intentionally to see if 
waiting would make me nervous. 
Interview Discussion & Questions 
The conversation covered a mix of standard HR questions and personal topics: 
• 
Knowledge about Citi: He asked what I knew about the company. I shared 
information I had gathered from a senior who currently works there. He then asked if 
I had attended the Pre-Placement Talk (PPT). I answered honestly that while I had 
joined the session, I was traveling and couldn't pay full attention. 
• 
Motivation: He asked the classic question, "Why do you want to work at Citi?" 
• 
Future Aspirations: He asked, "Where do you see yourself in the future?" 
• 
Personal Interests: My resume mentioned an award in football. As a fellow football 
enthusiast, the HR manager started a friendly discussion about the sport, which helped 
build a good rapport. 
Conclusion & Feedback 
As the interview concluded, the HR manager gave me two key pieces of feedback: 
1. He advised that I should research a company more thoroughly before an interview. 
2. He shared that my technical interviewer had given "great feedback" on my 
performance. I asked him to pass on my thanks. 
I left the interview a little worried that my answer about the company wasn't strong enough. 
However, the results were announced an hour later. 
Final Result 
Out of the 11 candidates who made it to the final round, 7 were selected for the internship, 
including me.  
General Tips 
1. Be strong in DSA, DBMS, OOP, and OS. 
2. Create a clear resume and know every detail on it. 
3. Prepare explanations for your projects and a short pitch for each skill. 
4. Be honest and show them you are a capable candidate. 
 
All the very best!!

APPIAN INTERN INTERVIEW EXPERINCE 
 
Appian (pronounced App-ian) Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise 
software company headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology Corridor. 
The company sells a platform as a service (PaaS) for building enterprise software applications. 
It is focused on lowcode development, process mining, business process management, and 
case management markets in North America, Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia. 
Appian offers a lowcode platform with a visual interface and pre-built development modules. As 
with other low-code platforms, it enables businesses to create apps and automations using little 
or no code. 
ONLINE ASSESSMENT: 
The online assessment was held in glider.ai virtually and we had to solve 3 questions in 1 hr. 
Question 1: 
You will be given a set of paths where each path consists of a set of strings representing a path 
to a particular page starting from the ‘Main’ (homepage) element. You need to construct a tree 
representing every path given as input. The root node will be the ‘Main’ element. Finally print it 
out as per the required format. (Breadcrumbs problem) 
Sample input: 
 arr = [[‘Main’, ‘Dashboard’, ‘Settings’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Uploads’], [‘Main’, ‘Dashboard’, 
‘Products’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Remove], [‘Main’, ‘Manager, ‘Work’], [‘Main’, ‘Manager, ‘Review’]] 
Expected Output: 
Main 
  -Dashboard 
    -Settings 
    -Products 
  -Profile 
    -Uploads 
    -Remove 
  -Manager 
    -Work 
    -Review 
Question 2: 
Tickets are sold to a concert in a particular order. The number of tickets, the step size, and the 
offset will be given. Number of Tickets: This is the total count of tickets available for the concert. 
Step Size: This is the number of tickets to skip before selecting the next ticket. For example, if 
the step size is 3, every fourth ticket will be chosen. (It can also be a negative number.) Offset: 
This is the starting position from which the ticket selection begins. It can be thought of as the 
initial position in the sequence of tickets where the process starts. The challenge is to generate 
a sequence of ticket numbers based on these parameters, reflecting the order in which tickets 
are sold.  
It is a variant of the famous Josephus problem but with negative step. 
Question 3: 
You are provided with a list of group names and a list of people belonging to each group. 
Additionally, you are given an array of strings representing individuals standing in a line. The 
task is to reorder the array such that:  
1. If a person from a group is in the line, all other subsequent members of the same 
group should move to stand next to each other in the order they appear. 
 2. Once all members of a group are adjacent, replace their names with the group name. 
 
I was able to solve all 3 questions within 1hr. 22 people were selected for interview. 
INTERVIEW: 
Technical Round 1:  
This was a pair programming kind of round that happened for all students from CEG, but it was 
skipped for some students from MIT including me. 
 
Technical Round 2:  
This was a mix of technical and behavioral. As I had mentioned aspiring web developer in my 
resume, I was asked about what happens when an URL is typed in the browser. Then to name 
some data structures and their real-life examples completely away from computers. Then I was 
asked what my favorite subject was in general and in computer science and why. I explained 
why math was my favorite and why theory of computation was my favorite. Then, my favorite 
programming language and why. I said Scala and I was asked to list out its characteristics and 
about functional programming. Then, I was presented with a list of problems  
●​ identify the odd ball among eight balls, where seven balls were of the same weight, and 
one was either heavier or lighter. The challenge was to find the odd ball using a balance 
scale with just two weighings.  
●​ Pseudocode to distribute a total value according to weights in an array 
●​ Bubble sort and its complexity 
●​ Paranthesis matching 
Following that I was asked about OOPS concepts and if I had used them in any of my projects. 
And then I was asked to explain what was the favorite project I did and why I chose Appian. 
Finally, I was given an opportunity to ask questions in general or about Appian. 
Behavioral Round:  
This round was with two interviewers. I was asked some basic questions regarding my 
academics and personal background. Here too I was asked about favorite subject, programming 
language and why. Here it was more in detail with multiple follow-ups to my responses. I was 
then asked to compare Python and Scala. Then, I was asked what was my favorite project 
(Turing Machine simulator) out of those I put in my resume and the motivation behind it. Then, I 
was asked why I chose Appian. They remarked that I seemed more research-oriented and why I 
wanted to get into software development. Finally, I was given an opportunity to ask questions. 
 
In the end, 5 of us (4 of us from CEG and 1 from MIT) got selected for the position of Software 
Engineer Intern. 
 Tips: 
●​  Enhance your problem solving skills so that when faced with any problem in the wild, 
you can solve it without asking what species it is. 
●​ Be sincere and honest. Don’t express interest in a topic either when asked or in the 
resume because it would sound good. Mention what you really like so that you can 
speak more confidently. 
●​ Stay true to your interests. If your projects are aligned with your interests, it would give 
the impression of genuine interest. 
●​ In subjective questions like project related or favorite subjects, there is no right or wrong. 
Be confident. 
●​ Listen to the pre-placement talk carefully and try to learn as much as possible about the 
company. This can help you answer why you chose the company.  
●​ Prepare questions for the interviewers if possible. 
 
All the very best,
