A total of 89 students were shortlisted for the interview. The results were announced just one day 
before, in the afternoon, which meant I had only from that afternoon until the next morning to 
prepare. From this experience, my key takeaway is that you should start preparing for interviews 
alongside your coding round preparation. This way, even if the results are announced at the last 
moment, you will already be confident and well-prepared for the interview. 
 
 
 
 
 
Interview: 
 
There were 4 Rounds of interview. It was conducted in person at CUIC. 
1. Technical Round 1 
2. Technical Round 2 
3. Techno-managerial Round 
4. HR Round 
 
Round 1 – Technical Round 1: 
Duration: 50 mins 
Panel: 1 interviewer  
1. Introduction & Project Discussion 
• 
The interviewer started with: “Introduce yourself.” 
• 
Next, he asked me to explain one of the projects I am most familiar with. 
• 
Since I mentioned being deeply interested in Web Development, he asked: 
o 
What is Web Development? 
o 
What are its components? (Front-end, Back-end, Database, etc.) 
2. Database & Web Development Question 
Scenario: 
I was asked to design a form consisting of Name, Skills, and Submit button. 
This form is used by CUIC: when a company arrives for placement, we need to return students whose 
skills match the company’s requirements. 
My Approach: 
• 
I explained that Skills is a multivalued attribute, so it needs to be stored in a separate table. 
• 
For unique identification, I added Roll No to the form. 
• 
I designed the database schema and wrote queries to retrieve the students with matching 
skills. 
3. DSA Question – Graphs 
Problem Statement: 
There are N number of files with dependencies given as A -> B (file A depends on file B). 
From the given dependencies, find the order of selecting/executing the files. 
My Solution: 
• 
I identified this as a Topological Sort problem. 
• 
Explained the approach step by step (BFS/DFS method). 
• 
Wrote the code clearly while explaining my thought process. 
 
• 
Important Tip: Even if you know the solution, take time to think, explain clearly, and structure 
your answer logically. 
The interviewer was impressed with my explanation and coding. 
4. Project Discussion (Again) 
• 
We had a short discussion about my projects again. 
• 
He appreciated the way I explained them.  
• 
My project explanation created a positive impression and led to further discussion. 
5. Final Interaction – Candidate’s Questions 
• 
At the end, the interviewer asked: “Do you have any questions for me?” 
• 
This is the best chance to show your interest and willingness. Don’t skip it. Ask meaningful 
questions (about role, team culture, learning opportunities, etc.). It creates a positive 
impression. 
Overall, this round tested my ability to explain projects, apply database knowledge, solve DSA 
problems, and communicate effectively. Clear explanation and confidence played a key role. 
After this round, I was shortlisted for the next stage. Out of all the participants, around 25 students 
were shortlisted for Technical Round 2. 
 
Round 2 – Technical Round 2: 
Duration: 1 hour 15 mins  
Panel: 2 interviewers  
The round began with a short introduction from both sides. I was asked to briefly explain one of my 
projects, after which the interview quickly moved into DSA-focused questions. 
Question 1: Searching order with Ranking 
Problem Statement: 
• 
A set of words is given, each with a rank. 
• 
When a user types in the search bar, all words matching the prefix of the search key should 
be displayed. 
• 
The results should be ordered by rank (descending). 
Example: 
Words with rank: 
app (10), application (8), apple (9), apply (5), banana (15) 
 
Search Key = "app" 
Result = app (10) → apple (9) → application (8) → apply (5) 
 
(Note: “banana” is ignored since it does not match the prefix "app") 
My Thought Process & Approach: 
• 
Since the problem deals with prefix matching, the first idea that came to my mind was to use 
a Trie data structure. 
o 
Each word can be inserted into the Trie. 
o 
Searching for the prefix is efficient (O(length of prefix)). 
• 
After finding the prefix node in the Trie, we need to return all words under that prefix sorted 
by rank. 
Clarification I Asked: 
• 
I asked the interviewer: “If there is a word like banana(15), should it appear when the search 
key = app?” 
• 
The interviewer said No, since only prefix matches are valid. 
• 
That confirmed my Trie-based approach was correct. 
Optimization Discussion: 
• 
My initial idea: Insert all words in the Trie → When searching, collect words under the prefix 
→ Sort them by rank before displaying. 
• 
Interviewer expectation: Further optimize. 
o 
Sorting every time can be costly if there are many words. 
o 
Possible optimization: At each Trie node, maintain a Heap (Max-Heap or Min-Heap) 
or a pre-sorted list of top words for quick retrieval. 
• 
We discussed how using a Heap along with Trie could give better performance for large 
datasets. 
 
 
Question 2: Infection Spread in Cities (Grid + BFS) 
Problem Statement: 
• 
A grid is given where each cell represents a city. 
• 
Each cell has two possible states: 
o 
1 → City is already infected 
o 
0 → City is not infected 
• 
Infected cities spread the infection to their adjacent (up, down, left, right) cities in 1 day. 
• 
The task is to determine the day on which all the cities become infected. 
 
 
My Approach & Explanation: 
• 
I quickly recognized this as a variation of the  
Rotten Oranges problem(https://leetcode.com/problems/rotting-oranges/) 
• 
The natural approach here is to use Breadth-First Search (BFS): 
o 
Treat all initially infected cities (1) as sources and push them into a queue with day = 
0. 
o 
Perform BFS to spread infection level by level (day by day). 
o 
Track the maximum day reached when the last city becomes infected. 
Code Writing: 
• 
The interviewer asked me to write the code. 
• 
I implemented the BFS solution step by step: 
1. Traverse grid → push all 1 cells into queue with day = 0. 
2. BFS → spread to adjacent cells and mark them infected. 
3. Keep track of days. 
4. Return the maximum day. 
Edge Cases (Discussed with Interviewer): 
1. No city is infected initially → Answer should be 0 (no spread possible). 
2. All cities are already infected → Answer should be 0 (no extra days needed). 
3. Some cities can never be infected (isolated regions with no adjacent infected cities) → 
Should return -1 or handle accordingly. 
 
 
Question 3: Designing a Stack with Min/Max Operations 
Problem Statement: 
• 
Design a Stack that supports the following operations: 
o 
push(x) → Add element at the top of the stack. 
o 
pop() → Remove the element at the top of the stack. 
o 
getMin() → Return the minimum element present in the stack. 
o 
getMax() → Return the maximum element present in the stack. 
• 
The stack should efficiently handle MinElement and MaxElement queries. 
 
 
 
My Approach & Explanation: 
1. Brute Force Approach (Initial Idea): 
o 
Use the standard stack container in C++ for push and pop. 
o 
For getMin() and getMax(), traverse the entire stack to find min/max. 
o 
Simple but inefficient → O(n) time for min/max queries. 
2. Optimized Approach (Inspired by MinStack Problem): 
o 
Store each element as a pair (a, b), where: 
▪ 
a = element itself 
▪ 
b = minimum element in the stack till now 
o 
Similarly, maintain another stack (or extend pair) to track maximum element till 
now. 
o 
This way, push, pop, getMin(), and getMax() all work in O(1) time. 
Discussion with Interviewer: 
• 
I explained both brute force and optimized approaches. 
• 
We discussed pros and cons: 
o 
Brute force → Easy to implement but slow for large inputs. 
o 
Optimized (with pairs) → Extra space usage but very efficient. 
• 
The interviewer appreciated that I explained the trade-offs and thought about scalability. 
 
Database & DBMS Discussion 
1. Initial Questions: 
• 
The interviewer asked: “Are you strong in DBMS? Have you ever worked on projects involving 
DBMS?” 
• 
Since I had mentioned MySQL and MongoDB in my resume, I answered confidently “Yes”. 
2. SQL Queries:

I was asked to write some basic SQL queries (CRUD operations, simple joins, filtering, etc.). 
• 
Then they asked me about Triggers and Stored Procedures: 
o 
What is a Procedure? 
o 
Why do we need it? 
• 
I explained the concept: 
o 
Procedures are reusable blocks of SQL code stored in the database. 
o 
They help in modularity, reusability, and better performance. 
 
3. Project-Based Question (Concurrency Handling): 
• 
From my project experience, they asked: 
“How will you handle concurrent access to the database?” 
• 
I explained using locking mechanisms in DBMS: 
o 
Locks ensure that when one transaction is accessing/updating a data item, others 
wait until it is released. 
4. Follow-up Question (Lock Failure Case): 
• 
The interviewer went deeper: 
“If someone acquires a lock for a particular data item (say X), but during execution the

• 
I initially explained using rollback functions, but I was a bit confused here. 
• 
I tried my best to answer, but I wasn’t fully confident. 
 
API Discussion

“What is an API?” 
• 
“How will you write an API in Java?”

“Write some example APIs in Java.”

I explained the concept of API (Application Programming Interface) as a way for two systems

The interviewers were satisfied with my explanation. 
Interviewer’s Feedback & Soft Skills

Both interviewers appreciated the way I explained my answers and highlighted my confidence

Key Point: Confidence plays a huge role. Even if you don’t know the exact answer:

Take a moment, think logically, and explain what you know.

If still stuck, politely ask the interviewer for hints — they are often ready to guide you. 
 
Final Interaction

At the end, they asked me: “Do you have any questions for us?”

As I mentioned earlier, this is a very important opportunity. I spent nearly 15 minutes asking

The interviewers appreciated my curiosity and concluded with: “It was a very wonderful 
interview with you, Gurumoorthi.”

Round 3 – Techno-managerial Round: 
Duration: 1 hour 20 mins  
Panel: 2 interviewers (Managers) 
This was one of the most important rounds in the whole selection process, as it was completely 
focused on real-world application-oriented questions and tested my practical knowledge. 
As usual, the round started with my introduction, followed by the interviewers introducing

For CRUD operations, what API methods are used.

How to handle dynamic API routes.

What is a Socket and its uses. 
• 
Questions about new technologies I am currently learning. 
• 
My understanding of Docker and Kubernetes. 
This round was quite challenging as it connected theoretical knowledge with practical scenarios. I

The interviewers were very interactive throughout, and they often asked follow-up questions to test 
my depth of knowledge. I stayed confident and took a moment to think before answering whenever I

After the technical and conceptual questions, they asked me to explain any one of my projects and

3. Sand Clock Puzzle – Two sand clocks of 11 and 7 minutes are given. We need to measure 15

I am very happy to share this whole experience. 
 
 
Tips & Notes: 
For Coding Round: 
• 
Confidence is key → Don’t panic if you get stuck, break the problem into smaller parts. 
• 
Attempt all questions → Even partial code can fetch marks, so don’t leave questions blank. 
• 
Start with Brute Force → First write a correct solution, then optimize step by step. 
• 
Think about Edge Cases → Empty input, single element, large constraints, negative values, etc. 
• 
Practice previous year questions → Get an idea of the difficulty level and common patterns. 
 
• 
Time Management → Don’t spend too long on one problem, move to the next and come back 
later. 
• 
Dry run your code → Before submitting, test with small cases manually. 
• 
Stay calm during OA → Focus more on accuracy first, then optimization. 
 
 
 
For Interviews: 
• 
Be confident – Confidence is the most important quality throughout the interview process. 
• 
Revise your resume – Before every round, go through what you have mentioned in your 
resume. You should be able to explain every point with clarity. 
• 
Research the company – Be curious about the company and the role you are applying for. This 
shows your genuine interest. 
• 
Show eagerness to learn – Even if you don’t know everything, show that you are a quick and 
eager learner. 
• 
Have a good discussion – Don’t just answer; interact with the interviewer. Try to connect with 
them by asking meaningful questions about their work and experience. 
• 
Think before answering – When a question is asked, take a moment to think. Then explain 
your answer clearly and confidently. 
• 
Handle unknown questions smartly – Even if you don’t know the exact answer, try to say 
something related to the question and show your thought process. Interviewers often value 
the way you think. 
 
  All the very best to you! 
Remember – CONFIDENCE IS THE KEY TO SUCCESS. 
Thank you!

Goldman Sachs Internship Interview Experience 
 
About Goldman Sachs:- 
 
 
Goldman Sachs, founded in 1869 and headquartered in New York, is a leading 
global investment banking, securities, and investment management firm. It provides financial 
services to corporations, governments, and individuals worldwide, with a strong focus on 
technology, innovation, and sustainable finance. In India, the firm established its presence in

Eligible Branches: UG: AE, AU, AIDS, BM, CE, CS, EE, EC, EI, GI, IE, IT, MN, MS, ME, MI, PT, PE, 
RP & RA 
 Eligibility Criteria: 6 & above; No current arrears 
 Title: Engineering Analyst Office  
  Location: Bengaluru and Hyderabad  
 Stipend :  Rs.1,62,500 
  Internship Duration: 8 weeks  
About Me:- 
I am Hariharan R I, a Computer Science and Engineering student at Madras 
Institute of Technology, Anna University, with a CGPA of 9.54(then), and the 
department topper. I began practicing Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) from my 
second semester on Problem Solving section of HackerRank and then later moved on 
to LeetCode from my third semester. Though I initially struggled with consistency 
during 3rd semester, I regained momentum in my fourth semester and have since been 
solving LeetCode problems daily, completing more than 500+ problems including the 
LeetCode Top Interview 150 and Striver’s A2Z DSA sheet on the time I attended GS 
Interview. Alongside, I explored full-stack and MERN stack web development during 
my first-year summer break, which greatly supported my fourth-semester Full-stack 
Technologies coursework. I also utilized my 1.5-month summer break after my second 
year for focused internship preparation and consistent DSA practice, which I consider 
as Golden Time in my Intern Preparation Journey, and advice all to prepare seriously 
during that time. 
Online Assessment:- 
The test was conducted on the Hackerrank platform on 27th July 2025, and 
it was the very first online assessment we attended during the internship season. 
 The test had 7 sections with a total duration of 1 hour. 
 All sections were mandatory. 
 Coding Section 1 – 1 question, 10 mins. 
 Coding Section 2 – 1 question, 15 mins. 
 Coding Section 3 – 1 question, 15 mins. 
 MCQ Section 1 – 5 questions, 7 mins. 
 MCQ Section 2 – 2 questions, 3 mins. 
 MCQ Section 3 – 3 questions, 5 mins. 
 MCQ marking scheme – +3 for correct, -1 for incorrect. 
 “Team Conflict HR” section – 1 question, 5 mins. 
The MCQs covered topics such as OOPS, Java, Output, Debugging, Time 
Complexity Analysis, DBMS, OS concepts, along with Aptitude and Maths 
(especially Linear Algebra) questions. Both the coding and MCQ sections carried 
equal weightage, which many participants were not aware of, so it is important to 
give your best in both sections instead of focusing only on one. The total duration 
was just one hour, making time management a key factor. Additionally, there was 
one HR-style question based on team conflict, which tested our situational 
judgment. 
DSA Question-1 :- 
Question 
A robot starts at the top-left corner of an m x n factory floor grid and needs to 
reach the bottom-right corner. The robot can only move in two directions: either one 
step to the right or one step down. The task was to determine the total number of 
unique paths the robot can take to reach the destination. The input consisted of two 
integers representing the number of rows and columns, and the output was the total 
number of unique paths possible under the given movement constraints. 
Approach 
I identified this as a combinatorics problem. To reach the destination, the 
robot must make (m-1) downward moves and (n-1) rightward moves, i.e., (m+n-2) 
total moves in any order. Thus, the problem reduces to computing the binomial 
coefficient C(m+n-2, m-1). I implemented this in C++ using an iterative formula for 
binomial coefficients to avoid overflow from factorials. This gave an efficient solution 
with O(min(m, n)) time complexity and O(1) space. 
Result 
 
In the First Attempt itself, the code had passed all the Test Cases and 
successfully submitted the question and moved on to next. 
 
DSA Question-2 :- 
Question 
Another OA problem was about creating a balanced fertilizer mix. A farmer has N 
organic substances, each with two properties: 
 
Growth Boost (G) → a multiplicative factor that boosts plant growth 
 
Soil Toxicity (T) → an additive factor that harms soil quality 
The goal was to select at least one substance such that the absolute difference 
between the total growth boost (product of selected G values) and the total toxicity 
(sum of selected T values) is minimized. 
The input consisted of an integer N followed by N pairs of integers (G, T), and the 
output was the minimum possible difference across all valid combinations. 
Approach 
Since N ≤ 10, I solved it using a bitmask subset approach. 
 
For each non-empty subset of substances, I computed: 
 Total Growth as the product of selected G values. 
 Total Toxicity as the sum of selected T values. 
 
I then tracked the absolute difference between them and updated the 
minimum difference. 
 
This works efficiently because there are only 2^N - 1 subsets (at most 1023 
when N=10). 
The solution had O(2^N * N) time complexity (iterating through subsets and 
elements) and O(1) extra space. 
Result 
 
The Code had only passed 11 out of 12 Test Cases and due to time constraint, 
I later moved on to next question. 
 
 
DSA Question-3 :- 
Question 
One of the OA problems was to implement a mentor allotment system in a 
consulting organization. Each employee is described by two attributes: skill level (A) 
and project exposure (B). An employee can be mentored only by someone with 
equal or higher skill and exposure. Among all valid mentors, the one with the 
smallest exposure difference is chosen, and if there’s a tie, the smallest skill 
difference is used. Each mentor can guide only one employee, and if a seeker re-
queries, their previous mentor is permanently barred from mentoring anyone else. 
The input consisted of a series of operations: E A B when a new employee joins and 
Q i when the i-th employee requests mentorship. The task was to output the most 
suitable mentor’s ID or "NE" if none exist. 
Approach 
Since the constraints allowed up to 22,000 operations, a brute-force search for each 
query would be inefficient. To solve this, I used a segment tree of sets: 
 
Employees were stored in the tree based on skill levels, with each node 
maintaining a sorted set of {exposure, id} pairs. 
 
When a new employee joined, they were inserted into the tree. When an 
employee queried for a mentor, their data was temporarily removed to avoid 
self-selection. 
 
For queries, I searched in the range of skills ≥ seeker’s skill and used 
lower_bound to quickly find the first mentor with exposure ≥ seeker’s 
exposure. Tie-breaking was done using exposure first, then skill difference. 
 
Once a mentor was assigned, they were removed from the tree to ensure one-
to-one matching. If the seeker re-queried, the old mentor was permanently 
blocked. 
This approach ensured efficient handling of joins and queries in O(log A) per 
operation, with overall complexity around O(N log A) and space O(N). 
Result  
 
I had atteneded this question after completing MCQs Section, So I had very 
short time for solving this and so I got strucked up with Runtime errors and had no 
time to debug, so I haven’t completely attended this question and this is the hardest 
question of all the three. 
 
 
Interview Rounds :- 
After three days on 30 July 2025, the interview shortlist was released, and 30 
candidates were selected for the next rounds—19 from MIT and 11 from CEG. However, 
since the Appian interview took place before the Goldman Sachs interview, two of the 
shortlisted candidates had already been selected by Appian. As a result, only 28 
candidates attended the Goldman Sachs interview on 11th August 2025. 
There were three interview rounds in total—two technical rounds and one 
resume-based technical + HR round. The process was organized across three panels, with 
each panel handling a different round for a candidate. For example, if a candidate 
attended Round 1 in Panel A, then they would attend Round 2 in Panel B and Round 3 in 
Panel C. In my case, I attended Round 1 in Panel C, Round 2 in Panel B, and Round 3 in 
Panel A. 
Interview Round 1 :- 
 
Round 1 was conducted in two batches. 15 candidates attended the morning 
session, while I was part of the afternoon batch of 13 candidates. In the morning batch, 
out of 15 participants, 4 were shortlisted for Round 2. During that time, the rest of us 
were asked to wait and instructed to go for lunch while those 4 attended their next 
round. 
I attended my Round 1 interview at 3:00 PM, and it lasted for about 30 minutes. 
The interviewer began by asking me to introduce myself, where I highlighted my 
academic background, technical skills, and problem-solving experience. After this, he 
asked if I was familiar with Sudoku. I replied yes, and he then asked me to explain the 
rules of the game. Once I explained, he acknowledged my response and gave me a DSA 
problem based on it — Valid Sudoku, where the task was to check whether a given 
solved Sudoku is valid or not. 
I explained  my approach step by step: I planned to use static 2D arrays to check 
for duplicates in rows, columns, and 3x3 fields. For this, I created three 9x9 arrays, 
where each array tracked whether a number had already been used in a particular row, 
column, or field. I then demonstrated the logic, showing how I would store and check 
values in each structure. For the fields, I calculated the field number and applied the 
same duplicate-checking logic. Although the interviewer expected a set-based approach, 
I clarified that my static array method also worked correctly, and he was satisfied with 
my explanation. 
Next, I discussed the time and space complexity of my solution: O(n²) for 
iterating, storing, and checking, with space complexity of O(3·n²), which reduces to O(1) 
for a fixed-size Sudoku (9x9). As a follow-up, he asked about the property of n, to which 
I answered that n should be a perfect square number. Toward the end, he asked me if I 
had any questions for him, and I specifically asked about the GS Legend platform and its 
role in open-source contributions. He explained it briefly and concluded the round by 
wishing me good luck for the next one. I came out feeling confident that I had 
performed well in this round and had been selected for Round 2. 
Interview Round 2 :-      
In the morning batch of Round 1, 4 candidates were shortlisted for Round 2, 
and in the afternoon batch, 3 more were shortlisted, including myself. My Round 2 
interview started around 4:15 PM and lasted for about 40 minutes. At the 
beginning, the interviewer asked me to introduce myself and then requested me to 
explain the projects I had mentioned in my resume. I walked him through my 
projects, after which he asked a few simple clarifying questions on them. 
The first DSA problem was the Airport–Plane problem: there are multiple 
airports connected by planes, with a cost associated with traveling between them. 
The task was to start from one airport, visit all airports exactly once, and return the 
shortest possible cost, while also explaining the data structure and algorithm to 
implement this. I immediately recognized this as the Travelling Salesman Problem 
(TSP). I explained that the problem can be modeled as a Graph, where airports are 
nodes and planes with costs are weighted edges. I then described the TSP approach, 
explained its working, and wrote pseudocode for the solution. I also mentioned that 
the time complexity of the exact solution using dynamic programming is O(n² · 2ⁿ), 
which becomes infeasible for larger values of n, and that approximate/heuristic 
methods are often used in practice. The interviewer was satisfied with my 
explanation and pseudocode. 
The second problem was about a server handling requests with a fixed buffer size. If 
extra requests arrive when the buffer is full, the server should reply with a message 
that it is full. Each request is handled in a fixed time slot, and after each time slot, the 
next request is served. I identified this as a Queue-based problem. I explained that I 
would enqueue requests as they arrive, respond with a “server full” message if the 
buffer is at capacity, and dequeue a request after every time slot. The time 
complexity for both enqueue and dequeue operations is O(1). Additionally, I 
optimized the dequeuing logic by maintaining a time variable and a time array that 
tracks which requests must be dequeued at each time slot. With this, the time 
pointer simply moves to the next slot for automatic dequeuing in O(1). I further 
explained that we could even optimize by storing the next request’s dequeue time in 
the array itself. The interviewer appreciated this detailed explanation and said my 
logic was correct. 
Toward the end, he asked if I had any questions. I asked about the purpose behind 
setting up the Goldman Sachs Hyderabad office, which was recently opened in 
2021. He explained the reason and wished me all the best for the next round. I came 
out of the room feeling happy and confident that I would be shortlisted for the 
following round. 
 
Interview Round 3 : - 
  
In total, 7 people attended Round 2, out of which 3 were shortlisted for 
Round 3 — one from the morning batch and two from the afternoon batch, and I 
was one among them. I attended my Round 3 interview at 5:15 PM, and it went on 
for about 35–40 minutes. 
The interviewer started by asking me to introduce myself and then moved to my first

 
Where I had used AI in the project. 
 
How I had integrated AI features, and to explain in detail about some of the

 
Whether encryption was required in the network layer, to which I explained 
various cryptography techniques that could be implemented in web 
development projects. 
Next, she asked me about my second full-stack project. The questions included: 
 
Whether I had done any testing on it. 
 
If I had made improvements after testing. 
 
Questions on database indexing, where I also extended my answer by 
explaining paging and lazy loading techniques. 
After that, she shifted to the skills section of my resume and asked: 
 
What I know about Google Cloud (I explained the areas I am most familiar

 
Whether I use Canva for website design (I clarified that I used it mainly for

 
What security features I had implemented in my projects. I explained about

She also asked a few more minor questions related to my resume. Toward the end, 
she asked if I had any questions for her. I asked about the mentorship provided

my queries and then asked me to wait outside. That concluded my final Resume 
based technical + HR interview round. And I came out at around 6pm. 
 
 
 
Aftermath:- 
 
After all the interviews were completed, the three of us shortlisted for the final 
round waited together. We were a bit stressed and spent time discussing the questions 
asked in the previous rounds. At around 6:45 PM, the GS coordinator (four people had 
come in total, including the three panelists) called us in and announced the results. To 
our delight, all three of us were selected for the internship offer. I was overjoyed to 
hear the news, and we were congratulated personally by the coordinators, followed by 
a short conversation with them. 
Out of the three selected candidates, one was from CSE at CEG, while the other two 
were me and my friend from CSE at MIT. With immense happiness, we finally left CUIC 
at around 7:20 PM. 
Personal Tips :- 
 Know your resume well – this is the personal mantra. 
 Practice DSA using LeetCode 150 and Striver’s DSA Sheet. 
 Engage with a senior from 2nd year onwards for intern preparation – in 
my case, a senior from my college guided me throughout. 
 Communicate clearly – explain every step of your thinking while solving 
problems. 
 Use your 2nd-year summer holidays wisely for dedicated internship 
preparation. 
 Don’t get demotivated if others move ahead before you. For example, 
some attended Round 2 in the morning while we were still waiting for 
Round 1 in the afternoon. Ultimately, it’s not first-come-first-serve; they 
select the best performers, and most of the afternoon candidates were

AMAZON SDE INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Amazon is one of the world’s most influential technology and innovation companies, excelling in global e-
commerce, cloud infrastructure (AWS), artificial intelligence, and consumer technology. As a core member of 
the MAANG group (Meta, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, Google), it continues to drive large-scale digital 
transformation and offers developers diverse, high-impact opportunities across cutting-edge domains. 
 
ROLE OVERVIEW 
• 
Position: Software Development Engineer (SDE) Intern 
• 
Duration: 6 Months (performance-based FTE conversion possible) 
• 
Selection Process: 2 Rounds (Online Assessment + Technical Interview) 
 
ROUND 1 – ONLINE ASSESSMENT 
The online test was conducted on HackerRank and included two coding problems followed by personality-
based questions assessing behavioral alignment and problem-solving attitude. The total duration was 70 
minutes, and optimal solutions were required to pass hidden test cases. 
1. Problem Solving 
Question 1 – Dataset Cleaning Cost 
You are given a string dataset of even length consisting of lowercase English letters. You can remove any two 
characters in one operation: 
• 
If both are the same → cost = matchCost 
• 
If different → cost = mismatchCost 
Your goal is to remove all characters while minimizing the total cost. 
Example: 
• 
dataset = "ouio" 
• 
matchCost = 2 
• 
mismatchCost = 4 
Step-by-step Explanation: 
1. The string is "ouio" → characters: 'o', 'o', 'u', 'i'. 
2. Remove 'o' and 'o' → cost = 2 → remaining string = "ui". 
3. Remove 'u' and 'i' → cost = 4. 
Total Cost = 2 + 4 = 6  
 
Question 2 – System Stabilization 
You are given a string pipeline representing service nodes. A node type failedService disrupts the system: 
• 
Every second, each occurrence of failedService removes the preceding character. 
• 
All removals happen simultaneously every second. 
• 
Continue until no more removals occur. Return the number of seconds for stabilization. 
 
Example: 
• 
pipeline = "database" 
• 
failedService = 'a' 
Step-by-step Explanation: 
• 
Initial pipeline: "database" 
• 
Time = 1 → Each 'a' removes preceding characters → removes 'd' and 't' → remaining string = "aaase" 
• 
Time = 2 → Each 'a' removes preceding characters → remaining string = "ase" 
Output = 2 seconds  
 
2. Workstyle Assessment 
A short behavioral test based on Amazon’s Leadership Principles like Customer Obsession, Ownership, and 
Deliver Results. 
• 
Example statements: 
o 
“I take initiative even when tasks aren’t assigned to me.” 
o 
“I try to simplify complex problems.” 
Tips: 
• 
Be consistent with answers. 
• 
Show ownership, teamwork, curiosity, and customer focus. 
• 
Avoid extremes like “Always Disagree.” 
• 
Review Amazon’s Leadership Principles before the test. 
 
3. Work Simulation Assessment 
A situational judgment test simulating real-world scenarios faced by Amazon engineers. 
• 
Example scenario: 
“A teammate finds a critical bug close to deadline — what’s your first step?” 
Tips: 
• 
Prioritize customer impact and data-driven choices. 
• 
Show ownership and balance speed with quality. 
• 
Collaborate and consider team input. 
Both assessments are screening rounds — strong coding alone isn’t enough; alignment with Leadership 
Principles is equally important. 
After the coding section, candidates completed a Work Simulation and reasoning questions aligned with 
Amazon’s Leadership Principles. 107 candidates were shortlisted for the technical interview. 
 
 
ROUND 2 – TECHNICAL INTERVIEW 
• 
Mode: Online (Amazon Chime) 
• 
Duration: 60 minutes (completed early – ~40 minutes) 
• 
Focus: DSA problem-solving, optimal approach discussion, structured reasoning 
The interview began with a brief introduction, followed by two live coding problems emphasizing optimal 
solutions. 
Question 1 – Word Break 
Determine if a string s can be segmented into words from wordDict (reuse allowed). Optimal DP/memoization 
solutions expected. 
• 
Example: 
o 
s = "applepenapple" 
o 
wordDict = ["apple", "pen"] 
o 
Output: true 
o 
Explanation: "applepenapple" → "apple pen apple" 
Question 2 – Sum of Prefix Scores of Strings 
Given an array of words, the score of a string is the count of words that have it as a prefix. Efficient Trie-based 
logic expected. 
• 
Example: 
o 
words = ["abc", "ab", "bc", "b"] 
o 
Output = [5, 4, 3, 2] 
o 
Explanation: 
▪ 
"abc" → prefixes: a, ab, abc → counts: 2 + 2 + 1 = 5 
▪ 
"ab" → a, ab → 2 + 2 = 4 
▪ 
"bc" → b, bc → 2 + 1 = 3 
▪ 
"b" → b → 2 
Bonus Discussion – MST Edge Classification 
Discussion on Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST, Kruskal’s Algorithm, Union-Find, and identifying edges 
that increase MST weight when removed. 
Finally, I asked a few questions about team culture, collaboration, and work-life balance at Amazon, such as: 
• 
How does the team encourage knowledge sharing and mentorship? 
• 
What does a typical day look like for an SDE Intern? 
• 
Opportunities for learning and contributing to impactful projects. 
This led to a positive and engaging closing conversation with the interviewer. 
 
 
 
 
RESULT 
Pleased to have realized my dream — selected for the Amazon SDE Internship (6 months).
 
LEARNINGS & TIPS 
• 
Focus on clarity of explanation — Amazon values reasoning as much as code. 
• 
Think aloud and explain your thought process clearly. 
• 
Revise Trees, Graphs, DP, and Heaps. 
• 
Understand Amazon’s Leadership Principles. 
• 
Always aim for optimal solutions — brute force may fail hidden cases. 
• 
Be confident and ask thoughtful questions to the interviewer. 
 
MY LEARNING JOURNEY 
• 
DSA Concepts: Striver (A–Z) 
• 
Writing Clean Code: NeetCode (YouTube) 
• 
Problem-Solving Mindset: Larry (YouTuber)

About Dover 
Dover is a diversified global manufacturer and solutions provider headquartered in Downers 
Grove, Illinois, USA, with revenue exceeding $7 billion. The company provides innovative 
equipment and components, consumable supplies, aftermarket parts, software and digital 
solutions, and support services across five operating segments: Engineered Products, Clean 
Energy & Fueling, Imaging & Identification, Pumps & Process Solutions, and Climate & 
Sustainability Technologies. For over 70 years, Dover has been recognized for its 
entrepreneurial approach. With a team of approximately 24,000 employees, the company 
embraces an ownership mindset and works closely with customers to redefine what’s 
possible. 
 
 
Dover visited our campus for summer internship hiring. The usual selection process included 
one online assessment, two technical interviews, and an HR round. However, in my case, I 
had only one online assessment followed by a single technical interview. 
 
Round 1: Online Assessment 
The test consisted of 60 multiple-choice questions to be completed in 60 minutes. It was 
divided into four sections: 
• 
Computer Science Fundamentals (15 questions) 
• 
Logical Reasoning (15 questions) 
• 
Quantitative Aptitude (15 questions) 
• 
Data Structures and Algorithms (15 questions) 
The level of difficulty was moderate, but speed and accuracy were essential. With consistent 
practice in problem-solving platforms, I found this round manageable. Out of all the 
participants, 15 students were shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Round 2: Technical Interview 
Since I had highlighted my strength in problem-solving, the interview mainly focused on DSA 
and project discussion. 
• 
DSA Problems: 
o Count Number of Nice Subarrays: 
The interviewer asked me to solve this problem, as it was one of the daily 
challenges I had recently solved on LeetCode. I walked them through the 
problem, explained the naive and optimized approach, and wrote clean code 
using prefix sums and hashmap for an efficient solution. 
o Frequency of the Most Frequent Element: 
For this, I was asked to explain only the logic. I discussed the sliding window 
technique after sorting, maintaining a window with two pointers, and how to 
keep track of the current frequency. I also mentioned the time complexity 
and edge cases. 
• 
Project Discussion: 
I was asked to explain one of the projects I had listed on my resume. I chose a full-
stack project where I used DSA in backend logic. I explained the overall architecture, 
the flow between frontend and backend, and how specific data structures (like 
hashmaps and arrays) helped in efficient data handling. 
The interviewer also asked why I chose certain structures and whether the system 
could scale with larger data. 
Overall, it was a very engaging and focused interview. I appreciated that the interviewer took 
interest in my LeetCode profile and asked relevant questions. 
 
Out of 15 shortlisted students, only 3 were selected for the summer internship at Dover, and 
I was fortunate to be one of them.  
 
Tips for Preparing for Dover Internship 
1. Master the Basics + DSA: 
Focus on CS fundamentals (OS, DBMS, CN, OOP) and practice DSA daily. Most 
interview questions come from topics like sliding window, prefix sum, and hashmaps. 
2. Practice Aptitude & Logical Reasoning: 
The online assessment isn’t just coding—quantitative and logical questions also 
matter. Practice regularly on platforms like IndiaBix and GeeksforGeeks. 
3. Keep Your Profiles Ready: 
Update your LeetCode and GitHub profiles. Interviewers might ask to see your recent 
activity or pick questions based on your solved problems. 
4. Know Your Projects Well: 
Be ready to explain your projects, especially the logic and DSA used. Highlight design 
decisions, challenges faced, and how you solved them.

MICROSOFT INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
Microsoft Corporation is an American multinational corporation and technology company 
headquartered in Redmond, Washington.[2] Its best-known software products are the Windows 
line of operating systems, the Microsoft 365 suite of productivity applications, the Azure cloud 
computing platform and the Edge web browser. Its flagship hardware products are the Xbox 
video game consoles and the Microsoft Surface lineup of touchscreen personal computers. 
 
Role Offered: Software Engineer 
Stipend:  1,25,000 INR per month 
Sequence of Selection: Online Test, Technical Interview 1, Technical Interview 2 
(Completely Virtual) 
  
Online Test: 
It consisted of two problems. 
 
 
First Problem:  
Given two very long numbers in a linked list, how many swaps do you need to make the 
absolute difference between the number’s minimum. 
You can only swap corresponding indices. That is A[i1] can only be swapped with B[i2]. 
 
Example Test Case:  
 
A = “9 8 6 5” 
 
B = “2 3 4 5” 
 
 
Output: 1; 
Explanation: We can swap 8 with 3 and 6 with 4 and the answer would be 2 swaps with a 
minimum value of 6480 (9345-2865). But this is not the minimum. The minimum is swap is 
1 because 9345-2865 is also 6480. 
 
Second Problem: 
Given a string of directions consisting of “N” (North), “E” (East) and “W” (West). Find the 
shortest path from the end point to the initial point by using only “E”, “S”, and “W”. The 
extra note given was to that the first and last move will be North for all testcases. 
Example Testcase:  
 
S = “NENWN” 
This would look like, (The black stroke) 
 
 
End point: 
 
     
 
 
 
 
Start point: 
The output would be, 
 
Output: “WSSSE” (The red stroke) 
 
My Experience: 
The text editor was like, we can run testcases and custom inputs. But after submission we 
cannot modify the submitted solution. So, I did submit after thinking of a solution. It was a 
greedy approach. I think personally think the right answer would be in dp. For the second 
question I was able to come up with a right solution. We can also use bfs also. I did an 
iterative solution directly. 
 
The result was declared after one hour. Only 5 candidates were chosen out of the whole 
college. 
Technical Interview 1: 
This was 36 hours later. 
9:30 am 
First, I had to install teams in my system to attend the interview. 
Then for the whole interview, I had to keep my camera ‘on’ and also share my screen. He 
also gave a common link for us both to code at the same time. 
Then the first question the interviewer asked me was that if I was familiar with linked lists. I 
answered Yes. 
He asked me the question, that I have to delete any nodes that has than more than one 
occurrence. The given list was also sorted. So, for example, if the given list was 1->2->2->3, 
the result should be 1->3. The deletion should be in-place. So, the head should be returned.  
I was able to talk to him and also come up with a working solution. 
 
Then we moved on to the next question. The question was to print all the prime numbers until 
500. I used Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm to come up with an efficient solution.  
Takeaway:  
In my opinion, the questions were easy. The critical factor in the interview was the way you 
solve it. 
Your approach only matters. If you can talk and do the solution parallelly, it shows the 
interviewer your thinking process. So, be sure to talk during the interview. 
Technical Interview 2: 
3 hours later (Same day) 
The same initial setup with sharing my screen with my camera. 
The interviewer first asked me not to be nervous and asked me to explain my thought at each 
step. Then the question asked to me was https://leetcode.com/problems/restore-ip-
addresses/description/ . I never solved this question at leetcode but I was able to come up 
with a solution during the interview. I coded the entire solution in notepad. 
The interview lasted for an hour.  
Takeaway: 
As I said talking out your solution is what matters the most. The solution I came up with was 
not very efficient at all but I still explain my though process. 
Conclusion: 
After 2.5 hours (Same day)  
I got the message that I was selected for their internship. (Completely over the moon).  
Always try to explain your thoughts. The interviewer helps you to come to the right solution. 
Don’t panic, stay calm and try to code your answer.  
 
All the very best.

Wells Fargo & Co. Interview Experiences (Internship)  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
About Wells Fargo & Co. 
 
Wells Fargo is a major American financial services company headquartered in 
San Francisco, California. It provides banking, investment, mortgage, and consumer 
finance services to individuals, businesses, and institutions. Founded in 1852 during the 
California Gold Rush, Wells Fargo is now among the largest banks in the U.S. by assets 
and branch network. 
 
Eligibility Criteria 
• 7.5 & above 
• No current arrears 
 
Selection Process 
1. Pre-Placement Talk (Online) 
2. Online Assessment (Offline at CUIC) 
3. Technical + HR Interview (Offline at CUIC) 
 
Preparation Overview 
• Try to solve questions on Leetcode, Hackerrank, GeeksForGeeks to improve your problem-
solving skills.  
• I used the NeetCode-150 DSA sheet and Striver’s sheet as well.  
• Avoid jumping directly to solutions; try solving them on your own first. Memorizing 
solutions doesn’t help in the long run. 
• Focus on pattern-based learning. Almost all of the DSA problems will fall under a certain 
pattern for which solutions will need a similar approach. 
• Go through common interview questions, and study DSA, OOPS, DBMS, OS, Networks, 
and SDLC concepts, as well as the fundamentals of at least one programming language. 
• Be confident in everything you include in your resume. Always be ready to explain your 
projects with clarity. Never include a topic in your resume or talk about it in the interviews 
that you are not comfortable with. You will be asked follow-up questions as well, so have a 
deep understanding of whatever you include in your resume.  
• Try solving aptitude questions as well. 
• Don’t get discouraged by failure. I failed one interview, but succeeded the next. 
 
Online Assessment 
It was conducted on the SHL platform at CUIC. It consisted of 4 sections. 
 
 
Section 1: Coding (60 minutes, 1 question) 
• The driver code wasn’t provided. We were required to code from scratch. 
• There weren’t any choices for the language. We were asked to code in Java. 
• The question was a medium-hard graph-based question. 
• There was a different set of questions. 
 
 
 
 
Question: 
You are given a set of cities and the cost to reach from one city to another in a matrix (each row is a 
distance vector of a city). Find the minimum cost to visit every city only once and return to the start 
city. ( -1 indicates that the city is not reachable) 
Input: 
Number of cities: 3 
Cost matrix: 
-1 10 50 
50 -1 10 
10 50 0 
Output: 30. We can visit 0->1->2->0 and cost = 10+10+10 = 30. 
 
Out of two test cases, my code passed one. The challenge I faced here wasn’t the problem itself, but 
the language. Since we studied Java in our 3rd semester, it took me around 40 minutes to recall the 
syntax and libraries. Later, I read the document where they specified details of the internship and 
found that they anticipate Java as a compulsory language to be known. This taught me the 
importance of carefully reading company-specific guidelines before appearing for any online 
assessments or interviews. 
 
Section 2: Verbal aptitude (15 minutes, 20 questions) 
• Reading comprehension, detecting what is wrong with the sentence, finding the 
synonym/antonym, etc. 
• IndiaBIX has similar questions, which you can use to practice. 
• This section required basic English knowledge. 
 
Section 3: Business aptitude (25 minutes, 14 questions) 
• This section contained pie charts, bar graphs, and candlestick graphs interpretation 
questions. 
• It also had questions on some basic Math and logic, like ratios, etc. 
• IndiaBIX provides similar practice questions. 
 
The problem I faced in this section was the time constraint. I read seniors’ experiences, which 
guided me on what can be asked in this section. Familiarity with basic stock market and finance-
related terms can also be helpful. 
 
Section 4: Coding (45 minutes, 2 questions, free navigation) 
• The questions were of easy-medium difficulty. 
• Each one of us received different questions. 
• The driver code was provided, and choices for the language as well. 
 
Question 1: Find the duplicates in the array. 
Question 2: Given a 1D array that represents the state of lamp posts along a street (0-off, 1-on). If 
the states before and after a particular lamp post are both off then the next day this lamp post will 
also be turned off. Given such an array and the no of days, find the state of the lamp posts at the 
end. 
Input: 
Array: [0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0] 
Days: 2 
Output: 
Initial: [0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0] 
Day 1: 
• index 0 → no left neighbor (keep as 0). 
• index 1 → neighbors = (0,1) → not both 0 → stays 1. 
• index 2 → neighbors = (1,0) → not both 0 → stays 1. 
• index 3 → neighbors = (1,1) → not both 0 → stays 0. 
• index 4 → neighbors = (0,0) → both 0 → becomes 0. 
• index 5 → neighbors = (1,0) → not both 0 → stays 0. 
• index 6 → no right neighbor (keep as 0). 
Result after Day 1: [0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0] Final result: [0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0] 
 
39 students were shortlisted for the interview, and I was one among them. 
 
Technical Interview: 
• This interview round was conducted virtually through a video call at CUIC.  
• I was asked to give a self-introduction. 
• The interviewer asked what language I am comfortable with, and I said C++. He asked me 
OOPS-related questions on inheritance, polymorphism, abstract classes, overloading Vs. 
overriding specific to C++. 
• I used SHA-256 in one of my projects, and so he asked me about it. He asked me why I used 
SHA-256 and asked me to explain it.  
• I was asked DBMS-related questions, like the difference between relational and document-
type DB. I used MongoDB in my projects, and so he asked me about its applications and 
why we use it. He asked me about joins and indices. He asked me in which kind of databases 
joins and indices are powerful. 
• He asked me about Semaphore, and its use. I explained that it helps in concurrency control 
during race conditions and can also prevent deadlocks to some extent. 
• He asked me server-side fundamentals, like API and types of API, RestAPI. I was also asked 
about SOAP.  
• I was asked DSA questions at the end. I was asked to explain Doubly Linked List, Deque, 
BFS & DFS, along with their real-life applications. I was asked to explain Dijkstra’s 
algorithm and Red-Black tree. 
• I was also asked to explain one of my projects. 
These are the questions I recall, though the interviewer asked several more. To crack any technical 
round interviews, be prepared in DSA, OOP, DBMS, OS, Computer Networks, and your resume as 
well. 
 
Technical-HR interview: 
• This round went about 20 minutes and was conducted in person. 
• I was asked to give a self-introduction. 
• The interviewer asked me questions on OOPS. He asked why the concept of OOPS was 
introduced, given that modularity was already achieved through procedures in Procedural 
Programming Languages. 
• He then asked me to explain exactly how real-world mapping is done in OOPS. 
• He asked me to explain all of the OOPS concepts I know. 
• He asked me to explain one of my projects.  
• I used MERN stack, and so he asked me to tell the difference between Node.js, Express.js, 
and React.js. 
• He asked me about an internship I did for automation in our department. 
• He asked me how I study and what strategies I use to learn a concept. 
• I was given a chance to ask questions. I asked about Wells Fargo’s projects, work culture, 
and ownership provided to the interns. 
• He then started explaining how the corporate world works and work-life at Wells Fargo. He 
gave me some tips and advice. 
 
HR interview: 
• This round was conducted in person.  
• I was asked to give a self-introduction. 
• I was asked why I want to do an internship at Wells Fargo. 
• I was given a chance to ask questions. I asked about the same I asked in the second round to 
know her perspective. We then started discussing a lot about Wells Fargo. She was very 
approachable and friendly. We talked about her domain in Wells Fargo and her experience 
working in Wells Fargo.  
And finally, the result came with my name included, along with the other nine selected candidates 
for the intern role. 
General Advice: 
• Practice platforms: LeetCode, HackerRank, GeeksForGeeks, takeUForward, NeetCode, 
IndiaBIX 
• https://neetcode.io/practice?tab=neetcode150  
• C.O.D.E series conducted by ACM-CEG 
• Blind 75 - LeetCode 
• MUST-DO Questions for [DBMS, CN, OS] Interviews: SDE Core Sheet - Tutorial  
• Striver’s SDE Sheet – Top Coding Interview Problems

1.Balancing Elements 
When an element is deleted from an array, the higher-indexed elements shift down one index to fill 
the gap, A “balancing element” is defined as an element that, when deleted from the array, results 
in the sum of the even-indexed elements being equal to the sum of the odd-indexed elements. 
Determine how many balancing elements a given array contains. 
Example: 
Input: 
n=5 
arr = [5,5,2,8] 
Output: 
2 
When the first or second 5 is deleted, the array becomes [5, 2, 5, 8]. The sum[even] = 5+5=10 and 
sum[odd]=-2+ 8-10. No other elements of the original array have that property. There are 2 
balancing elements: arr[0] and arr[1].  
 
2. Maximum Difference  
Given a number of nodes and edges, construct an undirected graph, connected component of the 
graph is any group nodes, For each connected component, determine the difference between its 
maximum and minimum value. Return the maximum of all of these differences.  
For example given a graph with 4 nodes, connected as follows: (1, 2, 3), and (4). The only connected 
component has a difference 3-1=2 
Input: 
int g_node; 
int g_from[g_edegs]; 
int g_to[g_edges]; 
Output; 
int maximum_absolute_diff 
 
3. Paint the Ceiling 
Given n, s0, k, b, m, a 
s[i] = ((k*s[i-1] mod m)+1+s[i-1]) for 1<= I <=n 
Example: 
s=[2,4,6] 
Take all combination of 2 elements with repeation and find how many s1*s2<=a.   
 
4. Positive Negative 
The traders of Hackerland have stock prediction model that they want to backtest. The predicted 
profit earned every month by a certain portfolio is given by an array pnl of n integers where a 
negative integer denotes loss. 
 The traders decide to iterate through the months from 1 to n, and if the pnl is positive, they add it 
to their profit and if the pnl is negative, they can either subtract it or skip it. The net profit must 
always be greater than or equal to 0. 
Given the array pnl and an integer k, find the maximum total profit that can be earned by the 
portfolio after subtracting at least k losses. If it is not possible to take at least k losses, report -1 as 
the answer.  
Example: Suppose n=5, k=2 and pnl=[4, -3, 6, -2, -1] 
The optimal strategy is to add the profits 4 and 6, and the losses [-2, -1] to get a net profit of 7 which 
is the maximum possible.

The interview was conducted in person at CUIC. It went for about 30-35 min. 
The interviewer first went through my resume and asked about the Clubs mentioned in my

The interviewer started to test me in Data Structures. She asked about stacks, queues, and

The interviewer gave one scenario. Consider your friend or neighbor asking you to develop 
the pickle e-commerce for their business. You have to develop the website including the 
backend and database. She asked what tech stacks are you going to use, and what is the 
website architecture and asked about website flow. 
 Fortunately, this website looked like my DBMS project. I explained all the things. She’d 
asked why did you use PostgreSQL instead of MySQL or any other tool. I said that I already 
implemented PostgreSQL in my project.  Then she asked what if I searched manga instead 
of mango, and asked me how to implement this. I said if there is only Tamil language. I will 
store all Tamil equivalent words in the database. For all region words, I will use Google 
Translate API to convert region words to English. She asked what if I searched maavadu 
instead of manga, and I said that I would use a dictionary for it.  
The interviewer gave a Palindrome problem( https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/c-program-
check-given-string-palindrome/)  and I said the brute force approach. She asked me to write 
code on paper. She did not tell to optimize the code but I said I have optimized approach 
which is using two-pointer without any extra space, we can solve problem.    
She asked questions related to my certifications. She asked what is REST API and I was 
able to answer. She asked full form of REST and I said I forgot it. Then, She asked full form 
of API and I answered. She inquired about what is stateless and stateful. I could not answer 
it correctly. 
 
Overall, the interview went very well and I was shortlisted for the next round. 
10 students were selected for the next interview. I was fortunate to be one of them. 
 
Round 3 – Technical + HR Interview

The interviewer asked me what is HTTP default port number. He explained how client-server 
communication works and then asked the same question what is the HTTP default port 
number? I took some time to think about it. He asked don’t you know? I said yes. He moved

Which language you know, he asked. I said C++. He gave me the paper to write code. The

ONLINE TEST 
The Online test consisted of four sections and was conducted on SHL platform at CUIC. It is a 
proctored test where you need to keep your webcam on. The total duration was 1hr 51 
minutes which was around 2hrs. 
 
SECTION 1(CODING SECTION) 
This section consisted of 2 coding questions which were easy to medium difficulty level 
timed for 45 minutes. 
Question 1: 
This question was similar to finding number of non-repeating characters in a string. 
I used an unordered map to solve this problem. 
Question 2: 
This question was described to sort in ascending order till k and after k ,we should sort it in 
descending order and finally merge and print the result. 
I used inbuilt functions like sort()  and reverse() to solve this problem as they did not 
mention about restricting the use of inbuilt functions. 
All test cases passed in both the questions. 
 
SECTION 2 (SQL) 
This section consisted around 13-15 MCQ questions regarding SQL which included queries, 
SQL keywords and finding correct queries and so on. This section was timed for 15 minutes. 
You can score full marks in this section as this only tested the basic knowledge in SQL. 
 
SECTION 3 (APTITUDE) 
This section actually took a lot of time to solve as there were 24 questions timed for 36 
minutes. 
The questions were from : 
1.Numerical Reasoning(Graphs,percentages,profit,sales) 
2.Logical Reasoning(Ranking,Ordering,Scheduling) 
3.Abstract/Inductive Reasoning(Figure patterns,alphanumeric sequence) 
They come under Cognitive Ability test and section had more weightage ,so try to solve as 
many questions and there is no negative marking. 
 
TIPS: 
1.Read questions carefully because for five questions same question can be repeated. 
2.Be fast in doing calculation when dealing with stock,profit,percentage related questions. 
3.Learn shortcuts for pattern solving,percentages,etc… 
4.Solve many questions from this section because it can boost your total marks in the overall 
assessment. 
 
SECTION 4(ENGLISH AND COMPREHENSION) 
 
This section consisted of nearly 10-12 questions of MCQ type and  can be solved easily with 
basic English grammar knowledge.You can score full marks in this section too.This section 
was timed for 15 minutes. 
The questions were like to choose appropriate words,synonyms,antonyms and error 
spotting. 
They gave a comprehension and asked 2-3 questions from that. 
You can choose any section to work on and there is no restriction between navigating 
questions and also you will have sufficient time to choose next section after you have 
completed one section as the timer will stop and then starts once you enter the next section  
and choose accordingly.

RtBrick Interview Experience 
 
Company Overview: 
RtBrick is a pioneering company in the networking industry, dedicated to 
transforming traditional network infrastructures with innovative software 
solutions. Our mission is to enable service providers to build more flexible, 
scalable, and cost-effective networks using cutting-edge technologies. 
This was the first time Rtbrick recruited the students in CUIC and first time 
they recruited the freshers.  
Role Offered: Associate Software Developer 
Stipend: 35,000 INR per month 
Sequence of Job Selection: Online Assessment, Written test, 3 Technical 
Interviews  
Online Assessment (08.08.2025): 40 mins MCQ Assessment 
The test lasted for 40 mins. The MCQ consisted of topics like Networks especially 
in Subnetting and IP addresses, basics of C, Operating systems. 
On 12.08.2025, I wrote one written test and did 3 technical interviews 
1. Written Test 
• The written test consisted of one question that was an ID allocation 
problem and they insisted us to write the code in C. 
• I was given two functions alloc_id() and free_id(int n). 
• I was asked to write these functions where in alloc_id () I need to 
alloc minimum possible id available in a pool of id from 1-INT_MAX. 
• In free_id (int n), I want to free the given id. 
• They expected to solve using Heap and Priority Queue. 
Out of 32 candidates, 16 were shortlisted. 
2. Technical Interview 1 (40 mins): 
 
The technical interview started with an introduction of myself.  The 
interviewer asked about my Blockchain Project and he asked me to convince him 
to use Blockchain Technology, He also asked, what is Blockchain Technology and 
where it is used. 
 
And he asked me whether I know C, python. But I said I am currently 
practising in Java and he accepted me to write in Java. He asked to implement 
the optimal sorting algorithm. The second question was to implement the 
greatest repeating numbers. Next question was to check if the number is even 
or odd without using modular operator, he was expecting bit manipulation. 
Out of 16, 7 were shortlisted for Technical Interview 2. 
3. Technical Interview 2 (60 mins): 
 
In Second Interview, I was interviewed by a 2011 CEG passed out student 
named Dhivya. She asked the intro and then she asked about the projects. She 
asked whether I know C, Python Because the company works in those languages 
and she asked me to learn C and python to work with. 
 
She asked me to explain about the OSI model with the example of how 
the Mail is send from one user to another user. Next, she asked about the basic 
definitions of Routers, Switches, Hub and what are the differences. Then she 
asked about the difference between IP address and MAC address. And then, she 
asked me whether I know bit wise operator and implement the bitwise addition 
of two numbers and next the question was, what happen when we declare a 
variable internally and how the variable is stored in the computer.  
 
Then I was asked to implement whether the given IP is valid IP or not and 
then next question was to identify the IP addresses in a given text document.

About Tekion: 
Tekion is a cloud-built platform focussed on transforming consumer experience in the automotive 
industry. The company uses advanced machine learning and AI capabilities for a personalised and 
seamless consumer experience. 
Online Round: 
Online round consists of 2 sections 
MCQ: 20 questions (DBMS, CN, Dsa, OOPS) 
DSA: 
Problem 1: 
 You are given two arrays of equal length: process[] and state[].The process [] array contains 
alphanumeric process identifiers such as "A1", "B102", etc., while the state [] array contains only "Login" 
or "Logout", indicating the corresponding action for each process at the same index. A process’s 
activation counts increases by 1 whenever it logs in, and decreases by 1 whenever it logs out, but the 
count can never go below 0. After processing all the events in order, you are given an integer m, and 
your task is to determine which processes remain active in the system with an activation count strictly 
less than m. process = ["B1", "A1", "B1", "B1", "A1"] state = ["Login", "Login", "Login", "Login", "Logout"] 
m = 2  
Problem 2: 
 You are given a matrix of size n × m. You must pick exactly (m-1) rows. Then construct a "set" (really, an 
array of column maximums): For each column j, take the maximum value among the chosen rows in that 
column. 
The score = the minimum value in that array of column maximums. 
Goal: Find the maximum possible score over all choices of (m-1) rows. 
 
I did the MCQ well. I was able to solve the first question completely and in second question I passed 
8/15 with a greedy approach. The results were announced on the same night 
 
Interview: 
Round 1 (Virtual) - Technical 
The interviewer began by asking me to introduce myself. After that, I was asked to open a 
compiler of my choice and solve the following  DSA problems. 
Problem 1: 
Given integers n and k, return an array containing all possible numbers with n digits such that 
the absolute difference between every pair of adjacent digits is k. The number should not 
contain any leading zeros. 
My Approach: 
• 
I first explained a brute-force solution where I would generate all possible n-digit 
numbers and check whether they satisfy the condition. 
• 
I then provided an optimal solution using recursion, where only valid numbers were 
generated without exploring invalid possibilities. 
• 
The interviewer tested my recursive solution with a few test cases, and it worked 
correctly. 
After this, he asked me about my favorite data structure. I mentioned that it is the stack, and 
explained the reasons behind my preference. I was expecting a stack-related problem next, but 
the interviewer moved on to a different question. 
Problem 2: Subarray Sum Equals K 
My approach: 
• 
I first explained a brute-force solution by generating all subarrays and checking whether 
their sum equals k. I also discussed the time and space complexity of this approach. 
• 
Then, I provided an optimal solution using a hash map to keep track of prefix sums. 
• 
The interviewer tested my solution with a case where the array contained negative 
numbers. My solution also worked correctly for this scenario, and the test case passed. 
At the end of the interview, I asked the interviewer some questions about the work 
environment and also requested tips for the upcoming rounds. 
 
Round 2: (Offline) - Technical 
• 
The interviewer introduced himself, spoke about his experience at Tekion, and then 
asked me to introduce myself. 
• 
In my introduction, I mentioned that I had participated in competitive coding 
competitions. This caught his attention, and he asked me more about it. 
• 
He asked what I knew about Tekion. 
• 
He also asked whether Tekion was in my wishlist of companies. I replied yes and 
explained why. 
• 
Then, he asked me a series of technical questions: 
o What are microservices? 
o What are asynchronous and synchronous functions? 
o What is concurrency, and how it is different from parallelism? 
o What is a garbage collector, and why is it useful? 
o What scheduling algorithms do I know, and how can they be used to achieve 
concurrency? 
• 
DSA Problem: Missing Number 
I explained three different approaches to solve this problem and discussed the time and 
space complexity for each approach. 
• 
He then moved to computer networks and asked: 
o What is computer networks? 
o What protocols do you know? 
o Explain the SMTP protocol. 
o What are TCP and UDP? 
o What is the difference between TCP and UDP, and where are they useful? 
o Which protocol would you use if you had to announce an upcoming disaster to a 
group of people? 
• 
At the end, he asked whether I had any questions for him. I said yes, and I asked him to 
tell me more about Tekion and also about the role of a software engineer at Tekion. 
Round 3: (Offline) – hr round 
• 
The HR interviewer introduced herself and then asked me to introduce myself. 
• 
 She asked me about my experience in the first and second rounds. 
• 
 She asked what my favorite project is and why. 
• 
 She asked me to explain what I know about Tekion. 
• 
 I explained the projects I had built, the difficulties I faced during those projects, and how I 
overcame them. 
• 
 She asked why I chose Tekion. 
• 
 At the end, I asked her the same questions I had asked in Round 2. With her permission, I also 
asked a third question (which I don’t fully remember). 
 
 
Takeaways: 
• 
Strong knowledge in DSA, OS, CN, DBMS, and OOPS is required.  
• 
Prepare your self-introduction well. Try to include your interests, the tech stacks you know, the 
projects you have done, and any achievements if you have them. 
• 
 For DSA questions, don’t jump directly to the optimal solution. First, explain the brute force 
solution along with its time and space complexity, then move to the optimal solution, 
highlighting why it is better and how you arrived at the intuition. Even if you don’t know the 
exact solution, share your thought process and intuition. 
• 
 Be well-prepared on your projects—know them in detail, including challenges faced and how 
you solved them. 
• 
 Always prepare a few questions to ask the interviewer at the end of the round. 
• 
Be confident and Be prepared 
Results were announced on the same day

APPLE – INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
About the Company: 
An internship at Apple has always felt like a dream come true for many tech enthusiasts, and 
I was no exception. Apple, the company that’s always been a step ahead in tech, is based in 
Cupertino, California. It's been shaping the future for as long as I can remember. I had the 
privilege of interning at their Hyderabad campus, which is a hub of innovation in India. The 
work here focuses on areas like maps development, machine learning, and analytics, all 
while fostering a collaborative environment. What stood out most to me was how much 
emphasis is placed on solving real-world problems with real impact. 
This internship was not just a professional milestone but also an opportunity for immense 
personal growth. Working alongside industry experts and contributing to meaningful projects 
made it a truly rewarding journey. 
 
Internship Details: 
• 
Timeline: January 2025 – June 2025. 
• 
Role: Software Dev Eng. in Test Intern(SDET Intern). 
• 
Monthly Stipend: ₹105,000 per month. 
• 
Monthly Housing Allowance: ₹35,100 per month. 
• 
Relocation Services: One time Economy-class round trip and relocation assistance for 6 
months. 
Interview Process 
The selection process comprised the following stages: 
1. Online Test (Eliminatory) 
2. Resume Shortlisting 
3. Interview Round 1: Technical & Eliminatory 
4. Interview Round 2: Techno-Managerial 
Online Test 
The first stage of the Apple SDET internship process was an online assessment. This round included: 
• 
Coding Questions: Two problems related to arrays and strings, rated as easy to medium 
difficulty. 
• 
Technical MCQs: A set of 6-7 multiple-choice and multi-select questions covering software 
engineering and testing concepts. 
Honestly, my coursework in Software Engineering turned out to be a lifesaver here. It helped me 
approach the MCQs confidently, and overall, this round felt pretty straightforward. 
Interview Round 1: Technical 
This round was eliminatory and was conducted via Webex Video and CoderPad. It involved live 
coding, where you code and compile while explaining your approach. 
 
Coding Questions: 
1. Add Two Numbers Represented by Two Arrays 
o 
Problem Overview: You are given two arrays representing two numbers. Each array’s 
elements correspond to the digits of the number. The task is to compute their sum and 
return the result as another array. 
o 
Approach: 
▪ 
Traverse both arrays from the last digit to the first, maintaining a carry for 
sums exceeding 9. 
▪ 
Append result digits to a new array in reverse order, then reverse it to get the 
final answer. 
2. Linked List Problem: 
o 
The interviewer posed subquestions of the above question to evaluate edge case 
handling and optimization of code. 
3. Resume-Based Discussion: 
o 
The second half of the interview revolved around my research internship project 
(DeepFake Related). The interviewer asked technical questions that dug deep into the 
project’s workings. Since I had prepared thoroughly and knew the project inside out, I 
felt confident handling these questions. 
Key Takeaways: 
Apple interviewers are subject-matter experts who tailor their questions to your resume. This 
ensures discussions are meaningful and relevant, which I found incredibly refreshing. 
 
Interview Round 2: Techno-Managerial 
Round 2 was all about seeing if I clicked with Apple’s culture—how well I work with others, handle 
challenges, and adapt to new situations. 
 
Nature of Questions: 
• 
Situational challenges like: "How would you handle a disagreement with a teammate?" or 
"Can you share a moment when you were inspired by someone?" etc. 
• 
The questions were dynamic, and several were fired back-to-back, which kept the 
conversation lively. 
 
 
 
 
Key Takeaways: 
1. Be Honest: Just be yourself—honesty goes a long way. If you’re genuine, people can tell, and 
that makes all the difference. 
2. Show Confidence: Share your thoughts assertively, backed by clear examples from your 
work or projects. For instance, when discussing teamwork, I linked it to a group project I had 
worked on, showcasing how collaboration and adaptability helped us achieve success. 
3. Be Relatable: Even for general questions, I found ways to connect them to my professional 
or personal journey. For example, as a GenAI enthusiast, I shared how attending conferences 
in Chennai shaped my learning, exposing me to industry trends, Industry leaders and life 
lessons. The interviewers appreciated these stories, as they highlighted both technical and 
personal growth. 
The interviewers made the whole experience feel chill and engaging. By the end, I felt like I had made 
some great connections, which speaks volumes about Apple’s commitment to ensuring a positive and 
enriching candidate experience. 
 
Key Takeaways for Juniors 
1. Engage with Alumni: Proactively connect with seniors from various companies to gain 
diverse and valuable insights. Since Apple visited our campus for the first time, we were the 
fresh batch to experience their recruitment process, making external perspectives even more 
crucial for preparation. 
2. Master DSA: Trust me, a solid grasp of data structures and algorithms is a game-changer. 
Don’t skip the basics—they come up everywhere. 
3. Know Your Projects: Be well-versed in the technical details of your projects, as interviewers 
will test your depth of understanding. Deploying projects, when possible, adds practical 
credibility. 
4. Highlight Relevant Skills: Include only those skills on your resume that you are confident in 
discussing or demonstrating during interviews. 
5. Prepare Thoughtful Questions: At the end of interviews, ask insightful questions about 
Apple's culture, the team you might work with, or the values they uphold. It shows genuine 
interest and curiosity. 
6. Showcase Your Thinking: While solving problems during interviews, articulate your 
approach clearly. Even a brute force method is a good starting point before optimizing your 
solution. 
7. Understand Apple’s Vision: Research Apple’s core values and work culture. Be ready to 
discuss how your skills and experiences align with their mission. 
8. Maintain Academic Excellence: While practical skills are critical, a strong CGPA reflects 
your commitment and discipline, often acting as an additional advantage. 
9. Craft a Crisp Resume: A clean, focused resume with impactful projects and relevant skills 
can make a strong first impression. Avoid clutter or exaggeration. 
10. Iterative Problem Solving: Instead of feeling pressured to provide the most efficient solution 
upfront, focus on starting with a basic approach and iteratively improving it during 
discussions.

Interview Experience – RAMCO Systems 
Key Preparation Tips (Summary) 
●​ Prepare all standard problems from LeetCode, especially data structures such as 
linked lists, binary trees, stacks, and two-pointer/string problems.​
 
●​ Prefer Striver’s SDE Sheet for structured DSA preparation.​
 
●​ Understand all core Computer Science concepts including OS, DBMS, CN, OOPs, 
and SQL thoroughly.​
 
●​ Be confident and communicate clearly – strong articulation helps significantly during 
both technical rounds.​
 
●​ With proper preparation and confidence, the interview process is very crackable.​
 
 
Introduction 
This document summarizes my complete interview experience at RAMCO Systems, 
including all rounds, questions asked, and my overall performance. The entire selection process 
consisted of multiple filtering stages, including assessments, technical written tests, and two 
in-depth technical interviews conducted at CUIC. 
 
1. Online Assessment Round 
The process began with an online assessment that focused mainly on coding and basic 
aptitude.​
 I was required to solve two coding problems, both heavily centered on Linked Lists 
(operations, traversal, and edge-case handling). The round also included a set of MCQs related 
to programming fundamentals, computer science concepts, and logical reasoning. I performed 
well and progressed to the next stage. 
 
2. Offline Written Technical Test 
A total of 73 candidates were shortlisted for the Written Technical Test.​
 We were given three coding problems, and ample time to write complete solutions, including 
optimized logic and edge-case handling. 
Questions Asked 
1.​ Longest Non-Repeating Substring​
 
2.​ Finding All Duplicate Subtrees in a Binary Tree​
 
3.​ Longest Harmonic Subsequence​
 
This round tested our ability to write structured, clean, and optimized code. My solutions were 
well-received, and I was shortlisted for the first technical interview. 
 
3. Offline Technical Interview – Round 1 (CUIC) 
A total of 42 candidates advanced to Technical Round 1.​
 This round involved detailed analysis of the answers written in the previous coding test as well 
as conceptual depth in DSA and SQL. 
Questions & Topics Covered 
●​ Optimizing my written test solutions​
 
●​ Checking whether two binary trees are identical​
 
●​ Implementing a Queue using two Stacks​
 
●​ Detecting a loop in a linked list​
 
●​ Writing an SQL query using LIMIT and explaining execution order​
 
●​ A detailed discussion about all my projects:​
 
○​ Tech stacks used​
 
○​ Architecture​
 
○​ Challenges faced​
 
○​ Problem-solving approach​
 
●​ A short System Design task for a Vehicle Enterprise Management System​
 
●​ Conversation about extracurricular activities and leadership roles​
 
This round went very well, my explanations were strong, and I answered all the questions 
confidently. I expected to progress to the next round—and I did. 
 
4. Offline Technical Interview – Round 2 (CUIC) 
Only 20 candidates were shortlisted for this deeper technical evaluation.​
 This round primarily focused on project depth, CS fundamentals, and problem-solving 
skills. 
Topics Covered 
Project Deep-Dive 
They asked about: 
●​ Complete project architecture​
 
●​ Every tech stack I used​
 
●​ “Why” questions such as: Why MongoDB over PostgreSQL?​
 
●​ Data structures used in each module​
 
●​ Future scope and improvements​
 
●​ Real-world scalability considerations​
 
Coding and Problem-Solving 
1.​ A recursion program where I had to explain the output and derive time complexity​
 
2.​ A Binary String problem requiring pattern analysis​
 
Core CS Concepts 
A thorough discussion of: 
●​ OOPs Concepts (Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction, Encapsulation)​
 
●​ Operating Systems (deadlocks, scheduling, memory)​
 
●​ DBMS (ACID, indexing, joins)​
 
●​ Computer Networks (OSI layers, protocols)​
 
This round was challenging but went smoothly. I answered confidently and showcased a strong 
understanding of both my projects and CS fundamentals. 
 
Final Shortlist 
After all rounds, 10 candidates were finally selected for the final stage.​
 The interviewers emphasized confidence, problem-solving approach, and conceptual 
understanding as key differentiators.
