MORGAN STANLEY FTE INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
ABOUT THE COMPANY: 
Morgan Stanley operates as a global financial services company. It mobilizes capital to help 
government, corporations, institutions and individuals around the world achieve their financial 
goals. Morgan Stanley is consistently highly ranked in mergers and acquisitions and initial public 
offerings.  
 
Eligibility Criteria: CGPA - 8.0 and above 
Branches Considered: CS & IT 
CTC: 29.48 LPA 
Sequence of Selection: PPT & Online Assessment, 2 Technical Interviews and HR Interview. 
Job Role Offered: Technology Analyst 
 
ROUND 1 – ONLINE ASSESSMENT 
Duration: 90 minutes (about 1 and a half hours) 
The assessment was conducted through SHL platform. It consisted of three sections: Aptitude, 
Debugging and Coding.  
Aptitude – This section was of medium-hard difficulty level.  
Debugging – This section consisted of code snippets to rectify the logic. This was of easy-
medium difficulty level. Initially chosen programming language cannot be changed till the end. 
Coding – This section consisted of 3 coding questions. The three questions were easy. As the 
questions were easy, the time of submission played a greater role in selection. 
Every section was time bounded, that is a section cannot be backtracked once its time slice is 
over.  
After the online assessment, 14 members were shortlisted for the next round – Technical 
Interview I. 
ROUND 2 – TECHNICAL INTERVIEW I 
Mode: Physical 
Duration: 1 hour 
Difficulty level: Easy-Medium 
The interview was based on OS, SQL, DSA, OOPS. It almost covered all major CS domains. The 
interviewer was friendly and he made me feel comfortable so that my anxiety and nervousness 
came down. The interviewer started off with some casual questions like where do I come from? 
And then, he asked me what is the eligibility criterion to enter Anna University? Then he started 
asking technical questions. Below are the questions asked: 
I was asked to debug a code snippet which was written in Cpp . It was a code snippet which 
included dynamic memory allocation using new pointer, array declaration and stuff like that. I 
was able to debug it completely.  
What is the use of header files? Why do we use it? 
Question on cpp library - vector.h. (I am good at Java language. So, I said to the interviewer that 
I am good and comfortable with Java. So, he started asking Java questions.) 
Questions on OOPS: 
Define an abstract class. Write the code to illustrate an example of an abstract class. 
What are the access specifiers available in Java Programming language? 
Questions on OS: 
What are threads? 
What is a deadlock? 
What are the necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur? 
Questions on SQL: 
Write a query to create a table. 
Write a query to insert data into a table. 
What is left outer join, inner join and cross join? 
Coding questions: 
What is time complexity? Write the time complexity classes in ascending order. 
Write any sorting algorithm. Explain the working of it. Write down the best-case time 
complexity for it.  
What is the difference between a run-time error and a compile time-error? 
Given four persons at designations – D1, D2, D3, D4, respectively where D1 is the least 
designation and D4 is the highest. The person can skip one position and get promoted to the 
second next higher position or he can get promoted to the next higher position. Write down 
the possible ways to reach D4. This question is similar to this problem. STAIRCASE PROBLEM - 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/count-ways-reach-nth-stair/ 
Given login time, logout time for n employees in a company. The task is to find the minimum 
number of chairs in a company to accommodate ‘n’ employees. This question is like this 
problem – MINIMUM NUMBER OF PLATFORMS REQUIRED FOR A RAILWAY/BUS STATION - 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/minimum-number-platforms-required-railwaybus-station/ 
Write a program to find if a string is palindrome or not. 
For the coding questions, I was asked to write the logic. 
I was able to answer all the questions. The most important thing is to communicate with the 
interviewer throughout the interview session. Even if you get stuck up thinking logic, the 
interviewer gives you hint to crack it. So, communicate your thoughts throughout. Do not 
hesitate to do that assuming that you are wrong. Be bold and confident with your answers. 
Shortlisting was done for the next round. 
ROUND 3 – TECHNICAL INTERVIEW II 
Mode: Physical 
Duration: 45 minutes 
Difficulty level: Easy 
The interviewer started off asking me to give a self intro. This round was on System Design. He 
gave me a scenario to design a system. I was asked to design a vending system which is used in 
malls.  
Functionalities required: 
• Display the items available. 
• Receive the order. 
• Check the inventory. 
• Check for successful payment and deliver the products. 
• Update the inventory. 
I was asked to draw the class diagram for the system proposed. Usage of appropriate data 
structures in some of the functionalities were asked. I made corrections as per his views. He 
asked me to draw the database tables required for this system. I included a third-party Payment 
Processing system in my diagram. So, he asked me to draw a class diagram which focuses on 
the Payment modes – UPI, Cash and Card. He asked me to explain the OOPS concept involved in 
that diagram. Some OS questions which were asked: 
What is multithreading? What are its benefits? What are the ways in which multithreading can 
be established using Java? 
Write a code snippet which illustrates the principle of Mutual Exclusion. 
Which is the keyword in Java used to ensure mutual exclusion?  
Given two tables – Students information table and Company selects table. Write a SQL query to 
find the name of the student and the company name where he got placed who belongs to XYZ 
college and receives the highest salary offered in the city Mumbai. 
Questions were asked on the tech stack mentioned in the projects.  
Finally, I was asked to ask a question to the interviewer. I asked him about the work culture at 
Morgan and being a FinTech company, how does it differ from the other product-based 
companies. 
I found him impressed with my answers and had a hope that I would get selected for the next 
round. Shortlisting was done for the final round. 
ROUND 4 – HR INTERVIEW 
Mode: Physical 
Duration: 25 minutes 
This round seemed little harder than the previous rounds. The interviewer greeted me and 
started asking the questions. The questions were like: 
How many projects have you worked on? 
What are the difficulties that you have faced while working on projects? 
The requirements of the project changed in the middle while your project was half way 
completed. How would handle that circumstance? 
Tell me about the toughest decision that you have taken while working on projects. 
Assume that you are the team lead and your teammate is not very co-operative. He is very 
lethargic and does not submit the assigned work within the deadline. What will be your action 
towards him as a team lead? 
Assume that you are proposing a solution to a problem while the other person is proposing an 
efficient one compared to yours. Will you give up on your idea or stand by yours? 
Tell me your experience when you have taken risks while working on projects and the results 
occurred from your risks were not as you expected. 
I found this round a very formal discussion and it was a bit tough. Going through sample HR 
questions and a light preparation, rather than on spot answering will help you ace the HR 
interview. 
3 of us were selected as FTE at Morgan Stanley. 
Tips: 
Strictly restrict your resume to 1 page. List out honestly only the skills what you possess in your 
resume. 
Think aloud. Express your views and ideas. 
Be strong in OOPS concepts, DBMS, OS, DSA.

1

3 
123343

 It includes conceptual questions, finding missing terms, finding the correct

WELLS FARGO INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
(INTERNSHIP) 
 
Role: 
 
Software Engineer Intern 
 
About the company: 
 
Wells Fargo & Company is an American multinational financial services 
company with a significant global presence. The company operates in 35 
countries and serves over 70 million customers worldwide. Today, Wells Fargo 
India operates in Hyderabad, Bengaluru, and Chennai. It is considered one of the 
"Big Four Banks" in the United States, alongside JPMorgan Chase, Bank of 
America, and Citigroup. 
 
Online test : 
     
The online test was conducted on the SHL platform.  It was a proctored test 
and we were asked to attend  the test in IST dept labs. The test consisted of four 
sections. 
The first section consisted of verbal questions. It had twelve questions which 
had to be completed within 15 minutes which could be answered pretty easily if 
you had basic English knowledge. You can’t move between sections. 
     
 The second section consisted of 14 business aptitude questions with a time 
limit of 25 minutes. Questions were based on stocks and shares, data 
interpretations based on pie charts, bar charts, graphs. You should be able to solve 
those easily with basic math. Just don’t spend too much time on a question and try 
to complete all the questions. There is no negative marking and you can’t navigate 
between questions of a section.  
     
The third round consisted of two easy to medium level coding questions with 
a time limit of 40 minutes. In this section you can navigate between questions. 
Driver 
code 
will 
be 
given. 
The 
first 
question 
is  
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/area-of-intersection-of-two-circles/ . You have to 
return the answer rounded up to six decimals. 
The second question was simple. You have to return a lexicographically sorted list 
of repeated words in a given string. You could see how many test cases your code 
has passed. It was clearly stated that they are expecting a solution which passes 
all the test cases rather than an efficient code which is partially passing the test 
cases. 
      
The fourth section consisted of one question which has to be solved in 10 
minutes. The question was based on Königsberg's seven bridges. I was only able 
to solve 1 of the 3 coding questions. More weightage was given to aptitude and 
verbal than coding questions. You can solve the coding problems in the language 
of your choice. 
22 students got selected for the interview. 
 
Round 1 : 
      
The round started with a standard tell me about yourself question. The 
interviewer asked me to design a database and write a select query with joins. Also 
asked me to write any sorting of my choice other than bubble sort and merge sort 
(because most of the people before me have chosen those 2 algos). I chose 
quicksort, visualized how it works to the deepest and wrote pseudocode for it. It’s 
okay if you don’t know the answers for some questions. At last, the interviewer 
asked me if I had any questions for him. Be prepared with some questions. Most 
of the candidates who passed this round were selected. 
 
Round 2: 
      My experience of this round will be different from my peers. They had 
technical as well as questions from my resume. This round was completely me 
asking questions to him. This was the best round of experience that I had in the 
interview. We were just sharing our thoughts and brainstorming about conducting 
hackathons (because I mentioned that I have participated in 2 All-India level 
hackathons). He even asked me to have some biscuits that were given to him 
  . In this round he was just observing how curious I was.  
 
Round 3: 
      This was a typical HR round. During the interview, I was asked to introduce 
myself. I mentioned my strengths, technical interests, club responsibilities, and 
personal interests. The interviewer asked me why I want to intern at Wells Fargo. 
You just need to let them know that you are a good team player. 
After this, 12 candidates were selected out of the initial 22 for internship. 
 
TIPS: 
● Be prepared with the DSA concepts. 
● Don’t panic at any point. 
● Being a bit good in communication would really help you in your interview. 
● I was not selected in my very first interview, so try until you get it right.

ONLINE ASSESSMENT:

of an array and compare them. If the 2nd last element was greater than push

This round was purely based on ranking on the leaderboard on HackerRank. It was also

TECHNICAL INTERVIEW:

HR INTERVIEW(TECHNICAL ROUND 2):  
 
Duration: 60 min 
 
Out of the 13 people only 5 got selected for the next round.  
 
In Vegrow there were just 2 technical rounds and no HR round. I initially thought it was 
for 20 min(based on seniors' experience) and was just browsing through my projects 
and practising some pre-defined answers. I was the first one to go into the panel so I 
really wasn’t expecting any questions, but however these were the questions asked to 
me:  
1.​ Tell me about yourself 
2.​ Tell me about a project in my resume(They choose the project to talk about)

and what model I used for NLP and how does it work 
6.​ After that, they went into System Designing - which I had no clue about and didn’t

like Netflix look like. Some example questions were:  
●​ If the video buffer time needs to be faster what would I use  
●​ If too many people access the website what should I do 
●​ The login API’s  
●​ Hosting mechanism - where I explained about docker and EC2 instances.  
 
  ​
Some concepts on how to improve system efficiency was learnt from the AWS 
course that I completed during my summer holidays. So I was able to answer to these 
questions  
7.​ Then the went into DSA round were I asked:  
●​ Climbing Stairs - All the methods including space optimisation  
●​ LCA of a binary tree - which I wasn’t able to do the entire solution but they 
encouraged me to do so and told me I was on the right track.  
8.​ They ended off with a simple DBMS concept based question on shared locking 
and unlocking 
 
Out of 5, 2 got selected for the Internship.  
 
 
 
TIPS:  
●​ Start your preparation as soon as possible on leetcode or any other platform.  
●​ If you don’t have time, go through the leetcode blind 75 questions and once you 
are done with that make sure to solve some extra questions based on the same 
pattern - doing this you will get an idea on how each question is being 
approached.  
●​ If you are python developer I would strongly suggest you to go through neetcode 
- NEETCODE QUESTIONS and solve as many questions as possible  
●​ If you need a quick intro on how each concept works and some basic questions 
to get you started then go with Youtuber - Greg Hogg. 
●​ If you want to have good knowledge about DSA as a whole you can refer to the 
STRIVER DSA COURSE. I’m telling you, he is the one who is going to save your 
recursive, Dynamic Programming and Greedy. So don’t forget to check this out.  
●​  Building a clean and presentable resume is very important so take time to build 
a good resume. You can trust AI to get some catchy phrases, but do not use AI to 
build your entire resume. It’s ok to allocate time and build your relationship with 
your resume.  
●​ A day before your interview, learn one good project thoroughly and make sure 
you can answer any question about that one good project and keep answers 
ready for known questions like ‘Tell me about yourself’, ‘Why did you choose this 
role?’, etc. 
●​ The most important part is to be confident in your interview. If you don’t feel 
confident, prepare and practice for interviews with as much importance as you 
prepare for DSA.  
 
Keep pushing yourself as much as you can for the next 3-4 months of your internship 
drive and always maintain a calm nature during your preparation and interviews.

HSBC Interview Experience 
 
About HSBC: 
HSBC (Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corporation) is one of the world’s largest banking and 
financial services organizations, founded in 1865. It operates in over 60 countries, serving millions of 
customers through retail, commercial, and investment banking. It is the largest Europe-based bank by 
total assets, and the 3rd largest non-state owned bank in the world. HSBC is known for its global 
reach, strong digital transformation initiatives, and focus on sustainable finance. With its headquarters 
in London, it plays a significant role in bridging Eastern and Western markets. 
 
Role offered : Trainee Software Engineer 
 
Selection Process: Simulate, Online coding Assessment, Technical Interview, HR Interview. 
The Selection Process: 
 
370 candidates applied for the role and 295 people were eligible to appear for the Online 
Assessments. 
Note : 75 candidates were excluded since they hadn’t registered in HSBC website or provided the 
wrong mail-id for registration. We were instructed to provide our college mail id for registration. So, 
make sure you register using the specified mail-id. There wasn’t any resume-based shortlisting 
process. 
1. Simulate Assessment: 
  
The Simulate assessment was conducted on 2nd September 2025 at IST department lab and it 
was the first filtering round in HSBC’s selection process. It was more like a personality assessment, 
where candidates are tested on how well they adapt to the work culture of HSBC. The round’s 
duration is 70 minutes. The round consisted of many sections including 
 
Situational Judgement Questions. 
 
Personality Questions. 
 
Formal / Informal Questions. 
 
Aptitude Questions. 
 
Basic English Questions. 
 
The results were declared after a few minutes and out of the 295 candidates, 9 candidates were 
selected for the next round. 
Tip: The simulate assessment is the most crucial part of the filtering process at HSBC than the coding 
round. There were probably 8 sections, and I would recommend to give your best in at least 3 or 4 
sections especially the numerical aptitude section, the English section and the section in which 
candidates were given a scenario and 5 actions or responses. Based on the scenario, the candidate 
must rank the 5 actions. Also, We were allowed to use scientific calculator for the numerical based 
questions. I would also recommend referring the following links, so that you can prepare yourself for 
the Simulate assessment: 
 
How I would prepare for the HSBC Simulate Assessment 
 
HSBC Practice Zone 
2. Coding Assessment: 
 
The second round was conducted on the same day, right after the first round and it was a 
comparatively easy assessment. The assessment took place on Codility platform and candidates were 
tested on 3 questions of easy to medium difficulty. 
1. Find the largest 2 digit substring : 
Given a string consisting of numbers, return the substring with largest 2 digit number. 
Examples: 
1. S = “12345” return 45. 
2. S = “79361” return 93. 
3. S = “00009” return 9. 
 
2. Dictionary usage :  
Given 2 strings dictionary and message, modify message such that if a word in message is not 
present in dictionary, replace the word with “###”, else use the same word. 
 
 
Example: 
1. Dictionary = “Footballer Ronaldo best Messi the earth in leo” 
Message = “Leo messi is the best footballer in the world” 
Return “Leo messi ### the best footballer in the ###” 
      3.    Latest Date Possible: 
Given a string of  5 characters and the first 2 characters represent the month of a year and the 
last 2 characters represent the date. The string might contain the character “?” in those 4 
positions. Return the maximum date that is possible by replacing the “?”, while also ensuring 
the date is logically correct. If a valid date isn’t possible, return “xx-xx”. 
 
Example: 
1. Date = “1?-31” return “12-31” 
Here the “?” mark can be replaced by 0,1,2. If you replace it with , the string 
becomes “11-31” but that is invalid, since the month November(11) has only 30 
days. So, the options now available are 0 and 2. Here, Even though replacing with 
0 is a valid argument, One should select 2, since we need to return the latest valid 
date. 
 
2. Date = “31-04” return “xx-xx” 
Here “04” represents the month April, which has only 30 days, and the date 
represents “31”, which isn’t correct. hence the result must be “xx-xx”. 
3. Date = “0?-?9” return “09-29” 
I was able to solve the first 2 problems completely and partially solve the 3rd question. The results 
were declared on 10th September 2025. Out of the 9 candidates, 8 were shortlisted for the interview 
process and fortunately, I was one of them. 
Interview Process 
The interview was scheduled on 18th September 2025 at SSN College of Engineering. The interview 
consisted of 2 rounds – a technical round and a HR round. Almost 80 candidates from 4 colleges 
attended the interview process. 
3. Technical Interview : 
 
There were 10 interview panels and all the candidates from Anna university were interviewed 
in the first panel. The questions were fully based on resume and hence the questions for each 
candidate were different. The interview started with an introduction about the interviewer and he also 
asked me to introduce myself. I clearly expressed my strong interest in problem-solving, and 
mentioned I have solved over 600+ problems on geeksforgeeks and leetcode combined. This was 
important because the interviewer was very much interested in assessing how well-versed you are in 
your specific area of interest. He asked me about my favorite programming language and I mentioned 
Python. Then, He asked me to solve one DSA question: 
 
Validate Parenthesis in a string containing letters, symbols and parenthesis 
At first, I jumped straight into writing the solution. I would recommend not to jump right in 
and to first explain your approach to the interviewer thoroughly and proceed on writing your 
solution.  
He first asked me to explain my approach and I also asked some questions so that I could get 
better clarity regarding the question, this indirectly made a good impression about me for the 
interviewer and he instructed me to write down the solution in python. I wrote down my 
approach of using a stack (a list in python) and showed him a dry-run of my approach. He 
added some edge-cases and tested whether my approach would work well for those too. I was 
very confident about my approach and explained that it would satisfy all the test-cases. 
He was satisfied and started questioning about other topics. 
Other questions asked: 
1. How do you handle exceptions in python? 
2. Explain me about one of your project. 
3. Explain about the google - drive project.(I mentioned that project in my resume) 
4. What was your role in all these project? 
I explained the projects briefly and mentioned I worked on the frontend part in all the projects. The 
round concluded after I asked some questions regarding HSBC. 
My Questions: 
1. What’s the best way to develop knowledge and skill in finance? 
  
He mentioned that HSBC being a fintech organization, an employee would get a lot 
of exposure on cyber security and authentication systems. He also added, working on various 
projects will automatically result in developing the skill-set that one should require to grow in 
their professional career. 
Since he mentioned cyber security, I framed my next question based on cyber security. 
2. What are some resources that I can refer to gain knowledge in cyber security domain? 
He instructed me to read some articles and mentioned some forums and communities 
that I can refer to know more about it. 
       These questions show that you are genuinely interested in joining the organization. This is one 
important aspect I would recommend to everyone. 
The results were declared after 2 hours and Overall 21 people were shortlisted for the final round. 
4. HR Interview: 
 
Fortunately and unfortunately, I was the first one to be called for the HR round. The 
interviewer congratulated me for entering the final round and asked how I feel at that moment. We 
had a normal conversation and officially the round started after sometime. This round was more like a 
rapid fire round. Questions were immediately fired once you end your response for the current 
question. The round was conducted for only 3 to 5 minutes, and the questions included: 
1. What’s your CGPA? 
2. Are you willing to relocate to Pune? 
3. What’s your favorite subject in Computer Science? 
4. Where do you see yourself in 5 years? 
5. Are you planning on doing masters? 
6. What are your strengths? 
7. Have you ever worked in pressure situations on meeting the deadlines? 
8. What are 3 areas or aspects that you would like to improve on yourself? 
Even though these are typical HR questions, the way he asked made it a fast-paced interview and I 
answered all questions with my best effort. The interviewer also assessed my fluency in answering the 
questions and Even though, I was able to answer well, I struggled with last question and the 
interviewer calmed me and advised me to stay cool. The interview was hence concluded. 
The results were announced after an hour and I was extremely elated to know that I was one among 
the 18 people selected and the only one selected from Anna University. 
 
 
PREPARATION TIPS: 
I chose Python for solving DSA problems. But feel free to choose a language, you are comfortable 
with. And for core concepts preparation, I would recommend the following:

os-interviews-sde-core-sheet/ 
Be confident on what you mention in your resume. Try to completely understand the question and 
come up with a solution. It’s completely normal to get stuck in a question, feel free to admit it to the 
interviewer and move on to the next question. Try to score better in the next question. Try to 
showcase your strengths in your self introduction, since it can set-up a perfect foundation and helps 
guide the interview in a way, where you could give your best performance. Try to be more articulate 
(think out loud) and express things so that the interviewer can better understand your thought-process.

Amazon Interview Experience 
 
Amazon is an American multinational technology company focusing on e-commerce, cloud 
computing, online advertising, digital streaming, and artificial intelligence. It is considered one 
of the Big Five American technology companies, alongside Alphabet (parent company of 
Google), Apple, Meta (parent company of Facebook), and Microsoft. It is a product-based 
company and one of the famous MAANG companies. 
  
Role Offered                   -            SDE – 6 months Intern (placement based on performance) 
Stipend                           -            1,10,000 + 400 USD / Month 
Number of Rounds       -            2 (Both Online). 
  
The Assessment pattern may change for every year. This year it was only coding round and 
personality questions whereas they would also add debugging, Aptitude as well. 
This year the selection was based on two rounds: one online coding round and an interview. 
  
Round 1: (ONLINE ASSESSMENT) 
It consists of two phases: 
• 
 Coding. 
• 
Personality questions. 
The online assessment consists of two coding questions, which have to be done in 70 minutes. 
This round was held in HackerRank platform. 
  
Coding Question 1:  
This question was based on regex pattern. 
A regex pattern consists of lowercase English letters and some wildcard characters (“?”) with 
any English character. Two patterns are called intersecting if the wildcard characters in the 
respective string can be changed in a way to form the same string from both patterns. 
  
For Example: pattern p=”?cc?” and q = “dc??” intersect as we can form s = “dccx” from both 
p and q. 
  
Given an array pattern of N regex strings, find the minimum number of “?” characters possible 
in a pattern that intersects with all the patterns. 
  
Sample Input: 
Given n = 2, m = 10, patterns = [“ha??rrank”, ”?a?ke?bank”] 
Sample output: 
So here the minimum number of wildcard characters that we need to generalize this array is 
1. 
  
Coding Question 2: 
Problem Statement 
You are given: 
• 
An integer array of parcels, where each element represents the number of parcels 
each agent currently has. 
• 
An integer extra_parcels representing the total number of extra parcels that need to 
be distributed among the agents. 
Distribute the extra parcels such that the maximum number of parcels any agent has 
after distribution is minimized. You need to return this minimized maximum number. 
Example: 
Input: 
parcels = [7, 5, 1, 9, 1] 
extra_parcels = 25 
  
Output: 
10  
  
Explanation: 
One possible way to distribute the parcels is: 
- Agent 1: 7 parcels + 3 extra = 10 parcels 
- Agent 2: 5 parcels + 4 extra = 9 parcels 
-Agent 3: 1 parcel + 9 extra = 10 parcels 
- Agent 4: 9 parcels + 1 extra = 10 parcels 
- Agent 5: 1 parcel + 8 extra = 9 parcels 
  
There could be other distributions, but the minimum possible maximum number of parcels is 
10. 
After the coding round you will be asked to answer some personality-based questions there 
will be no marking for this round and there Is no time constraints too. In this round the same 
questions may be repeated so ensure that you are thoroughly reading the questions and 
answer the same for the repeated questions. This will evaluate your personality and how you 
are thinking to solve some scenarios. 
Based on this online assessment, around 85 people were shortlisted for the interview. 
  
Round 2: (TECHNICAL INTERVIEW) 
Mode: ONLINE. 
Before the interview, you will be allotted a slot (timing for the interview), and the link for the 
meet and LiveCode link will be shared via email once you are registered on the Amazon 
careers. 
The interview was conducted in Amazon’s Chime platform. 
For me there was two panels. 
The interview started with the introduction of the interviewers and then I was asked to 
introduce myself. Suggestion: (Keep your introduction short and crips since the interview 
session will be 1 hour and the interviewer will have more time on questions.) And also, there 
will be no questions based on the resume and your soft skills ensure that you add those points 
when you introduce yourself). 
Then you would be asked to share the screen and open the LiveCode session link you have 
received on your email. 
  
The interviewer asked how strong I am in Heap data structure and asked me some questions 
on HEAP. 
1. What is Heap? 
2. Types of Heaps? 
3. How heap maintain its structure? 
4. How heapify will happen if an element is deleted? 
5. Time complexity for Heapify. 
6. How you derive the time complexity for Heapify? 
7. Given one tree and asked whether it is a heap or not? 
  
After answering all questions then he started with some questions on Tree 
1. What is a binary tree? 
2. Difference between a complete and full binary tree? 
3. Asked about the time complexity for the traversal? 
4. Given a number of nodes find the height for both complete and full binary tree? 
5. Asked about how you derive the formula for finding the height of the tree. 
  
I answered all questions, and the interviewer was satisfied with my answers. 
Then he moved on to the coding question. 
  
Question Asked: 
Step-by-Step Directions from a Binary Tree Node to Another 
You are given the root of a binary tree with n nodes. Each node is uniquely assigned a value 
from 1 to n. You are also given an integer startValue representing the value of the start node s 
and a different integer destValue representing the value of the destination node t. 
Find the shortest path starting from node s and ending at node t. Generate step-by-step 
directions of such path as a string consisting of only the uppercase letters 'L', 'R', and 'U'. Each 
letter indicates a specific direction: 
  
Firstly, I was asked to explain my intuition and to code the solution. 
Since there is no brute force approach for this problem, I directly gave the solution and coded 
the same. 
  
After coding the solution for this question, the interviewer asked for some test cases since the 
I was coded for all edge cases in the first attempt, I can answer the questions very easily and 
the interviewer was satisfied with my solution. 
Then asked for the time and space complexity. 
Then some questions on the basics of programming from my code since I wrote the code in 
CPP, he asked a few questions like 
1. Why do you pass the argument as a reference? 
2. Why not pointer, why reference? 
3. Since I used some built-in functions, I was asked how this function will work and 
explain the pseudocode for the same. 
The interview ended with “is there any question for me”. This is the first time in an interview 
I asked no question at the end I don’t know why? But the interviewer seemed happy. 
But make sure you ask some questions at the end. 
 
RESULT: 
There were fifteen people offered six-month internships out of eighty-five, and I was one of 
them. 
 
Suggestions: 
• 
Amazon’s interview is different from all interviews they don’t ask any questions from 
your resume. They mainly focus on your thought process for solving the problem. And 
make the interview like a discussion explain all the things that you are thinking of the 
question. Use the whiteboard that was shared with you to make the explanation clear. 
You can use paper and pen but yet make sure you use that whiteboard. 
• 
Please go through Amazon’s leadership principles before the interview. Not only for 
Amazon, but for all the companies, do the research before the interview. 
General Suggestion: 
• 
Start your coding journey earliest and practice problems on a daily basis. Prefer quality 
over quantity numbers doesn’t matter. 
• 
Don’t feel bad if the result doesn’t go on your way. Be confident and try until you 
succeed. 
• 
Get strong fundamentals in DSA, OOPS, DBMS, OS, and CN and brush up some system 
design concepts. 
• 
After the online assessment you may not have enough time to prepare for core 
concepts so be ready beforehand.

Wells Fargo Interview Experience  
Wells Fargo is an American multinational financial services company that offers a wide range 
of banking, investment, and mortgage products and services.  
Role Offered: Technology Program Analyst  
Sequence of Selection:  
1. Online Test 
2. Technical Interview (Virtual) 
3. Technical + HR Interview (Offline) 
4. HR Interview (Offline) 
 
1. Online Test:  
1. Coding Question – Level Medium - Minimum Length of Anagram Concatenation  
2. Coding Question – Level Easy - Count Vowel Substrings of a String  
3. SQL Question – Level Medium - Given 2 tables users and transactions, find the total 
transactions and total amount of users for the year 2023. 
Ans: select u.email, count(t.id) as total_transactions, round(sum(t.amount), 2) as 
total_amount from users u join transactions t on u.id = t.user_id where t.dt like '2023%' 
group by u.id, u.email order by u.email;  
The test was conducted on the HackerRank platform with a duration of 60 minutes. I completed 
it in 30 minutes since the questions were fairly easy and standard. 
The results were declared after 4 days, and 20 candidates were shortlisted. I was not part of that 
list. However, on the day of the interview, an additional list of 6 candidates was released, and 
I was included in it. 
 
2. Technical Interview (Virtual):  
The first round was a virtual interview conducted on Microsoft Teams. It began with a self-
introduction. The interviewer asked me which programming language I was most proficient in, 
and I said Python. The questions were related to general Python concepts, including decorators, 
lists, sets, loops, and OOP concepts. 
 There were also basic questions from Operating Systems (deadlocks), Computer Networks 
(DNS), and DBMS (an SQL query for the second most frequent item in a table). 
One question was about how to handle too many requests to an API. I attempted it but didn’t 
know the exact answer, so I admitted it. The interviewer then explained the solution. She also 
asked about my internship experience at IIT Madras as a research intern in cryptography. 
At the end of the interview, I asked her about her transition from her previous company to Wells 
Fargo, and she explained it clearly. The results were announced after 10 minutes, and I was 
selected. 
 
3. Technical + HR Interview (Offline):  
Although it was described as a technical + HR interview, only technical questions were asked, 
except for one: “Why Wells Fargo?” 
The interviewer wrote the questions on paper and handed it to me to answer. It began with a 
DSA application question on which data structure to use and how to approach it. I initially 
suggested a data structure with higher time complexity and then moved to a more efficient one. 
The interviewer also guided me toward the most effective data structure. 
Next, a question on time complexity was asked. I gave the correct answer at first, but when the 
interviewer said it was incorrect, I changed my answer. Later, he mentioned that my first answer 
was actually correct, so I explained why I had changed it. 
After that, there were questions on strings, lists, the range function, and generators in Python. 
Towards the end, I asked him about how code migrations to Java take place at Wells Fargo, and 
he explained it in detail. The results were announced immediately, and I was selected. 
 
4. HR Interview (Offline):  
The interview started with a self-introduction, followed by questions from my resume. I was 
asked to explain in detail about my internship on cryptography, which focused on verifiable 
SQL queries on outsourced databases. I was also asked about one of my projects, where I 
explained it clearly along with the tools that were used. 
Next, there were questions on Java vs. Python, where I gave a general comparison of both. The 
interviewer then asked whether Java is a completely object-oriented language. Questions also 
covered strings in Java, their immutability, and mutable alternatives such as StringBuffer and 
StringBuilder. Further, I was asked about OOP concepts like inheritance and polymorphism. 
At last, I asked him if he had any advice for me. He said that I had done well. I was the last 
candidate for the interview, and the results were announced after 10 minutes. I was delighted 
to be selected for the role. 
 
Be ready with your self-introduction, internship experience (if any), and project details. 
Communicate with clarity. Even if you don’t know the answer, stay calm, as the interviewer 
will help guide you to the right solution. Wishing you all the best!

SCHNEIDER ELECTRIC 
ABOUT THE COMPANY 
Schneider Electric is a global leader in digital automation and energy management solutions. 
It focuses on electrification, industrial automation, and digitilization, helping industries, 
buildings, and homes operate more efficiently. The company is well known for its 
commitment to sustainability and has received multiple awards in this area. Schneider 
Electric operates worldwide and hires across departments for multiple roles. 
I was shortlisted for the Electrical & Automation (E&A) role. 
SELECTION PROCESS 
1. Online Assessment 
o The assessment was a mix of technical MCQs and a gaming/behavioral test. 
o Technical MCQs:  
Domain-based questions. For Computer Science students, most of the questions were 
from Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA), Operating Systems and Computer 
Networks. Difficulty was of medium level. 
o Gaming/Behavioral Section:  
This stage lasted about 1.5–2 hours and was designed to evaluate both cognitive 
skills and behavioral traits through interactive tasks. It had two components; 
1. Puzzle-Like Games (Time-Bound) 
• Required fast thinking and quick responses. 
• Tested problem-solving, reasoning, decision-making, adaptability, and 
persistence. 
2. Situational Behavioral Questions (~100 questions) 
• Scenario-based personality assessment. 
• Measured teamwork, leadership, stress handling, consistency, and 
adaptability. 
 
 
o Around 120 students were shortlisted across all departments. For the E&A role, 
specifically, 70 students were shortlisted. 
Following this, candidates were called for interviews either in-person or virtual. 
2. Technical Interview 
My technical interview was conducted virtually with a panel of two interviewers. 
o The session began with general questions like 'What do you know about Schneider 
Electric?' and a quick check on my willingness to relocate since they hired only for 
Mysore location. 
o I gave a brief self-introduction, after which they moved to my projects. 
o Since I had developed an Electricity Bill Management System, which aligned closely 
with Schneider’s domain, they were highly interested. 
o For about 20 minutes, I was questioned on various aspects of the project: 
  • Workflow and core functionalities. 
  • The database tables used in the project. 
  • Real-world applications of the system. 
  • Possible extensions or improvements. 
o After the project, they tested my basics from the core subjects — DBMS, DSA and 
Computer Networks. 
o All questions were fundamental level, focusing more on understanding than 
difficulty. 
o The interview tone remained conversational, and I felt they valued clarity and 
relevance over complex answers. 
o At the end of my technical interview, the second panelist asked me some light and 
funny questions to test my spontaneity, such as: 
- 'What was your favorite subject in school?' 
- 'Why did you choose engineering?' (to which I honestly replied that I did not prefer 
medical and hence chose engineering). 
I felt they appreciated my honest and open answers. 
 
 
3. HR Interview 
      The next day, I had a dedicated HR round (~20 minutes): 
o Began with a self-introduction. 
o The HR explained details about the role, responsibilities, and work location. 
o I was given space to clarify queries, making it more of a two-way discussion than a 
Q&A. 
At the end of the selection process, a total of 19 candidates were shortlisted and 4 were 
from the CSE. The offer included a 6-month internship followed by FTE. 
 
KEY TAKEAWAYS 
- Be Honest: I believe my honesty throughout the interview made a strong impression. If I 
didn’t know an answer, I said so, rather than pretending. 
- Know Your Projects: Be ready to explain your projects in detail — they can drive most of 
the interview discussion. 
- Stay Calm in Casual Questions: Even when interviewers get informal, be yourself. It 
shows your personality beyond academics. 
- Understand the Company: Read about their focus areas (in Schneider’s case: 
sustainability, digital automation, energy management). 
 
I strongly believe that authenticity and clarity can take you a long way in interviews. 
Efforts get paid always. Stay CONSISTENT. 
ALL THE BEST!

NATWEST INTERN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
ABOUT NATWEST : 
NatWest Group plc, headquartered in Edinburgh, Scotland, is one of the leading banking and financial 
services organizations in the United Kingdom. Established in 1968 through the merger of National 
Provincial Bank and Westminster Bank, NatWest has grown to offer a wide range of services including 
retail banking, commercial and business banking, private banking, and wealth management. Known 
for its strong digital presence and customer-focused approach, the bank provides innovative mobile 
and online solutions while also emphasizing sustainability by committing to achieve net zero climate 
impact by 2050. With its long history, strong brand recognition, and dedication to responsible banking, 
NatWest plays a major role in the UK’s financial sector. 
 
POSITION : Software Engineering Intern 
 
SELECTION PROCESS : 
• 
Step 1 : Qualify preliminary criteria 
• 
Step 2 : Online Test 
• 
Step 3 : Competency based Interview 
 
1. PRELIMINARY CRITERIA 
• 
Degree : BTech ( Intern) 
• 
Branches eligible : Computer Science, Information Technology, Electronics & Communication 
Engineering, Electrical & Electronics, Mathematics & Computing, Mathematics & 
instrumentation 
• 
Xth : minimum 70% 
• 
XIIth : minimum 70% 
• 
Grad : minimum 65% aggregate as per the recent mark sheet / semester. 
 
2. ONLINE ASSESMENT 
 
It consists of the following sections: 
1. Verbal Aptitude (15 mins) 
This section includes reading comprehension, identifying the correct tense form, and 
synonyms and antonyms. If you are good at grammar, you can clear this with ease. 
2. General Aptitude (30–40 mins) 
This section covers: 
• 
Representing given data in a pie chart 
• 
Pattern finding 
• 
Logical thinking 
• 
Calendar scheduling 
• 
Profit and loss problems 
• 
Percentages 
The interface showed the number of questions solved and unsolved in one corner for this 
section, but I wasn’t focusing on it. Because of this, I wasn’t sure how many questions I had 
attempted or left unanswered, and I couldn’t complete all of them within the given time. 
PS: Don’t forget to carry a calculator. 
3. Technical MCQs (15 mins) 
This section mainly focuses on SQL and Database concepts. Some questions were based on 
choosing the correct query, while others tested SQL fundamentals. If you are thorough with 
SQL basics, this part will be manageable. 
4. Coding Questions (30 mins) 
I was given two simple scenario-based questions on strings: 
• 
Finding the count of consonants 
• 
Returning a string with no vowels 
Basic coding knowledge is enough to crack this section. 
I believe the General Aptitude section carried more weightage in this round, as many of us 
performed on a similar level in the other three sections. 
From this round, 23 of us were shortlisted for the interview. 
 
3. TECHINICAL AND HR INTERVIEW : (25-30 mins) 
The interview was scheduled the next day after the announcement of shortlisted candidates. We first 
attended the PPT session, which was followed by the interview. The panel consisted of two 
interviewers, and the discussion mainly revolved around my resume. Both interviewers had a copy of 
it. 
Introduction 
They began by asking if I would like to introduce myself. Never miss this opportunity if given, because 
you can guide the interviewers about yourself. A short and crisp introduction that defines you always 
makes a strong impression. 
Projects 
They started with my projects and asked me to explain one of the projects mentioned in my resume. 
Technical Questions 
Before diving into the details, I had mentioned the following technical skills in my resume: 
1. Programming Language – Java 
2. Database Management – SQL 
3. Core Concepts – Data Structures and Algorithms 
They asked me how strong I was in SQL, and I confidently said I was pretty strong. I was then asked to 
write queries for two moderately difficult problems involving JOINS, UNIONS, and AGGREGATE 
FUNCTIONS. For the second one, I made a small syntactical mistake, which I realized only after 
handing over the paper. However, I understood that ‘what matters is not just what you write on 
paper, but how clearly you explain your approach’. My explanation greatly convinced them, and they 
moved on to test my programming knowledge. 
When asked which language I was most comfortable with, I answered Java. They gave me a problem: 
Given an array of Strings, retrieve unique strings that do not have all letters in lowercase. 
I first suggested using a Set collection, but they added that the result should be ordered. I then 
answered that I would use a List and check on each update, but they expected me to use a 
LinkedHashSet, which I wasn’t aware of at that time. On realizing this, they asked me to explain my 
approach using a List. Here, they were testing how well I knew the language, since they repeatedly 
asked me about built-in functions to support my explanation. 
Later, they asked me to explain a sorting algorithm of my choice. I chose Merge Sort and explained 
what it is, why it is used, its time and space complexities, and also drew its working process. They 
were particularly interested in checking whether I truly understood the concept or had just 
memorized it. 
With this, the technical interview came to an end. 
HR Questions 
Next, they asked me how I would handle team conflicts. I struggled a bit to put it into words but 
made sure my answer reflected honesty and my willingness to handle situations positively. I also 
related it to my experiences working in projects and clubs. 
At the end, they asked if I had any questions for them. This step is as important as your self-
introduction—it’s always good to ask thoughtful questions that show you are genuinely interested in 
the role. 
(For some panels, candidates were also asked DSA problems and puzzles.) 
The results were announced the same evening. Out of the shortlisted candidates, nine were selected 
for the internship, and I am truly grateful to be one among them. 
 
PERSONAL TIPS : 
• 
Make it a habit to practice DSA regularly. I would recommend choosing at least 1 DSA 
problem + 1 SQL problem each day, with the difficulty level adjusted to your schedule. 
Practice coding on platforms like LeetCode, HackerRank, and GeeksforGeeks. 
• 
Also practice aptitudes on platforms like IndiaBIX. 
• 
Also, be comfortable coding from scratch, since some companies may ask you to write code 
starting from the main function itself. 
• 
During interviews, maintain good body language and avoid panicking, as it creates a positive 
impression. Always stay optimistic, greet the interviewers, and acknowledge them at the 
right moments—this leaves a lasting impact. 
• 
Before your interview, go through GeeksforGeeks archives, CSEA or other placement 
experiences to get a clearer idea of what to expect. Keep in mind that not all interviewers 
will be friendly or make it easy for you; some might intentionally test how you handle 
pressure, similar to dealing with difficult clients. 
• 
Do thorough research on the company beforehand—it shows genuine interest in the role 
and will definitely work in your favor. 
• 
Be honest about everything you have mentioned in your resume. As you can see from my 
experience, my interview was almost entirely tailored to what I had listed in mine. 
• 
Most importantly, don’t lose hope if you don’t get through a particular company—the best is 
yet to come.  
• 
Avoid self-doubt. Remember, all candidates are at a similar level. Confidence and presenting 
the best version of yourself is already halfway to success. 
 
Best wishes!! 
Feel free to contact me at any point in your preparation.

multiple methods and she was expecting me to talk on Promise in JS. 
 
Then she asked me how processes gets into CPU. I talked about every 
process states in OS, why premption is good / bad. While explaining I said ‘We 
shouldn’t keep our CPU idle right?’. She was like ‘Yeah obviosly’. And I explained 5 
popular job scheduling algorithms quickly one by one. 
 
 Technical round 2 (40-45 mins) 
 
 
Asked me to introduce myself. Introduced as same as previous round. She 
asked me which is the data structure I’m more familiar with. I said Graphs. She 
asked me to explain Graph. I opened sketchpad and started talking about everything 
I know in graph and explained her visually by sketching it in sketchpad. Started with 
nodes and edges. Then directed and undirected graphs. Then components, 
connected components and strongly connected components. Finally she asked to me 
add 3 more nodes to the existing graph that I drew while explaining and she also 
gave me the edges and asked a simple shortest distance question mentioning the 
source and destination. I told her the approach and dry ran it visually step by step 
(not asked to code). 
 
 
Again some questions on projects and internships. 
 
 
Then I was asked to design the linked list class with all methods available in 
collections. I asked the requirements and coded the same. 
 
 
Then she asked me about polymorphism. I explained it and talked about 
compile time and run time polymorphism. She asked me to code and show how run 
time polymorphism works. I did it and also told why its called as run time 
polymorphism. While explaining I said ‘this new keyword is responsible for creating 
an actual memory in heap which happens in run-time’. Then she suddenly asked me 
what happens if I keep on creating objects using new keyword in java. I told her 
about internal heap memory, Garbage collector and finalize method in java. 
 
 HR round (1hr) 
 
 
This is more of a discussion. He was talking more than me. Yes, he was talking 
for like half an hour. 
 
 
First he went through my resume for a minute and asked me what dart is. 
This is not even a tech question. He might wanted to ask something that’s odd in my 
resume. Told him about dart and why I had to learn it (Flutter). 
 
 
Then he asked me to tell what all I did in my 3 years of college life. Took 
easily more than 10mins to explain it (This is basically an introduction question. But 
here we need to introduce the actual us, not limiting it to techie us). 
 
 
Then he asked me, ‘Why are you here? Why are you giving this interview?’. I 
was like ‘Why in the sense?’. He said ‘What’s the actual purpose of you giving this 
interview? Are you sure you’re in the right path of your life?’. Then I was saying 
something for like 10mins. I told him why I got interested in computer science. How 
a person can evolve in this field and why it’s so interesting than a normal 9-4 job. 
And blah blah. Told about what all I can relate me with computer science. 
 
 
A small discussion on my internship. Then he asked me whether I have any 
questions for him. 
 
 
I asked him the difference between GBU and OFSS. He said it’s a great 
question and started explained everything for like 10mins (Oracle visited PSG tech 
for 3 different roles. That’s how I got to know about OFSS). Then I asked about 6m 
internship like do we work on production or is it fully a bootcamp. Then I said 
‘Every youtubers who work in big product based companies are showing 
themselves chilling all time in play area, cafeteria, etc...Is this true like don’t you 
guys work much’. He started laughing and started explaining the work culture of 
Oracle and how people with different learning curves work and chill there. 
 
 
After I’m done with my questions he said ‘Okay now let me ask you one 
question’. I was like ‘Yes, sure’. Then he said ‘Okay assume that I’m giving you an 
Oracle GBU offer. Sametime another org is also approaching you with an offer. 
Let’s say Subspace (I did my internship there). So which one will you accept?’. I said 
Oracle and also explained why. Like I said networking is more important for an 
individual (He nodded and told that network helps one to grow). I told him if I join 
Oracle I’ll get an opportunity to get connected with technically sounded minds and 
evolve myself along with them. So basically this was the context and I was talking on 
it for 3-4mins. 
 
 
Then he said ‘Okay let me ask you one more question. You watch cricket 
right. Now Indian team is performing good. Back then in 2000s, Sachin was the only 
saviour of India but if you see Australia, all 11 players were top talented players. So 
hypothetically if you were given a chance to join any one of these boards, which 
would have been your choice. To join Australia saying that all 11 players are talented 
or to join India saying I’ll make India a better team like Australia?’. We both started 
laughing at eachother and I was like ‘Is this what people call a typical HR question?’.  
 
He said ‘Yes this is, okay let me put it straight. Oracle, a new place for you which 
has better people, or Subspace, your current org which has more interns. Don’t 
you help Subspace to grow better’. I was telling him something for 4-5mins which I 
don’t even remember now. Then he said ‘To be honest, there’s no right answer for 
this question. It depends on individuals. I was just curious to know your answer. 
That’s it’.  
 
 
Then he said ‘It was nice talking to you Rishi. HR lead will get connected with 
you’ and disconnected the call. 
 
 
 
 
Tips to ace an interview, 
 
 Have a great Cp profile and you’re half way done with your interviews. 
 
 Don’t get overwhelmed. Treat interviewers like your Juniors and answer every 
question like you were given a chance to teach. 
 
 Don’t give one liners as answers. If you did, you will end up getting follow ups on 
follow ups. Try to avoid follow ups by explaining everything in depth. 
 
 Don’t be silent while you’re coding. Always have your interviewer’s attention. 
Keep on talking or explaining whatever you code in paper / codepair. 
 
 Try to build conversation with your interviewer and move your interview more 
of a discussion than a rapid fire round. 
 
 Always have your personal projects deployed and provide the live link in your 
resume if it’s web dev. 
 
 
Resources, 
 
 YouTube 
 
1. TakeUForward (Dsa), 
2. Kunal Kushwaha (OOP, Java internals, networks and Docker), 
3. Gate Smashers (Os, Dbms), 
4. CP ( Utkarsh Gupta, Priyansh Agarwal, Code master cpp, Erichto, Second 
Thread, William Lin, etc..) 
5. Keerti Purswani (Design Patterns) 
6. Gaurav Sen (System design) 
 
 For dedicated object oriented design rounds, one best and go to book is 
Grooking the Object Oriented Design Interviews 
 
 Dsa cheat sheet - Striver’s SDE sheet

Morgan Stanley Intern Interview Experience ‘23
About the company:
Morgan Stanley is an American multinational investment management and financial services
company headquartered at 1585 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan, New York City. With offices
in more than 41 countries and more than 75,000 employees, the firm's clients include
corporations, governments, institutions, and individuals. Morgan Stanley ranked No. 61 in the
2021 Fortune 500 list of the largest United States corporations by total revenue.
Number of rounds : 3
1) Online Test
2) Technical Interview
3) HR Interview
ONLINE ASSESSMENT:
Platform: SHL AMCAT
The online assessment had 3 sections.
1) 10 aptitude questions to be solved in 30 minutes
2) 7 debugging questions to be solved in 20 minutes
3) 3 coding questions to be solved in 1 hour
The aptitude questions were of medium difficulty and the challenging part was to solve them
within the given time. Questions were based on profit/loss, family member relations and
numerical reasoning mainly. Few questions were longer than others and the trick here was to
not spend too much time on a single question and move on to questions that consume less
time. Your aim should be to correctly solve as many questions as possible within the given time
frame. I was able to solve 8/10 questions correctly here.
As far as the debugging section was concerned we could choose between 3 programming
languages- C, C++ and Java. Once we chose our language we were shown 7 code segments
with syntaxial, semantic and logical errors. We had to rewrite the segment of code to perform its
task correctly. The important piece here is to read the question for each code segment carefully
as each segment of misperforming code is expected to do something particular and achieve a
particular task. After your modifications, the code should perform the same task correctly without
errors, passing all the test cases. I was able to solve 7/7 questions correctly here.
The final and all important coding section consisted of 3 questions to be solved in one hour. We
could choose to code them in our preferred coding language. The questionsI got were:
1) Given a sorted array arr[] consisting of N distinct integers and an integer K, the task is to
find the index of K, if it’s present in the array arr[]. Otherwise, find the index where K
must be inserted to keep the array sorted.
2) Given an array of N numbers and a positive integer K. The problem is to find K numbers
with the most occurrences, i.e., the top K numbers having the maximum frequency. If two
numbers have the same frequency then the number that appears first from the beginning
should be given preference. The numbers should be displayed in decreasing order of
their frequencies. It is assumed that the array consists of at least K numbers.
3) Given an array arr[] of integers, find out the maximum difference between any two
elements such that the larger element appears after the smaller number.
The questions were of easy-medium difficulty and I was able to pass all the test cases in all
three questions.
TECHNICAL INTERVIEW
This round went on for an hour and a half and was a face to face interview conducted physically
at CUIC. 14/350 students were shortlisted for the same. The interview strayed away from my
conventional notion of how an interview went and was intuitive and different.
The interviewer began by asking for my introduction followed by which he straightaway jumped
into a game of Tic Tac Toe following which he asked me to design the Tic Tac Toe game using
OOPS. I suggested using an interface with 2 abstract methods: one to make a move and
another to check if a player wins after making his/her move. He then proceeded to ask me what
the minimum number of function calls would be to check if a player has won. I initially said 6
after which with his help I was able to prove that it was 4. He then modified the problem to make
it a n x n board instead of the conventional 3 x 3 Tic Tac Toe board and then asked me to come
up with a generalized expression in terms of n for the minimum number of function calls
required. I was able to do this following which he modified the problem further, adding an
additional rule which says that whenever a player puts a ‘O’ or ‘X’ they must also remove a ‘O’
or ‘X’ provided the board has at least 3 of their symbols at any instance. That is, at any point of
time in the game there should be a maximum of 3 of my symbols on the board. He asked me
what the minimum number of function calls would be in this case.
Following this the interviewer asked me some questions on OOPS concepts. I was asked what
encapsulation was and also to demonstrate the need for encapsulation using an example and
what would happen if encapsulation didn't exist. He then proceeded to questions on Java,
asking me how I would pass command line arguments in Java as well as quizzing me on
Containers in Java. He then asked me a coding question related to sorting. The question
follows.
Given a train ticket consisting of multiple fields such as booking time, arrival time, departure time
and ticket ID, assume the tickets are already sorted based on the basis of their ID. Ask
questions to determine which sorting algorithm to use. Take the parameter based on which to
sort the tickets as an input and code the sorting algorithm.
I initially suggested merge sort for which he told me I hadn't asked all the necessary questions
and helped me realize that quicksort was the best option given that he wanted the code to be
more space efficient than time efficient. I then proceeded to write the pseudocode for the same
on pen and paper. He then asked me about hashing collision and collision resistance, which are
DSA concepts based on hashing.
The interviewer then moved to Python and ML based questions since I had mentioned it in my
resume. He asked me about adversarial attacks in deep learning since I was working on a
research paper based on that. He also asked me basic questions on Python such as the
difference between lists and tuples and about Type Error. Finally he moved to OS related
questions, asking me what a batch operating system was and what were the advantages of
Linux over Windows and vice versa.
After this he asked me if I had any questions and concluded the interview.
HR INTERVIEW
The HR interview went on for an hour and mostly focussed on behavioral and scenario based
questions. I was asked team based situational questions such as how I would react in conflict
scenarios and how I would make my teammates understand my point of view. I was also asked
questions with regards to my project and if I had stuck to deadlines on all the projects. Technical
questions were also asked with regards to some of my projects and I was asked how I would do
things differently in my future projects. Other than this I was asked a few other basic HR
questions following which the interview concluded.
Around 10:30 the same night I got an email telling me that I was selected for the internship at
Morgan Stanley. 5 students were finally selected out of the 14 shortlisted for interviews.
IMPORTANT TIPS
●
For preparations, stick to a few good resources instead of practicing problems on
multiple platforms.
●
Stick to Leetcode and solve problems from any sheet such as neetcode.io on a daily
basis without fail. 5-6 problems a day starting about 3 months before the online test
round is a must in order to maintain consistency. Always begin by coding the brute force
approach and proceed gradually to the most optimized solution.
●
Remember to revise your previously solved problems. Don’t memorize the code but
rather revise the approach you used and code it again from scratch.
●
For OS, DBMS go through your semester notes as well as GeeksForGeeks last minute
notes.
●
During the interview, remember to remain calm under all circumstances! Do not get
demotivated by any kind of corrections or interruptions from the interviewer’s side. Don’t
assume that the answer you gave was incorrect or not satisfactory just because of an
interruption on the interviewer’s side. Be confident with the answers you give and do not
get swayed.
●
If you do not know an answer to a problem based question, outline your thought process
and what you understand from the question. This will help the interviewer gauge your
understanding of the problem statement and help you go in the right direction
accordingly. Never keep silent during the interview.
●
If you do not know the answer to a direct theory based question, let the interviewer know
that politely rather than say something you are not sure of and make yourself prone to
follow up questions.
●
As far as the HR round is concerned, be completely honest and make sure to read up on
the company’s core values and leadership principles beforehand.
●
Last but most importantly, STICK TO YOUR PLAN. Never ever get intimidated by
what others are doing or how others are preparing or what internships others are getting.
You are the product of your hard work alone and be confident of your preparation and
NEVER get demotivated by a few negative results. At the end of the day there are many
factors at play in deciding whether you get an internship and sometimes all of these may
not be in your control. Your responsibility is just to focus on your work and do your best.
Then there is no mountain that is too high to climb :)
All the best!
Rithvik Senthil,
BE CSE,
CEG ‘24 batch

About The Company 
Citibank is the consumer banking division of Citigroup, one of the world's largest 
financial services organizations. Citibank offers a wide range of financial products and 
services to individuals, businesses, and institutions, including retail banking, wealth 
management, global transaction services, etc. Citibank has a significant international 
presence with operations in numerous countries and serves millions of customers 
globally. 
Citi CSC stands for Citi Solutions Centers. It is a global network of technology centers 
that provide support to Citi’s core businesses and clients around the world. The centers 
are in strategic locations across the globe, including Pune, India, where one of Citi’s 
largest technology centers is located. They deal with Markets & Securities, Risk & 
Capital Management, Cash Management, Trade & Treasury Services, Private Banking, 
Mobile, Compliance & Surveillance Technology, Finance and Risk Mitigation. 
Citi CSC came to our college to recruit for 2 of their domains: 
• 
International Consumer Group (ICG): This domain focused more on 
development. Knowledge on microservices, Java, Apache server, Kafka and app 
development were tested in the online round for students who chose this 
domain, and the coding round for this test included the coding of a form (don’t 
really remember the details). 
• 
Enterprise Operations & Technology (EO&T): This was the domain I picked as 
it aligned a lot more with the fields I am passionate about, i.e. cybersecurity and 
testing. The job description included tasks like risk analysis and mitigation, data 
protection, web and mobile app testing, API development and integration, 
analytics, and a lot more. I have explained the test and the interview process 
for this domain in detail. 
 
Online Round: 
Platform: SHL Amcat 
• 
Verbal Section: Consisted of questions on reading comprehension, grammar, 
vocabulary, and elementary grammar skills [14 questions – 20 mins] 
• 
Advanced Aptitude: Questions were based on number sequences, text encoding, 
blood relations, logic puzzles, etc. [12 questions – 12 minutes] 
• 
CS Fundamentals: Algorithms, basic DBMS, code error correction, finding the 
output, etc.   [12 questions – 12 minutes] 
• 
Coding: 2 questions (total 45 mins) – no. of negative numbers in an array, sum of 
prime numbers within a given range [since the questions were extremely easy, 
they could be completed in less than 5 minutes XD] 
 
Round 1: Tech Round [Two interviewers] 
• 
During the interview, we kicked things off with the standard "Tell us about 
yourself." However, the conversation took an interesting turn when they 
discovered I’m not originally from Chennai. One of the interviewers was curious 
about how I coped with the language barrier, considering that Tamil is the 
predominant language in Chennai. I explained that after a decade of living in 
Chennai, I had become decently fluent in Tamil. I also mentioned that I 
continued to learn and improve my language skills as it wasn’t my mother tongue. 
• 
Then, they delved into my resume, where I had listed problem-solving as one of 
my skills. They asked for a real-life example of how I had applied this skill, so I 
shared a relevant experience. 
• 
The interview also got technical with questions about file handling and database 
management systems (DBMS). For most of these questions, I had to walk them 
through the logic step by step. In one instance, they asked me to write an SQL 
query on paper. 
• 
Next, we ventured into discussions about data structures. They were curious 
about which data structures I was familiar with, and this led to conversations 
about linked lists and their related problems 
• 
I was asked to explain the different kinds of trees, and I had accidently 
mentioned AVL trees, and that sparked a more in-depth discussion. The 
interviewer delved into how AVL trees function, their significance, and their 
practical applications. 
• 
They also chose one of my projects and asked me to provide a detailed 
explanation. They were keen to know my thoughts on potential improvements for 
the project. 
• 
They inquired about my decision to pick the EO&T domain, wanting to 
understand my motivations and reasons for choosing this specific field. 
• 
They threw in a more casual question about my familiarity with Object-Oriented 
Programming (OOP) concepts. I mentioned that I had a basic understanding of 
OOP. 
• 
As the interview concluded, they gave me the opportunity to ask any questions I 
had in mind 
 
Round 2: Managerial Round [Two Interviewers] 
• 
The interview began with the customary request for a self-introduction. 
Subsequently, they inquired about my place of origin once again, seeking to 
understand more about my background. 
• 
The conversation then shifted towards a discussion about the backend 
mechanism of payment apps like GPay. While I didn't have a complete grasp of 
the topic, I made an effort to provide a reasonable answer. In response, the 
interviewer acknowledged my attempt and said I was close, and then patiently 
explained the intricate workings behind GPay. 
• 
They were also curious about my pursuits outside of academics. Specifically, 
they wanted to know how I had explored and pursued my passions beyond the 
classroom. 
• 
The interview delved into teamwork and collaboration when I was asked to 
narrate a situation in which I encountered difficulty working in a team and how I 
resolved it. They also inquired about who took the initiative to address the issue. 
Additionally, I was asked if there had been instances where I had to delegate 
my responsibilities to a teammate. 
• 
I had mentioned my certification as a piano player in my resume, which piqued 
their interest. They asked me about my music interests, including my preferred 
style, favorite composer, and the pieces that resonated with me. 
• 
The discussion turned to my technical interests, which I had highlighted in my 
resume. They noticed my inclination towards core subjects and asked why I 
hadn't considered pursuing higher studies. 
• 
Given that I had expressed an interest in research in the "About Me" section of 
my resume, I was asked to elaborate on why I found research intriguing and 
what drew me to it. 
• 
They were keen to understand why I would like to work in the finance sector and 
how it aligned with my career growth.  
• 
Looking to the future, they asked about my two-year career aspirations and my 
long-term plans beyond that timeframe. 
• 
The interview concluded by offering me the opportunity to ask questions I had  
 
Round 3: HR Round [One Interviewer] 
• 
The interview commenced with the interviewer introducing himself, setting a 
conversational tone. After that, he invited me to introduce myself and share a 
bit about my background. 
• 
We then discussed my experiences with Citi so far, as he inquired about how my 
journey with the company had been up to that point. 
• 
He asked me whether I had any relatives employed in the government or in Citi 
• 
One of the projects I had prominently featured on my resume involved a website 
designed to facilitate Food Donation Management. He was keen to know what 
had motivated me to choose this particular project and delve into its working. 
• 
Noticing that I had listed proficiency in around five languages on my resume, the 
interviewer inquired about the reasons behind my pursuit of multiple languages, 
wanting to understand the motivations behind each one. 
• 
As the conversation continued, he asked if there was any recent achievement in 
my life that I felt particularly proud of. 
• 
I was then asked about what my greatest weakness was, to which I elaborated 
on and mentioned ways in which I successfully battled it and how I’m still 
continuing to work on it. 
• 
Finally, the interview came to a close, and he provided me with the opportunity 
to ask any questions I might have about the company or the role. 
 
All the interviews took place on the same day, in-person at CUIC. 
There were about 20 students shortlisted from 100 who picked the EO&T domain, and 
by GOD’S GRACE I was one of the 5 students selected for the summer internship. 
 
My Tips: 
• 
DO NOT PANIC. At least with respect to Citi, the interview process is really 
chilled out and there is absolutely no need to stress 
• 
Don’t spend too much time on a single aptitude question. And on certain 
platforms like Amcat, they do no let you switch between questions or go back to 
a previous question, so tackle the questions on the spot and wisely. 
• 
The hiring committee really values students who have clear-cut interests and 
goals; those who know exactly what and why they want to do it. 
• 
At the same time, they are looking for those who have a strong passion to learn 
and explore everything; no company wants rigid and the always-in-your-comfort-
zone kind of employees 
• 
Try at least attempting to answer all the questions thrown at you 
• 
If you are asked something you don’t know, try to give a basic logical answer and 
make sure to mention that it is something you are open to learning/improving and 
you haven’t had the opportunity to explore it just yet 
• 
I received easy coding questions, but that may not be the case with other 
domains and companies. I had close to no coding practice for the internship 
drive and that is something I regret. Make sure you start practicing well in 
advance 
• 
DO NOT lie on your resume. Interviewers will easily be able to figure out 
whether you have bluffed or not 
• 
Know what the company does, how your skillset will benefit them and how they 
can help your career advance 
• 
Know your projects inside-out, every little detail can be questioned. Know the 
shortcomings of your project beforehand so that you’re not caught by surprise 
• 
Do not put a rating or level for your technical skills. I had done that and it 
landed me into slight trouble.
