APPIAN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Appian is a soŌware company that automates business processes. The Appian AI-Powered Process 
Plaƞorm includes everything you need to design, automate, and opƟmize even the most complex 
processes, from start to ﬁnish. The world's most innovaƟve organizaƟons trust Appian to improve 
their workﬂows, unify data, and opƟmize operaƟons, resulƟng in beƩer growth and superior 
customer experiences. For more informaƟon, visit appian.com.  
Roles Offered: Software Engineer, Quality Engineer, UI/UX Designer, Product Manager 
Stipend: ₹1,50,000 INR (all roles) 
Selection Process:  
Pre-Placement Talk (PPT) → Online Assessment → Technical Interview 1 → Technical Interview 2 → 
HR Interview 
Roles Applied For: Quality Engineer, Software Engineer, and UI/UX Designer 
Final Offer: Selected for the Quality Engineer role 
Online Assessment (for the role of QE): 
Test plaƞorm: Glider AI 
It consisted of 25 MCQs focused on: 
• ApƟtude and logical reasoning 
• Programming fundamentals  
The test lasted for 45 mins and I had the opƟon to answer the quesƟons in any order.  
Most of them involved quick thinking and focused on paying aƩenƟon to detail. 
These are some of the quesƟon categories: 
ApƟtude – puzzles, ﬁnding duraƟon / Ɵme, relaƟon between family members 
Finding output/error in the given code snippet 
Expression evaluaƟon and operators  
Reference variables and pointers 
Based on this online assessment, 17 people were shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Technical Interview – 1: (30-45 mins) 
The interviews were conducted at CUIC. The interviewer started oﬀ by going through my resume and 
appreciated me about the various acƟviƟes that I’d done in two years (I was a part of clubs, 
completed some cerƟﬁcaƟons (NPTEL, Hackerrank) and a JAVA training program. I had also done a 
summer internship at DCSE, CEG).  I was asked to talk about any of these.  
I spoke about my DCSE internship, where I was the team lead of a module. Then I was asked about 
my performance as the lead and how I helped my team develop our module. She also asked me 
about the things I did as the lead that I wouldn’t have typically done as a team member. 
 
Then I was asked to talk about my most favourite project from the resume. I spoke about my Full 
stack project (neighbourhood service ﬁnder). I was asked about how it stood out from similar 
plaƞorms like urban and also the struggles I faced while developing it. 
 
Then I was asked to solve two puzzles. 
1. Why are manholes circular in shape and not a square or a rectangle? 
 
I answered this by saying how a circle has no corners and hence it could be the shape that 
could get the least damaged. I also explained that it could ensure faster ﬂow of water, than a 
shape with an edge or a corner. 
 
2. Cut a circular cake into 8 pieces (not necessarily equal) using only 3 straight lines.  
 
(She oﬀered me a paper and pen to work out, in case of need) 
 
I tried diﬀerent ways but I was not able to answer this. So, I told her and then she moved on 
to the next quesƟon. 
 
Coding quesƟons: 
       I was asked two coding quesƟons (easy-level). 
1. Finding odd numbers from a stream of numbers. 
 
I was asked to write the code for it. I explained the code as I wrote. I used modulo operator 
to idenƟfy the odd numbers and stored them in an Array list instead of an array, which has 
ﬁxed memory (as we do not know the total number of elements).  
 
CODE: 
List<Integer> ﬁndOddNumbers(List<Integer> stream) { 
        List<Integer> oddNumbers = new ArrayList<>(); 
        for (int num : stream) { 
            if (num % 2 != 0) { 
                oddNumbers.add(num); 
            } 
        } 
        return oddNumbers; 
    } 
 
 
2. Then I was asked to solve the two sum problem (with a slight variation). 
 
Given two arrays, generate and return pairs of elements such that the first element is from 
the first array and the second element from the second. The catch was that the sum of these 
two numbers should be equal to a given target value. 
I asked if I could write the logic in pseudocode, she said yes. 
I started with the bruteforce solution, where I scanned each element in the first array and 
computed its sum with each element of the second array. If they added up to the target, I 
stored them in a 2D array (I had a counter to store elements in the 2D array) and then finally 
returned the 2D array. 
The complexity of this would be O(n²) (product of the sizes of the two arrays). 
To optimize it, 
I used a hashmap to store the elements of the 2nd array. 
I scanned each element of the first array and I computed the complement by subtracting the 
element from the target sum, and then I checked if the key of this complement was present 
in the hashmap. 
If it was present, I added them to a 2D array and finally I returned the 2D array. 
The complexity of this would be O(n) (size of one of the arrays). 
Then I explained the bruteforce approach. And when I started to explain how I optimized it, 
she said that bruteforce was enough but was curious to see how I optimized it. 
So I explained how I optimized it as well, she liked my answer. 
 
CODE:  
 
Bruteforce approach: 
List<int[]> findPairsBruteForce(int[] arr1, int[] arr2, int target) { 
List<int[]> result = new ArrayList<>(); 
for (int i = 0; i < arr1.length; i++) { 
for (int j = 0; j < arr2.length; j++) { 
if (arr1[i] + arr2[j] == target) { 
result.add(new int[]{arr1[i], arr2[j]}); 
} 
} 
} 
return result; 
} 
Using hashset: 
 
List<int[]> ﬁndPairsOpƟmized(int[] arr1, int[] arr2, int target) { 
    List<int[]> result = new ArrayList<>(); 
    Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>(); 
     
    for (int num : arr2) { 
        set.add(num); 
    } 
 
    for (int num : arr1) { 
        int complement = target - num; 
        if (set.contains(complement)) { 
            result.add(new int[]{num, complement}); 
        } 
    } 
     
    return result; 
}

The interview was conducted on Google Meet, and the interviewer joined virtually. 
He asked me to introduce myself ﬁrst.

of development.

Next quesƟon was a bit tricky, he asked me if I would choose SE if I had the opƟon. I said no, because 
it’s usually advised to sƟck to the role you have been shortlisted for (as you might get rejected

Finally, he asked me if I had any quesƟons for him.

The result were announced around 9pm the same day, and I was one of the 3 candidates who got

General Tips: 
 
Stay calm through out the process. Don’t let your anxiety take over you. 
Have a neutral mindset, don’t let anything get to your head. 
Peer pressure and insecuriƟes are challenges that you can get through only by believing in yourself. 
Answer all quesƟons honestly and conﬁdently. 
If you are unsure of something, admit it. They are looking for candidates, who are willing to learn. 
PracƟce DSA fundamentals (arrays, strings, stacks, linked lists, queue, tree), OOPs and DBMS 
concepts for coding assessments.  
Understand these concepts and try to apply them in real world scenarios. 
PracƟcing puzzles and general apƟtude quesƟons will build your criƟcal thinking skills. 
Be thorough in all the projects you menƟon in your resume. 
If you need a second to think, you can very well say that you need some Ɵme to think and then 
answer.  
The way you think and approach maƩers more than the soluƟon you compute. 
Be clam and talk slowly during the interview. 
Wear professional aƫre and look neat.

Coding Round:

Qn.1:  
We are given a list of player numbers from 1 to n that contains exactly one duplicate 
and exactly one missing number. 
We are also given a fixed set of teams, each containing certain players. 
 
Our task is to: 
1. Identify the duplicate number in the list. 
 
 
2. Identify the missing number in the list. 
3. Determine the team status: 
 
"Valid" if both duplicate and missing players belong to the same team. 
"Multiple Teams" if they belong to different teams. 
"No Valid Team" if they are not in any team together. 
 
Example Input: 
Arr = [1, 2, 2, 3, 5, 6] 
Teams = [ 
    [1, 2], 
    [3, 4], 
    [5, 6] 
] 
Explanation: 
       Numbers should be from 1 to 6. 
       Duplicate → 2 (appears twice).     Missing → 4 (doesn’t appear). 
 
Team membership: 
                     2 is in [1, 2] (Team 1). 
                     4 is in [3, 4] (Team 2). 
              Different teams → “Multiple Teams” 
Expected Output: 
               2 
4 
Multiple Teams

Qn.3: (Similar to Leetcode problem – Insert interval) 
        You are given a list of non-overlapping time intervals (the schedule) sorted by start time, 
and a new time interval representing a new session to add. 
Insert the new session into the schedule so that: 
 The resulting schedule remains sorted by start time.Any overlapping intervals are merged 
into one continuous interval. 
Example :  Input 
Existing schedule: {{9, 11}, {13, 15}} 
New session: {10, 14} 
Output:    Updated schedule after inserting and merging: 
[9, 15]

Wells Fargo Interview Experience
About the company:
Wells Fargo is an American multinational banking company that operates in 35 countries
and is considered one of the big four banks in the United states. They provide banking,
investment and mortgage products and services, as well as consumer and commercial
finance through more than 7,200 locations, more than 13,000 ATMs, the internet
(wellsfargo.com) and mobile banking.
Interview process:
The entire process consisted of 4 rounds
●
Online test
●
Technical Interview
●
2 rounds of HR interview
Online test:
The test was conducted on the amcat platform and consisted of 3 sections: verbal aptitude,
business aptitude and coding section.
The verbal aptitude section had questions on reading comprehension and interpreting and
understanding given passages to answer questions.
The business aptitude section contained questions involving interpreting and understanding
various graphs and some analytical questions.
The coding section consisted of three questions:
1.
First question: Given a string and a character find the frequency of the character in
the given string.
2.
Second question: Given an array and a position k, sort the elements from 0th position
to k - 1th position in ascending order and elements from kth till end in descending
order.
3.
Third question: Given an adjacency matrix representing a graph and a start vertex,
check whether it is possible to visit all nodes in the graph exactly once (check
whether Hamiltonian cycle exists).
The questions were on the easier side in all the three sections.
A total of 22 students were shortlisted for the second round.
Technical Interview:
The interview was held at MIT campus the day after the online test and it went for about 30 -
40 minutes.
The interview started with me giving an introduction about myself. Then the interviewer
asked which is my favourite/comfortable data structure and I answered stack. He then
proceeded to ask about the implementation of stack and how to store and retrieve
information in a stack.
The next question was “Given an array of strings, sort them with respect to the first
character, if they are equal, sort wrt second character and so on”. I gave a modified version
of bubble sort as the answer and he immediately asked about all sorting algorithms that I
knew. He then asked me to illustrate the merge sort with the help of an example.
Next question was “given a node of a binary tree, find its inorder predecessor and
successor”. I was able to give a satisfactory answer and he accepted it.He then asked me
about the time complexity and space complexity of my algorithm.
He then asked me questions related to DBMS concepts like what is a primary key, foreign
key, various normal forms a database can be in. I answered all the questions and he seemed
happy about it. He then asked me to write a SQL query to display a column in ascending
order of its values and I was able to write it.He then gave me a situation and asked me to
design the database and relations for it and asked me how to store the information of a
particular column more optimally. I was not able to come up with an answer which he
expected so he helped me to answer it.
He then went on to ask me about the projects i mentioned in my resume and questioned
about some competitions which i had in my resume. He also asked me why I preferred
python more (as I had mentioned I was more comfortable with it) and also asked me the
differences between Node.js and regular javascript.
Finally the interview got over and I was asked to wait.
HR Round 1:
This round went more like a conversation rather than an interview. The interviewer talked in a
jovial manner and told me about his career and stuff. He then asked me why I chose Wells
Fargo and I answered that I was interested and excited about learning how we as software
engineers can help a finance/banking company and he was pleased with my answer.
He then wanted me to ask a few questions and I asked him about 3-4 questions including
“how will an intern spend his time in Wells Fargo?” and “What does the company expect from
an intern like us?”. I also asked about what kind of security features were in place if a data
breach was to occur in the company.
Overall it went for about 25 minutes and I was confident in whatever I talked and asked
which seemed to work with the interviewer.
HR Round 2:
This round went for about 5 minutes. The interviewer asked me how my day was and how I
felt about the company. She then went to ask about my projects in my resume and asked
about whether I liked working in a team or individually. I talked about both of them and talked
about what I like and don't like in both situations.
She then asked me about my skills and strengths as a person. After that she wanted me to
ask a question and I asked about the projects we as interns would be working on in Wells
Fargo.
The results were announced in the evening of the same day and 12 people including me
were selected.
Tips and Insights:
●
Practice dsa questions on a daily basis from leetcode,geeks for geeks and
hackerrank or other platforms. Even if you end up solving less number of questions
make sure you completely understand whatever questions you solve.
●
The resources provided by CODE-ACM were really good and covered all topics that
could be asked in technical and coding rounds.Start your preparations around 2-3
months before to give yourself time to be strong in basics.
●
Prepare DBMS,OS and OOPS concepts along with dsa preparation as you won't have
time before the interview.
●
The aptitude part of the online test contained easy but lengthy questions. So take
your time, read the question and answer it. Try to answer as many aptitude questions
as you can to boost your chances.
●
Be confident and convincing and whatever you answer in the interviews. The
interviewer assesses how you answer and come up with solutions and not whether
you are correct in your answer. They always try to help you, so be confident and
maintain a cool head.
●
Your resume is very important as half of the questions in the interview will be from it.
So make sure to only include stuff that you know and can answer any kind questions
on it.
●
Also make sure to add 2-3 of your best projects and take a look at them before the
interview as a lot of questions will be asked about your projects.
●
Always prepare a few interesting and different types of questions to ask the
interviewer which shows your genuine interest in the company.

SAP LABS INDIA– INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
About the Company: 
 
SAP (short for Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is one of the biggest 
names in enterprise software, helping businesses around the world run smarter, whether it’s 
managing their supply chains, handling finances, or making sense of their data. 
 
SAP Labs India is a key innovation and R&D hub for SAP, with a global presence in five cities: 
Bengaluru, Gurgaon, Pune, Mumbai, and Hyderabad. Bengaluru hosts the largest campus, 
serving as a crucial center for driving AI solutions and enterprise technology development for 
global customers. The labs focus on a customer-centric and agile approach to innovation, 
covering user experience, business models, and cutting-edge technology.  
Role Offered: Developer Associate 
 
Selection Process: 
 
1. Online Test 
2. Technical Round 1 
3. Technical Round 2 
4. Techno-managerial Round 
5. HR Round 
 
For me, only four rounds were held including OA. I think they might have combined Tech 2 and 
Techno-Managerial. I spent almost the whole day waiting for round one, and once it started, 
the rounds blasted off like a rocket. 
 
Online Assessment(@CUIC): 
 
About 280 students made it to the online assessment round. The OA happened in the IT lab and 
ran for a solid 100 minutes (yep, 1 hour 40 mins — long enough to question your life choices). 
The test was on Codility, with both video and audio proctoring. 
We got 2 DSA questions each, and everyone had their own mystery set with different kinds of 
questions. One was supposed to be “easy”, and the other might fit in the “medium” zone. But 
I’d say, I got a 1+1 offer!! both turned out to be mediums. 
 
 
Questions asked for me: 
1) Problem: Cover the Holes with Wood 
You are given a table with holes at certain positions. Your task is to cover all the holes using 
exactly two wooden planks of the same length. You have woods of infinite size and also the 
table has infinite length, but you want to minimize the wooden plank size. 
• An integer array holes[] representing the positions of holes on the table. 
• 1 <= holes.length <= 10^5, 0 <= holes[i] <= 10^9(unique) 
Eg: holes = [1, 3, 4, 7, 9], Output: 5 
 
Explanation: One plank of length 5 can cover holes at positions [1,3,4], and other plank for the 
remaining holes.  
My thoughts: It is basically a medium-easy qns which can be solved using binary search. 
 
2) Problem: Graph Disappearance Time 
You are given a graph which may or may not be connected. Each second, any node with at most 
one edge disappears, and all edges connected to it also disappear. Your task is to determine the 
minimum time required for the entire graph to disappear. 
Eg:  
Input: n = 5, edges = [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,5]] 
Output: 3 
 
Explanation: 
- Second 1: Nodes 1 and 5 disappear (each has 1 edge), edges [1,2] and [4,5] removed. 
- Second 2: Nodes 2 and 4 now have 1 edge each, disappear, edges [2,3] and [3,4] removed. 
- Second 3: Node 3 disappears. 
Minimum time for full disappearance = 3 
My thoughts:  This is a perfect medium qns, can be solved using topo-bfs in graph. 
 
A total of 89 students were shortlisted for the interview. The results were announced just one 
day before the interview giving me roughly 12 hours to cram a month’s worth of prep. 
 
 
Round 1 (Technical round 1): 
The interview kicked off with the usual self-intro. As expected, he dove straight into my resume 
projects, skills, everything I had put down. The first part was basically a deep dive into my ML 
project: 
• What does it do? 
• How did I implement it? 
• Which model did I use and why? 
• Why did u choose  this? 
• And, of course, the results. 
The interviewer didn’t stop there; he went through all my other projects with the same level of 
curiosity. He also asked, how I implemented “Interest based pair pooling” which I have done in 
my project (like pairing some anonymous users to interact based on interest, similar to omegle 
but with filters). It was like a rapid-fire quiz: one question, one-line answer, bam, immediate 
follow-up. But somehow, I survived. 
Then came a simple DSA question: reverse the words in a sentence. Easy right? I went from 
brute force to optimal solution, and he hit me with a follow-up: “Now reverse the letters inside 
the words”. Literally twisting the whole string-reversal logic!! I answered that too. 
Suddenly, the Tech round felt more like a funny HR one. He asked about my native place, MLA 
there , obviously we discussed about TVK and some politics. Later, He asked about SAAS, which 
I had on my resume. I ended up explaining  Techofes, Agni in detail ... felt like a proud moment 
as an Organizer. We spent more time in this part. 
Then context switching happened in real life HR qns again turned into Technical qns where it 
was left off, he threw in some OS questions: system calls, CPU scheduling, scheduling types and 
he asked me to explain FCFS, Priority and Round Robin Scheduling and other related concepts.. 
He also asked some cool things about cpp, asked me to explain about pointers, its advantages 
and its limitations. He also asked much more OS questions. 
Finally, the round ended with a puzzle: I have three jugs => 5L, 8L, and 12L. How do I measure 
exactly 6L with minimum swap operations? At the end, the interviewer asked: “Do you have 
any questions for me?” I didn’t ask him any. But U guys Don’t skip it. Ask meaningful questions 
(about role, team culture, learning opportunities, etc.). It creates a positive impression. 
After this round, I was shortlisted for the next stage. Out of all the participants, around 25 
students were shortlisted for Technical Round 2. 
Round-2: 
This round had two interviewers — one virtual, one physical. The round started with me  
introduced myself. And then they asked 
First questions: 
• “Why CSE even though you were Bio-Math in 12th?”. 
• “Do you like DBMS?” …uh, really? How else do they expect me to answer? I said yes. 
Then came DBMS deep dive: 
• He asked, “Have you backed up any DB data?”, I clearly said no, still he questioned 
about DBMS backup, then asked about sharding, horizontal vs vertical sharding, shard 
keys, how to shard and its usage and lot questions on these two topics. 
• Then asked normalization, anomalies, why we need it, all normal forms with examples. 
At 3rd normal form, he threw me a mini-SQL task: get student names studying CSE 
across student and department tables. 
▪ I did a join query; he tried to trick me: “Why are you joining the tables? Now 
only you have split it! Still join requires? Then what is the need of that 
splitting” 
▪ I calmly explained: advantages and disadvantages of normalization and also 
showed him a subquery version too. 
Next, OS: 
• He asked about multithreading, parallelism, concurrency, multiprocessing and plenty 
of questions as follow - ups. 
• He asked, “what would exactly happen at context switching", about PCB etc.,. 
• He also asked, “You have pc of single core, and u want to run a program which requires 
multiple core what your computer do?”, I answered with time-slicing, context switching, 
multiprocessing also said “due to absence of parallelism the performance might be 
poor”. 
Next, DS: 
• He asked about hashing, Open addressing, closed addressing, chaining, linear & 
quadratic probing — basically my brain went on DSA overdrive. 
• He asked, “You have arr of size 10, but record of 10000, how do u hash and handle

• Then comes the classic: “What’s the last DS you learned?” this is a tricky question, 
knowing he would questions in that, I still answered  him as Trie, ended up explaining 
what is trie and what 's its real-world application and simulated a search operation, 
auto completion in trie.

nodes are operators. And a simple modification of  postorder traversal can do the magic

• He also asked me about heap and told me to explain its underlying concepts and told to 
implement it.

Next OOPS and CPP specific qns:

problem and asked me “How do I solve that?” and he asked me to explain virtual 
keyword in detail. Also, he asked about overriding in both Java and CPP. 
• Later they asked declaring array of size 5 and accessing arr[6] in both java and Cpp and 
asked, "what will happen in both scenario?”. 
HR + casual chat: 
• Later some HR questions ,here too... SAAS helped me to tackle some HR classical 
question like “How do I resolve team conflict?”. 
• Found out the virtual guy was from MIT, He went on to share his college days 
experience  and suddenly I realized this part of the interview was longer than all the 
tough questions combined.  
• At the end, the interviewers asked: “Do you have any questions?”, I flipped the script 
and asked: “Where do I see myself at SAP in 5 years?”, they passionately answered 
(good guy, right?). I literally asked three to four questions as  follow ups.  
• And At last, I asked about growth opportunities at SAP, got some solid insights. 
Fortunately, I was selected to the next round. 
Round-3 HR Round: 
Finally, the most awaited HR round happened. The guy was literally super chill. He started with 
the usual family background and some personal questions. Then came the classic: “Why did you 
switch from Bio-Math to CSE?” yep, third time today. I straight-up said, “Sir, this is the third 
time I’m answering this today!” He just smiled… and of course, he asked the same qns anyway. 
Then came the ultimate twister: He asked me, “From all your life experience, give me an idea 
that will succeed right now, without any investment i.e no time, money, effort, or skills. That 
must work at that second at that instant.”, I asked what he meant as success in that qns .He 
said, “Anything!”. I was like “Wait!... what kind of trap is this?”, I tried some random answers, 
but he is not satisfied, At that point, my brain officially tiried outsourced the question to 
ChatGPT, but unfortunately, no internet access in interviews. My brain threw a 404: Idea Not 
Found error. no more ideas clicked, just little more frustration.... Anyway, that question 
basically owned the entire HR round. And at last, the round ended with me, completely 
drained and spitting out random ideas, just trying to escape from that trap. 
Finally, I got selected into that company. HURRAH!!!! 
Disclaimer: 
All the above has been toned down to a professional version, removing the humorous elements 
of my interview. In reality, my interview was much more humorous. Honestly, it felt like all the 
rounds were HR-oriented, from the very first technical round to the last. I think, it is up to us to 
decide the flow and direction of the interview.  I cracked many jokes in this interview and 
surprisingly that worked too.. Pls  Don’t try this at home, these kinds of stunts are depends 
entirely on interviewers and their personalities, Thank God I had some cool guys. 
My Advice: 
My advice to juniors is to approach interviews with a calm and confident mindset. Be chill, 
Don’t panic, interviews aren’t boss battles in Soul Games, they’re just conversations (well, 
mostly). Avoid overthinking, and instead, focus on showcasing your passion and consistency, as 
both are key to long-term success . Be thorough with your resume and ensure you are well-
prepared to discuss every detail you have included. Confidence, clarity, and composure will 
help you stand out. Finally, remember that not being selected is not the end there will always 
be more opportunities ahead. Choose your company wisely. 
All the best Guys!!! 
Sakthipriyan V,  
CEG – CSE’ 26, 
E-Mail: sakthipriyan417@gmail.com

text:
EUCLOID INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE (Intern + FTE) 
About the Company: 
Eucloid is a premier Data/AI organization that leverages state-of-the-art AI and Data 
Science technologies to deliver high-impact projects. It creates business-critical solutions for 
Fortune-100 clients and industry leaders across various sectors, including Hi-tech, BFSI, D2C, 
Healthcare, SaaS, and Retail. Their deep partnerships with leading platforms like Databricks, 
Google Cloud Platform, and Adobe enables to build and deploy cutting-edge data products 
and massive-scale data platforms. Their world-class team, composed of professionals from 
top-tier institutes and leading global organizations, thrives in an environment that prioritizes 
growth, innovation, and excellence 
 
Total Rounds:  
1. Online Assessment  
2. Technical Interview  
3. Interview with Leadership Team (Technical + HR) 
 
 
1. Online Assessment - Virtual : 
 
The first round was an online assessment consisting of 30 multiple-choice questions 
and 2 coding problems, designed to evaluate candidates’ analytical, logical reasoning, 
quantitative aptitude, and problem-solving skills. 
 
 
• Logical reasoning 
• Quantitative aptitude 
• 2 Coding questions -String, Array manipulation -Easy level 
2.Technical interview: 
 
              All the candidates who attended the online test were invited to the pre-placement 
talk, where the company representatives provided an overview of Eucloid Data Solutions. 
The session also featured a virtual appearance by the founder, and we had the opportunity 
to interact with our seniors currently working at Eucloid. 
The list of shortlisted students for the technical round was announced at 11:45 AM 
— out of 150 participants, 32 were selected. My technical interview was scheduled virtually 
through CUIC at 12:30 PM. 
The technical interview lasted for around 30 minutes. It began with a brief self-
introduction followed by a detailed discussion about my projects. Since one of my projects 
was deployed, the interviewer asked about the tech stack used, the purpose of using Docker, 
and other related aspects. She then assessed my SQL knowledge with conceptual questions 
such as: 
• 
What is the difference between RANK and DENSE_RANK? 
• 
What is the difference between DROP and TRUNCATE? 
• 
What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL? 
• 
What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING clauses? 
I explained each question with suitable examples. 
Next, I was asked to solve a few SQL queries: 
1. From the Employees table, find departments where the average salary is greater than 
70,000. 
2. Write queries to: 
o Find the top 3 highest spending customers. 
o Fetch product IDs without duplicates. 
o Retrieve the 2nd highest purchase amount. 
I wrote and explained my answers clearly to the interviewer. 
The session then moved to the coding section, where I was asked to type and run two 
programs using an online compiler with different test cases: 
1. Majority Element – (Moore Voting Algo) 
Input: [2,2,1,1,1,2,2] → Output: 2  
2. Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters – (Sliding Window +Hashing) 
 Input: "abcabcbb" → Output: "abc" 
I explained my logic step-by-step and demonstrated the correct outputs. Towards the 
end, I had the opportunity to ask a few questions about the role, and she responded with 
detailed technical insights. 
With that, the first round concluded, and a total of 10 candidates were shortlisted 
for the next round. 
 
3. Interview with Leadership Team (Technical + HR): 
The final round took place after a day and was conducted by the Senior Director. He 
began the interview by directly diving into my full-stack project and asked me to share my 
screen since the project was deployed online. It was a great opportunity to demonstrate my 
technical and presentation skills. 
I walked him through each page of the project, explaining the purpose, design logic, 
and technical reasoning behind every feature. He showed keen interest in the functionalities 
and was particularly impressed by the customized features we had implemented. 
After the project discussion, he gave me a Camel and Banana Puzzle. I used MS Paint 
to illustrate my approach with a diagrammatic explanation. Before I could reach the final 
answer, he mentioned that he was satisfied with my reasoning and problem-solving 
approach. 
Towards the end, he invited me to ask questions, and I took the opportunity to learn 
more about the company’s operations, clients, and the type of data-driven solutions they 
provide. 
This round lasted for about 25 minutes and went very smoothly. 
 
The results were announced two days later, 8 were selected. I’m delighted to share that I 
was one among them. 
 
RESOURCES: 
• 
A2Z DSA Sheet by Striver – for complete data structures and algorithms preparation. 
• 
NeetCode 150 – for practicing standard coding problems and improving problem-
solving skills. 
• 
Core Subjects – DBMS, OOPS, Operating Systems, and Computer Networks (refer 
from GeeksforGeeks). 
• 
Be prepared to explain your projects in depth, covering your technical stack, design 
choices, and challenges faced. 
• 
Dry-run DSA questions using pen and paper to strengthen your conceptual clarity. 
• 
Maintain concise notes for last-minute revision before the interview day. 
• 
Conduct Mock interviews 
• 
Above all, stay confident and face every round with a positive mindset. 
 
POINTS TO SAY: 
This was my 10th interview, and the journey wasn’t easy. I faced several rejections 
— sometimes because I couldn’t provide an optimal solution to a hard coding question, 
sometimes due to topics outside my resume, and a few times for no clear reason at all. I’ve 
been rejected in the first round, the second round, and even after clearing all rounds . 
There were moments of disappointment, self-doubt, and frustration, but every 
rejection taught me something valuable. 
 
 
 
To everyone preparing — remember this: 
“You didn’t come this far to only  come this far.” 
Keep believing in yourself, trust the process, and never give up. 
There’s always something greater waiting ahead  
 
Reach out: 
SANDHIP SURIYA K S, 
2022506100, 
Information Technology, 
MIT-Anna University. 
mailto:sandhipks@gmail.com 
LinkedIn

ORACLE INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
  
Oracle Financial Services Software (OFSS) is a wholly owned subsidiary of 
Oracle Corporation that delivers enterprise-class IT solutions to banks and 
financial institutions worldwide. Headquartered in Mumbai, India, OFSS 
provides a comprehensive suite of products for core banking, digital 
banking, risk and compliance management, analytics, and payments. 
The company combines Oracle’s global technology leadership with deep 
financial-services domain expertise, helping institutions modernize their 
infrastructure, improve customer experience, and meet regulatory 
requirements. With clients in more than 145 countries, OFSS offers both 
product development and IT services/consulting, supporting banks of all 
sizes in their digital-transformation journeys. 
 
Role Offered: Associate Application Developer (OFSS) 
 
Selection Process: 
1.Online Test 
2. PPT @CUIC  
2.Technical Interview  
3.Techno-Managerial 
 
Online Test: 
The online test was conducted on the HackerRank platform and consisted 
of 3 sections: 
1. Technical Questions 
• 
Covered Core CS Fundamentals: Computer Networks (CN), OOPS, 
DBMS, and Operating Systems (OS). 
• 
Level: Easy to Medium. 
2. Aptitude & English Knowledge 
• 
Focused on basic aptitude skills and English comprehension. 
3. Coding Questions (2 LeetCode Medium-level Problems) 
Problem 1: Find Special Nodes in Tree 
Problem Statement: 
You are given a tree with tree_nodes nodes. A node is considered special if 
it lies at the end of the tree diameter. 
• 
The diameter of a tree is defined as the longest path between any two 
nodes, measured in edges. 
• 
Return a binary list res[] of size tree_nodes where: 
res[i] = 1 if the (i+1)-th node is special 
res[i] = 0 otherwise 
Example: 
Input: 
tree_nodes = 7 
tree_from = [1, 2, 3, 3, 1, 1] 
tree_to   = [2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] 
Output: 
[0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1] 
 
Problem 2: Detect High Load Windows 
Problem Statement: 
• 
You are given a list of system loads recorded each minute, a window 
size, and a threshold. 
• 
For each minute, compute the average load of the last windowSize 
minutes (including the current minute). 
• 
Return all minutes where this average is strictly greater than the 
threshold. 
Example: 
Input: 
loads = [8, 14, 5, 17] 
windowSize = 2 
threshold = 10 
Output: 
[1, 3] 
 
18 students were called for interviews @CUIC 
 
 
 
Technical Round: 
1. Introduction 
2. Project Discussion (ML & NLP Project + Full-Stack Project) 
3. Questions asked: 
• 
What is Cosine Similarity with examples? 
→ Explained with an example from my project: 
It measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors. 
Used in NLP to measure similarity between text embeddings. 
Gave example with some sentences and finding similarity 
 
• 
Explain how TextRank Algorithm works. 
→ I explained graph-based ranking for NLP tasks like extractive 
summarization. 
Represent sentences as nodes. 
Edges = similarity scores between sentences. 
Apply PageRank to rank sentences → select top sentences as 
summary. 
• 
What is BERT? Why Pegasus? 
→ BERT is a transformer-based bidirectional encoder pre-trained on 
Masked Language Modeling and Next Sentence Prediction. 
Pegasus is better for abstractive summarization, trained on massive 
news & web data, with gap-sentence generation objective. 
They asked on the architecture of these models 
• 
Preprocessing methods used? Why? 
Tokenization 
Stop-word removal 
Lemmatization 
Lowercasing 
 
• 
Why did you choose this project? Use cases? 
 
• 
Web Services flow of data with code (React & Express code 
snippets):  
→ Explained with request-response cycle: 
Client makes a request (async/await used for non-blocking). 
Server processes and sends JSON/XML response. 
Example with fetch, async, await in frontend to handle 
asynchronous calls. 
Code snippets were asked for the whole cycle 
 
• 
JWT – structure, need, and workflow (Code Snippets were asked): 
Header, Payload, Signature. 
Workflow: 
User logs in → token signed and issued. 
Client stores token (localStorage). 
On every request → token sent for validation. 
Server verifies with secret key. 
 
• 
Difference on Authentication vs Authorization: 
Authentication → verifying who you are. 
Authorization → verifying what you are allowed to do. 
• 
What is Open Authentication (OAuth): 
Standard protocol for third-party authentication (e.g., login with 
Google). 
• 
Why MongoDB for your project? 
→ Schema flexibility: post → comments → child comments → n nested 
comments → best represented as nested documents in NoSQL. 
→ Avoids complex joins of SQL. 
 
• 
Why combination of SQL + NoSQL for multimedia apps? 
SQL: Structured, relational metadata. 
NoSQL: Unstructured data (images, videos). 
Explained Multimedia Database Management System 
 
HTTP vs HTTPS – Why HTTPS is secure? 
HTTPS uses SSL/TLS encryption, ensures confidentiality, 
integrity, and authentication. 
 
 
• 
Self-rating in CP: I rated myself 8.5/10.Below was the question asked 
.. 
You are asked to design a data model to represent a large, dynamic 
class hierarchy with the following properties. Code implementation 
of the entire class structure and functions was asked. 
 
Root Class: 
  
There is a single base class called Node. 
Alphabetical Branching: 
  
Each class can have up to 26 direct subclasses, one for each 
letter A through Z. 
  
Each of those subclasses can themselves have A–Z subclasses, 
and so on to arbitrary  depth 
Inheritance Rules: 
    
Multiple inheritance is allowed, but the design must prevent the 
“diamond problem” (no class should inherit from the same ancestor 
along two different paths).In other words, a class may inherit from 
more than one parent, but the resulting graph must remain a directed 
acyclic graph (DAG) with no converging cycles. 
Dynamic Updates: 
  
Classes (nodes) may be added, removed, or modified at any 
time. 
  
The structure must handle these operations efficiently without 
downtime. 
Class Metadata: 
   
Each class can define its own set of methods, properties, and 
other metadata. 
 
Required Operations 
1. Fast Retrieval 
Quickly locate a specific class by its full hierarchical path (e.g., Node → 
C → F → …). 
2. Efficient Search 
Support lookups by name or prefix (e.g., “find all classes whose path 
starts with AB”). 
Handle queries such as “find all subclasses that override method X.” 
3. Traversal and Batch Processing 
Enumerate or update all subclasses within any subtree or inheritance 
branch. 
4. Dynamic Maintenance 
Support real-time insertion, deletion, and re-linking of inheritance 
relationships  
 
My Approach & Code was written in Python: 
My solution for the  system as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), where each 
class is a graph node. A node stores its name, its methods, links to its 
parents and children, and also a cached set of all ancestors for fast 
validation. To make class lookup efficient, we maintain a Trie (prefix tree) 
where each path like "A/B/C" maps directly to the corresponding class node. 
This gives fast lookups by path and also makes prefix-based searches easy. 
To prevent the diamond problem, whenever we add a new inheritance link, 
we check two things: 
1. No cycles are allowed (a class cannot become its own ancestor). 
2. No ancestor overlap between parents—if two parents share a common 
ancestor, adding this link would create a diamond, so we reject it. 
3. The structure supports dynamic updates: 
• 
Adding a class means creating a new node, inserting it into the trie, 
and linking it under a default root.Adding inheritance requires running 
the cycle and overlap checks, then updating ancestor sets.Removing a 
class just means unlinking it from its parents/children and updating 
descendants. 
• 
For retrieval and search, path lookups are done by following the trie, 
prefix-based searches are just trie traversals, and traversals like 
BFS/DFS can be run directly on the graph of nodes. 
Graph (DAG) + Trie + Ancestor checks 
 
Techno-Managerial Round: 
1. Self-introduction 
2. Pegasus Transformer – use cases, training data, architecture. 
3. How companies maintain data security – I explained RAG (Retrieval 
Augmented Generation) + access controls. 
4. About OFSS – main products (learned from PPT). 
5. Software Design Principles – my approach to problem-solving. 
6. Project explanations. 
7. Knowledge rating in Java & Python (with follow-up coding questions). 
8. Preference: AI or Development? 
9. Basic ML Concepts – e.g., overfitting, embeddings. 
10.How LLM’s understand semantic of a sentence? 
11.AI in Finance/Banking – explained fraud detection pipelines. 
     12.Spring in banking sectors – modular, secure, scalable. 
     13.Advantages of Spring Boot – auto-configuration, embedded servers, 
microservice-ready. 
     14.Web Services in Java – I wrote Java Servlet code for request/response. 
15.Testing methods & bug fixing – unit testing, integration testing, 
regression. For user-side errors → replicate & trace logs. 
     16.Deadline handling – standard deadline scenario questions 
     17.Learning new languages/concepts – start with basics documentation 
→ hands-on practice → build mini-projects. 
18.Future of AI – discussed industry shift towards AI + Finance + 
Governance. 
    19.Time given to ask interviewer questions. 
Final Thoughts 
Overall, the experience tested end-to-end skills: 
- Core CS fundamentals 
- Coding & problem-solving 
- System design thinking 
- Project depth in ML, NLP & Full-Stack 
- Managerial & communication aspects 
 
Preparation Tips: 
1. Understand Core CS Fundamentals Thoroughly 
o Revise OOPS, DBMS, Operating Systems, and Computer 
Networks. 
o Focus on concepts like transactions, concurrency, deadlock 
handling, HTTP/HTTPS, authentication vs authorization, etc. 
2. Coding & Problem-Solving 
o Practice medium-level coding problems on platforms like 
LeetCode, HackerRank, and GeeksforGeeks. 
o Focus on trees, graphs, sliding windows, dynamic 
programming, and design problems. 
o Learn to explain your approach, time/space complexity, and 
edge cases clearly. 
3. System Design & Projects 
o Be ready to explain your projects in depth, including your 
technical choices, challenges, and trade-offs. 
o Prepare to design data models and class hierarchies. 
o Understand web services, API flow, and authentication 
mechanisms like JWT. 
4. Machine Learning & NLP Concepts 
o Know transformer-based models (BERT, Pegasus) and how 
they work. 
o Be able to explain preprocessing techniques, embeddings. 
5. Programming Languages & Frameworks 
o Brush up on Java and Python, including syntax, data structures, 
and libraries used in your projects. 
o Be ready to write small snippets of web service code (servlets, 
async requests, REST APIs). 
6. Behavioral & Managerial Questions 
o Be prepared to discuss deadline management, learning new 
concepts, debugging approaches, and teamwork. 
o Practice explaining your approach to problem-solving and 
system design clearly. 
7. Mock Interviews & Explanation Skills 
o Conduct mock interviews focusing on explaining your 
reasoning clearly. 
o Be able to justify technical choices and explain project 
workflows concisely. 
8. Time Management & Confidence 
o Allocate time wisely during coding rounds; read the problem 
carefully before coding. 
o Stay calm, explain your thought process step-by-step, and don’t 
rush the solution. 
9. Documentation & Notes 
o Maintain project notes, diagrams, and pseudocode to help 
recall details during the interview. 
o Highlight your contributions and technical depth for each 
project. 
      10.Stay Updated 
o Keep track of latest trends in ML/NLP, AI, and cloud 
technologies relevant to your projects and the company 
domain. 
 
“The harder you work for something, the greater 
you’ll feel when you achieve it.”

Citicorp – Intern Interview Experience 
 
About the company  
Citi is a global bank that offers a variety of financial services, including 
banking, credit cards, and investment solutions. It operates in more than 160 
countries and values innovation, diversity, and teamwork, creating an inclusive 
workplace where employees can grow. Citi’s mission is to help people, businesses, 
and communities achieve their financial goals. 
Role: Personal Banking & Wealth Management Technology (PBWMT) 
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month  
Duration of internship: 8-10 weeks 
 
Selection Process : 
 
The Selection process had 3 rounds. 
1. Online test 
2. Technical Interview 
3. HR Interview 
 
1.Online test  
This online test had 4 sections basically. 
 
1st section  -  Numerical and Logical Reasoning 
 
2nd section -  Quantitative aptitude 
 
3rd section -  MCQs on Data Structures and Algorithm 
 
4th section -  Coding round 
 
First section contained questions from permutations and combinations, ratio 
and proportions, distributions, finding odd one out. One of the questions that I 
could recollect is the classic garland problem. It goes like “Number of ways in 
which ‘m’ different flowers can be strung to form a garland so that ‘n’ flowers are 
never separated are? “. 
Second section had questions like evaluating the expression and problems on 
profit and loss, discounts, time and work, speed and distance , LCM and HCF. 
 
The third section aims to test the candidate’s understanding of programming 
concepts and DSA. It included questions on trees, sorting, recursive algorithms, 
memory management, bit manipulation, binary arithmetic, function overloading, 
and pointer manipulation. 
 There were also questions where the output of a code snippet had to be 
selected. Apart from that we had questions with two code snippets and I was 
required to choose the correct conclusion based on both.  
 
Fourth section had 2 coding questions. Both the questions were based on 
string manipulation. They were straightforward and quite easy. This section had a 
40-minute time frame, and we were allowed to switch between the questions as 
needed. Whereas for the first 3 sections, we were given barely 1 minute for each 
question to solve.  
 
In this test, the first three sections were crucial, as solving the aptitude 
questions in 1 minute was very challenging and this played a major role in 
determining our selection for the next round, as almost all candidates were able to 
solve the questions in the fourth section correctly. 
 
 
2. Technical Interview (30.09.2024) 
 
A total of 25 students from CEG and MIT have been shortlisted for technical 
interview. Both HR and Technical interviews were in-person interviews which held 
at CUIC, CEG.   
 
The interviews were conducted across 5-6 rooms, with students being 
randomly assigned to each. Some rooms had a single interviewer, while others had 
two. I was interviewed by a panel of two interviewers. 
 
I entered the room carrying three copies of my resume, as instructed. After 
greeting the interviewers, I handed them my resume. The interview began with the 
interviewers introducing themselves, followed by them asking me to introduce 
myself. 
 I shared my educational journey, starting from my 10th grade to my current 
studies in college. I also highlighted my involvement in various clubs and 
organizations, emphasizing my roles and positions to showcase my leadership and 
teamwork skills. Be prepared with this self-introduction as it makes the first 
impression.  
 
After reviewing my resume, they asked me to explain the DBMS project I 
worked on in my 4th semester. It was an NGO Management project aimed at 
bringing together various NGOs to help people in need. I explained the project in 
detail, including the tech stacks used for frontend and backend development, as 
well as how it benefited the end users. I was also told to describe the structure of 
the database created. 
Since our team was very attached to the project, we named it “PRANAVAM” 
which reflects our motive of love in helping the needy and designed logo too. I 
mentioned this, and they appreciated it. They also asked about any unique or 
special features that set our project apart from others. 
They asked me about SDLC and the process of creating a product. At the 
time of the interview, I was in my 5th semester, and we hadn't yet studied "Software 
Engineering". However, I was familiar with a few basic concepts related to the topic. 
I believe it's good to be honest with the interviewer about not knowing all the 
details and that they may help you. So, I answered these questions based on my 
basic knowledge of the subject. They also asked me about my favourite subject, to 
which I replied DBMS. They then asked if I could write queries for any type of data 
retrieval, and I confidently responded with a yes. 
They asked me how the repetitive task of a person who manually verifies 
data correctness and inserts it into a table could be improved. I explained that 
writing traditional programs can only handle a limited set of predefined conditions, 
which reduces their overall effectiveness for dynamic and complex data validation. 
Therefore, I suggested using machine learning (ML) to automate data validation, 
allowing dynamic checks based on learned patterns before inserting the data into 
tables. Apart from this ,they asked me few questions on OOPS. 
In the end, they asked me what advice I would give to the juniors of my 
college. I told them that, apart from encouraging them to develop their technical 
skills, I would also strongly recommend joining various clubs and associations, as 
college is the most important platform for developing social and networking skills. I 
also asked about their journey with the company.  Finally, I thanked them and 
shook hands. 
 
2. HR Interview (30.09.2024) 
 
From the 25 students who were qualified for technical round, 12 were 
selected for the next round (HR). 
 
It was a one-to-one interview. Since he had a copy of my resume on his 
laptop, I didn’t hand over my resume. He began with his introduction, and then I 
followed with my own introduction. When I mentioned my involvement in a dance 
club, he asked about the style of dance we practice and the number of members in 
the club. I shared details about the events we participated in and the prizes we won. 
 
As I mentioned my participation in track events as an athlete in my resume, 
he asked about my main sport and my time record for the 100-meter sprint, as I 
told him I was a sprinter. He also inquired about the places we travelled to for 
sports meets. He seemed quite impressed by my versatility, being active in different 
fields. 
 
When he encouraged me to ask questions, I inquired about the work culture 
at Citi. He highlighted about various sports and cultural activities within the 
company and shared his involvement in sports.  
Later, he asked me a stress-based question: "What if we don’t hire you?" 
I replied that while I would feel disappointed for some time, I would learn from the 
experience, and start preparing for my next interview. I added that this mindset of 
resilience and adaptability is something my experience in sports has taught me. 
Out of 12 people, 6 were selected for PBWM intern role, and by god’s grace I 
was one among them. 
 
All the best! 
 
S SREENITHIKA (2022-2026) CSE (Q) 
 
Feel free to contact me! 
Email id:  sreenithikas@gmail.com 
Linkedin:  http://www.linkedin.com/in/sreenithikasaravanakumar

APPIAN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE (INTERNSHIP) 
 
NAME: Sachin Abhinav 
DEPT: CSE 
MAIL: sachinabhinavjb27@gmail.com 
CONTACT: 7418356711 
Batch: 2022 – 2026 
 
Round 1: Online Assessment Test 
 
The test was conducted on an online platform Glider AI platform. There were three 
coding questions. 
 
Question 1: Josephus Problem  
 
Given start person and  k, you have to print the order in which people die. Refer to the 
geeksforgeeks page. 
 
Question 2: Make Queue 
Inputs: 
 
You are given a group name with persons in each group. You are also given an array of 
strings with name of persons standing in a line. 
Output: 
You should return an array of strings (name of persons).  Array is constructed using 
following rules: 
i) 
If a person belonging to a group is standing in the line, then all other persons 
in that line belonging to the same group move behind him. Basically just 
making the people of same group adjacent to each other in the order in which 
the input line is. 
ii) 
After arranging using rule i), if you find all the persons belonging to a group is 
present then you have to replace all the person’s name with their group. 
Question 3: 
Given breadcrumbs for a website as an array of array of strings, Make a tree out of it. 
Sample input: 
arr = [[‘Main’, ‘Dashboard’, ‘Settings’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Uploads’], [‘Main’, 
‘Dashboard’, ‘Products’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Remove], [‘Main’, ‘Manager, ‘Work’], [‘Main’, 
‘Manager,  ‘Review’]] 
make a tree out of it by linking as follows: 
Main 
 
-Dashboard 
 
 
-Setting 
 
 
-Products 
 
-Profile 
 
 
-Uploads 
 
 
-Remove 
 
-Manager 
 
 
-Work 
 
 
-Review 
 
I was able to solve Question 2 and Question 3 (partial). I along with 21 candidates was 
shortlisted for next round 
 
 
Interview Phase 
 
The interview was conducted in CUIC. It consist of 3 rounds. 
 
Round1 (Technical – 25 mins): 
 
The interview starts with my self intro. Then he asked me how I would choose 
candidates, I were in the role of selecting candidates. I say, “I would check the candidates 
Computer fundamentals and his aptitude along with problem solving abilities”. Then he tested 
me on OOPS, DBMS and web technology. 
 
 
He asks me to find the object with less weight out of 8 similar looking objects but to 
use the balance weighing scale only twice. 
 
He then asked me Two sum problem (first problem of leetcode)  
 
He also tested my sorting algorithm techniques 
 
Round 2 (More like a HR – 10 mins): 
 
This interview is more like a HR round. Only technical question asked was the time 
complexity of Quick sort. He also questioned me whether I would do Masters or not. 
 
Round 3 (HR – 5mins): 
 
This round lasted for less than 7 minutes. He asked me why he should hire me. Then 
some questions on my family background was put up. Finally he asked questions on my project 
in the resume. 
 
In the eve results were announced. I was one of the 5 selected candidates. 
 
Tips: 
 
Learn basics of OOPS and your 4th semester DBMS concepts 
 
Know whatever in your resume 
 
General Tips: 
 
Be Confident 
 
Be Honest with yourself 
 
Don’t think about results, keep working on it…

Wells Fargo (Internship) 
Batch: 2019 - 2023 
Name of the Company: Wells Fargo  
About: 
Wells Fargo is a leading global financial services company headquartered in San Francisco (United States). Wells 
Fargo has offices in over 30 countries and territories. Business outside of the U.S. mostly focuses on providing 
banking services for large corporate, government and financial institution clients. They have worldwide expertise 
and services to help their customers improve earnings, manage risk, and develop opportunities in the global 
marketplace. 
            
My Interview Experience : 
Number of Rounds : 4  
1. Online Test  
2. Technical round  
3. Managerial round 
4. HR round 
           
Round 1 : Aptitude & Coding 
It took place in AMCAT platform. It consists of three sections, namely verbal aptitude, business aptitude 
and the coding oart.  
In verbal aptitude, questions were generally based on synonyms, choose the alternate phrases and some 
comprehensive questions. 
Next, comes business aptitude, where the questions are mainly based on stocks and business, data 
interpretations based on graphs and pie-charts. So, the key factor here is time management, be quick 
enough to read those large explanatory passages and understand exactly what is asked to interpret.  
Then, the third section consists of two coding questions which were bit easy. Also you have to code both 
these questions from scratch (driver code). 
And the questions that I had were, 
1 . Given a string of length N, which contains ‘1’ , ‘0’ and ‘?’ . Return all the binary strings that can be 
formed by replacing the ‘?’ with ‘0’ or ‘1’. 
Generate all binary strings from given pattern - GeeksforGeeks (Backtracking approach) 
2 . A balloon game is being played among people numbered from 1 to n in circle. Given an integer k which 
is the number of balloon pass, after which the person should be eliminated. The condition is before 
elimination of a particular person, the balloon should be passed to the next one. Find the winner ? (one 
who remains at last) 
Josephus problem | Set 1 (A O(n) Solution) - GeeksforGeeks (Similar technique) 
     
I was able to pass all test cases passed for both the questions. 
A total of 30 students were selected to the next round. 
 
General Tip : Try to answer as many aptitude questions as possible, which almost confirms your opportunity for the 
next round. Basic math and quantitative knowledge is sufficient. In case of any practice, you can refer IndiaBix . 
Coding Tip : Have a regular practice in  geeksforgeeks and leetcode. Also have a look at past coding problems of the 
company you are attending, that might be helpful. Get acquainted with lengthy problem statements. Be prepared to 
code from scratch, as we are always get used to code only the functionalities. 
       
Round 2 : Technical Interview (1 hr) 
The interview was conducted on Zoom. The interviewer first asked to introduce myself. She then asked me 
to share my screen and open any IDE that I have.  
She then gave me two coding questions, of basic level. The first coding question was asked to   Program to 
convert a given number to words and the interviewer was satisfied with my solution. Then the next one was 
Find the occurrences of digit d in the range [0..n] , after coding both of these questions, she moved on to some 
basics.  
She then moved on to some basics. Some of the questions on OOPS were encapsulation vs data 
abstraction, overloading vs overriding and polymorphism. Then there were questions on DBMS like what is 
RDBMS ,hashing etc. She then asked some basic Data Structures questions, and some sorting algorithms 
like Radix sort, Insertion sort. 
Atlast, the interviewer asked whether I have any questions (Always ask some questions to show that you 
are interested to build a conversation). I asked about the work-life balance, and the domain in which we 
would work our projects etc.. 
Overall the interview went very well and I was shortlisted for the next round. 
     
General Tip : Focus on DSA, DBMS, OOPS, OS. Always be positive and confident in answering your 
questions. Mention only the skills that your strong enough in your resume. 
     
 
Round 3 : Managerial Interview (40 mins) 
This round was also conducted on Zoom. I was interviewed by Senior Manager. He was very friendly, and 
asked to introduce myself. He then asked how was the day and what are your take aways from previous 
round.  
Before going on to my resume, he said that this round will be only on projects and some general talk and 
not based on technical aspect as it was in the previous round. Then I was asked to explain one of my 
project which was done in group. So, I went on with my DBMS project. After explaining my project, he 
asked some questions like how did we split our work as a team, conflict between friends, handling 
suggestions of everyone as a team, a problem at which all of you were stuck etc.. 
Then he moved on to my hobbies and we had a fun discussion around cricket (hobby).  
Then it was my turn to ask any questions that I have. So, I made use of this opportunity and asked him 
various questions like in what way Wells Fargo uniquely handled to overcome this pandemic over online, 
which one do you prefer btw work from home or office, tech stacks used in the company, and some 
questions about our internship. 
Overall it was a very good experience. After this I had an HR call. 
General Tip : Be bold and enthusiastic, and don’t hesitate to ask any questions. Have an idea or 
explanation of each and every topics of your resume that you have mentioned. Refer interview 
experiences in GFG, so that you would be well prepared. 
 
Round 4 : HR Interview (5 mins) 
This was a telephonic interview. He asked me to introduce myself. Then he asked me to give an overview 
on my projects. He then asked where I’m from and asked about relocation choices. 
Finally, 12 students were selected for internship. 
      
Preparation Tips :  
 Try to kick start your preparation atleat 2 months before with CODE-ACM materials, that gives you 
the base and exposure to variety of topics. If you ain’t started, nothing is too late, start of with a 
fresh mind. 
             
 Best practice is you ensure that you are good enough in a particular topic, and then jump into the 
next one. Most importantly, while solving a problem, don’t urge yourself unless you grasp the root 
cause of the problem and have a mind map to connect all those problems of same category, that 
will be the best effective way rather than solving many. 
             
           
 So CODE-ACM, gfg and leetcode will help you in your coding prep. And after completing all these, 
you can also try InterviewBit (has a standard set of problems in strings, DP, backtracking). Also for 
DP and backtracking you can get your doubts cleared by watching TECH DOSE - YouTube 
            
 For OOPS you can refer PREP INSTA and OOPs - InterviewBit  
            
 For DBMS you can have a look at DBMS - Interviewbit and DBMS - YouTube 
             
 For OS, Last Minute Notes – OS might help you during your preparation. 
 
 And finally, be strong in your Resume. 
     
 So atlast during your internship drive, before attending any interview of any company, have a look 
at the interview experience in gfg of that particular company, so that you would have a clear 
picture of the process. 
 
Take Aways :  
 Solve the problem whole heartedly by getting the logic behind of each problem you solve instead of 
urging to solve many. 
 Don’t have a mindset that interviewers are always there only to drill you. They always makes you 
feel comfortable and helps you in figuring out the solution. 
 Combine studies with your friend will be healthy helps you a lot during this journey. Don’t take any 
peer pressure. Always be positive and confident in any circumstances. 
 
Wishing you the best of luck in your future endeavours😇

BATCH: 2019-2013
FOR: Internship
NAME OF THE COMPANY:Wells Fargo International Solutions Private Limited,
Bangalore
ABOUT:
Wells Fargo & Company is an American multinational financial services company. They
provide banking, investment and mortgage products and services, as well as consumer
and commercial finance through more than 7,200 locations, more than 13,000 ATMs,
the internet (wellsfargo.com) and mobile banking.
NUMBER OF ROUNDS: 4
1. Online Test
2. Interview Round 1
3. Interview Round 2 (Senior Manager Round)
4. HR Round
ONLINE TEST:
This round consisted of three sections, conducted on the AMCAT platform.
First section was verbal aptitude. Questions like choose the correct meaning, fill in the
blank with appropriate words were asked. Second section was called business aptitude.
Questions were largely based on graph interpretations(pie charts, bar graphs, line
graphs) and also data interpretation, prerequisites regarding any business terms not
required. Third section consisted of two coding questions. Everyone got different sets of
questions for this section. Mostly the questions will be some commonly asked
questions, but it will be written in the form of a scenario, a paragraph per se. Do be
prepared to quickly grasp the constraints of the question.
The questions that I got -
1. Generate binary string
2. A variation of Find Minimum sum of product of two arrays where a 2D matrix was
given and I was required to find the minimum sum of product of all possible
pairs of arrays.
A total of 30 students were shortlisted from both CSE and IT.
INTERVIEW ROUND 1 (1h)
The interview was conducted on Zoom. The interviewer first asked to introduce myself.
He then asked me to share my screen and open notepad or any IDE. He then gave me a
coding problem (Number of overtakes | Practice Problems). I had suggested an O(n2)
solution and coded it. He asked me to optimize it, but I wasn’t able to. (PS - Do not ever
give up and say “I don’t know” to any answer, especially coding questions. Try to
approach it. The interviewer is mostly focussed on your approach rather than the final
solution).
He then proceeded to ask me Find missing element in a sorted array of consecutive
numbers. I began to suggest an O(n) solution to which he said he wanted the optimized
solution instead. I explained the Binary Search method. I wasn’t asked to code the final
solution. The interviewer then ended the interview by asking if I have any questions
(Always prepare some questions and keep. It shows that you are interested in the
position that you’re applying for). I asked him who in the organization would be
responsible for any security breaches that could happen.
INTERVIEW ROUND 2 (40 mins)
I thought I wasn’t shortlisted for the second round, as everyone I had asked had already
finished their second round. But I had received a call, around two hours after the first
round, asking me to attend the second round immediately.
The second round was also conducted on Zoom. I was greeted by the Senior Manager.
She first asked me to introduce myself. Then I was asked to say something about myself
that is not in my resume. I had told her about my strengths and weaknesses.
She then asked me about my hobbies. Some questions regarding a previous internship I
had done (PS: Be mindful of what you say in interviews as questions will often arise
from it.) I had mentioned something about the HTTP protocol. She asked me what it
is, and what other protocols I know.
I was then asked to write a function to check if a given string is a palindrome
(Palindrome String) . Then she asked me if I know radix sort, I said no (Do not lie in
interviews. If you don’t know something, politely admit.) She asked me to explain the
working of merge sort (Merge Sort). I wasn’t required to code it.
The interview ended with her asking if I had any questions. I asked about how a day
would look like for an intern at Wells Fargo.
HR ROUND (5 mins)
This round was a telephonic round. He asked me to first introduce myself. Then he
asked about my parents, where they’re from. He asked about my previous internship
too. Then the interview ended with him asking if I was willing to relocate.
TOPICS TO BE FOCUSSED:
●The usual important topics (DSA, DBMS, OOPS, OS) (Googling “Interview
questions on ____” gives a tonne of useful, consolidated resources that are
interview focussed)
●Ensure that you can provide an explanation for every single topic written in your
resume.
●Archives of the particular company on GFG give good insight into the general
difficulty level of questions asked, and also the number of rounds for the
particular company.
KEY TAKEAWAYS:
●Never panic if you don’t answer a couple of questions in the interview. The
interviewer is merely seeing how you would act in different situations.
●Have some interesting hobbies. (I had mentioned crochet, which was an
immediate hit with the interviewer in the second round)
●Always try to answer the questions asked. Instead of saying “I don’t know”, you
can say something like “I don’t know, but from whatever knowledge I have, I am
guessing …”.
●Prepare an introduction and practice it. Just winging it will never workout. It will
merely make a person look shabby, awkward and unprepared.
●Prepare answers for other non-tech questions also (What are your
strengths/weaknesses, Why do you want this internship, etc)
●Try to have a good rapport with the interviewer. Show that you’re excited to be
there, because they’re also humans like us.
Wishing you all the very best!

Arcesium Intern Interview Experience 2022 
About the company 
Global financial technology and professional services firm, delivering solutions to some of the 
world’s most sophisticated financial institutions. Launched by D.E. Shaw group with additional 
equity backing from Blackstone Alternative Asset Management and J.P. Morgan. 
Online Assessment (Platform : HackerRank) 
• Quantitative Aptitude - 15 MCQ’s (20 minutes) 
• Technical Aptitude - 15 MCQ’s (15 minutes) 
• Coding - 2 problems (45 minutes) 
For Quantitative Aptitude, refer indiabix.com. Problems were asked which made use of commonly 
asked formulas (profit/loss, family members relations and ages, train crossing, etc.). The only 
drawback is that the questions would have long workouts and insufficient time. So don’t go for high 
precision accuracy and waste time. Use tricks such as rounding off percentages as the choices are 
given such that they can be eliminated (i.e., the choices have significant differences between them). 
Also, there's negative marking of -0.25. But if you solve like about 8-10 questions then you can go 
with random choices for the remaining questions as in the worst case you might get a -1.75 to the 
total score. 
For Technical Aptitude, the questions were a mixed bag - output questions from C, C++ and Java, 
and a few DBMS queries as well. Same marking scheme as prior section: +1/-0.25. Try to be sure 
about the syntax in at least two of the three languages and make sure to be thorough with 
precedence of operators and oops concepts such as polymorphism and virtual classes, since these 
are applied in the questions that were asked.  
For coding, the following problems were asked - 
 1. Two players are playing a game which is as follows: There is a heap of n stones. In one move a 
player can remove a number of stones which is a power of 2. Whoever removes the last stone wins 
the game. Given n, print the winning player number (1 or 2). 
Solution: Basically, we have to find the number of set bits in the given n. If it’s odd, print 1. Else 
print 2. But the catch was the range of integers was 0 to 10^250. There's a class called BigInteger in 
Java under java.math to handle very large integers. 
Here's the code: 
import java.util.*; 
import java.math.*; 
 
class Solution 
{ 
 
public static void main (String[] args) 
 
{ 
 
    Scanner s = new Scanner(System.in); 
 
 
String str = new String(); 
 
 
str = s.nextLine(); 
 
 
BigInteger b = new BigInteger(str); 
 
 
if (b.bitCount() % 2 == 1) 
 
 
    System.out.println("1"); 
 
 
else 
 
 
    System.out.println("2"); 
 
 
 
 
} 
} 
The probability of getting such questions where you are asked to manipulate very large integers is 
very low. But still being familiar with BigInteger would help you a lot. 
 2. The question was similar to the one given below: 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/maximum-number-of-teams-of-size-k-possible-with-each-player-
from-different-country/  
As Arcesium is a Fin-Tech company, they give more weightage to the aptitude section. So, make 
sure to solve at least 50% of the questions in the first two sections and then rock the coding 
questions :). 
Fifteen students were selected for the subsequent Interview rounds. 
Round 1 - Code Pair 
I was asked to implement a binary tree and then find the ancestors of a given target node. After 
completing that, I was asked to optimize the code.  
[Code Pair round has a factor of luck since each one would get a different interviewer. From what I 
heard, many of us got relatively easy questions from binary trees and linked lists. But one guy was 
asked to do division without using multiplication, division or mod operation, which had to be done 
using bit manipulation. 
(Link: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/divide-two-integers-without-using-multiplication-division-
mod-operator/ ) 
So, try to solve lots of standard easy and medium problems from LeetCode and be thorough with 
the basic question patterns and expect the very best to happen. 
(Reference Links:  
https://leetcode.com/discuss/general-discussion/460599/blind-75-leetcode-questions  
https://hackernoon.com/14-patterns-to-ace-any-coding-interview-question-c5bb3357f6ed ) ] 
Round 2 - Technical Interview I 
The interviewer and I exchanged intros and then he asked me what my favourite subject was. I told 
him OS since I was strong in it. (You can answer whatever subject you feel strong enough in but 
mostly they would try to cover questions from all subjects - OS, DBMS, OOPS). He asked me about 
bootloader program and then about four pillars of OOPS. Then he proceeded with the difference 
between Run-time and Compile-time polymorphism. Run-time is slower as compared to compile-
time. He asked me the reason and the answer was VTABLE lookup. So, make sure to be thorough 
with concept of abstract class and virtual functions. Then he asked me about caching. 
Then a few common questions such as what is an API and what happens when you type google.com 
in your browser. Then he asked the difference between HTTP and HTTPS and about SSL. (These 
were general questions and were particular to this interviewer. In general, you don't need to worry 
much if you get these types of questions. Just manage to answer them to the best of your 
capability). 
Then he asked me to explain about the relation schema of my DBMS project. He asked me, "Do you 
see the problem here?" (I had two tables which contained similar types of data) So I answered that 
we can merge them into one. He asked me about normalization and then proceeded with "Do you 
see the problem here" again. Apparently on merging the two tables the API calls get made to the 
same table twice, which would lead to slower performance. He asked me for the solution and the 
answer was to use Indices. Then he asked questions which data structure is used to implement 
indexing and the difference between caching and indexing and then when to use indexing. So be 
thorough with the relation schemas of your project and learn about SQL indices, normalization and 
queries. They might not be asked directly but as part of questioning about your project. 
Round 3 - Technical Interview II 
The Director of Engineering had a talk with me and gave me a coding problem: 
Given a stream of posts like Instagram Feed, you have to pair posts which were 25 seconds apart in 
their timestamps. My idea was to use a queue and then remove the head of the queue as long as 
the difference was greater than 25. Then if the head was exactly 25 seconds less than the current 
post, make pair. I was asked only to write pseudocode. After that, another interviewer started 
asking me a stream of OS questions. He covered the following topics:  
• Program vs Process 
• Scheduling algorithms 
• Thread vs Process 
• States of a process 
• Semaphore vs Mutex 
• Deadlocks 
After these three rounds, I attended an HR round, where I was asked basic HR questions. 
Result: Three of us got selected. 
Do check out past intern interview experiences from GFG and practise company specific question 
sets too. All the best :)

Apple Inc., is an American multinational technology company, founded in 1976 by Steve Jobs,

global leader in consumer electronics, software, and services. The company is best known for

3. Technical Interview - Webex, Coderpad (Eliminatory) 
4. Techno-Managerial Interview - Webex, Coderpad

MCQs from DSA, DBMS, OS, OOP and Networks

The interview began with the interviewer introducing herself, followed by my introduction. She 
then asked about the project mentioned in my resume — including how the team was formed, the 
project’s objective, the challenges faced, and how they were resolved.  
 
After that, she moved on to DSA questions, such as explaining a Binary Search Tree (BST) and 
finding the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) in a BST. First, she asked me to explain my 
approach along with the edge cases, then to implement the solution in CoderPad, and finally to 
discuss the time and space complexities. Next, she asked a few questions from SQL. The 
interview concluded with me asking her a few questions. 
 
After the first round, I received mail for the second round. 
 
Techno-Managerial Interview - 45 min 
This round focused on in-depth technical knowledge, projects, and behavioral assessment. The 
interviewer asked me to introduce myself and then asked questions from every corner of my 
resume. 
 
Questions from JavaScript and React were asked (as mentioned on my resume). Then he asked 
me which language I was most comfortable with. I said Java, so he asked questions such as: what 
is static in Java, why is it used, and if a method is declared static, is an object needed to call it? 
 
Then came questions from my  project: 
●​ What is ML? 
●​ Explain the ML project. 
●​ Explain ML algorithms used in the project, why were these algorithms chosen? 
●​ What is Streamlit? Why Streamlit? 
●​ My contributions to the project. 
●​ Then he asked me to explain my other projects in my resume and the tech stacks used. 
 
Other technical questions include: (as I had mentioned them in my resume) 
●​ What is Firebase?  
●​ When is NoSQL used and when is RDBMS used? 
 
From DSA, I was asked to mention some advanced concepts I am familiar with. I said DP, and 
then he asked me questions on DP. 
 
He also asked about the club I am part of, which I had mentioned in my resume. 
 
Managerial and behavioral questions: 
●​ Why Apple? 
●​ Why you? 
●​ If you are in a team of 2 and the other member is not working on the project, what will 
you do? Will you report it to the team lead? 
 
The interview ended with me asking a few questions. 
 
10 students were selected for the SDE Internship. 
 
General Tips: 
 
●​ Know your resume thoroughly; interviewers can ask questions from anything you 
mentioned.  
●​ When answering DSA questions, first explain your approach and edge cases before 
starting coding. 
●​ Practice DSA mostly in Java or C++ 
 
Materials I used to prepare:  
 
●​ DSA: Striver’s A2Z DSA Sheet, Kunal Kushwaha, Abdul Bari, Jenny’s Lectures  
●​ CS Fundamentals: GeeksforGeeks (Computer Networks, OOP, OS, DBMS) 
 
 
 
Keep learning, keep growing, and give your best at every stage. All the best!
